title: The Central Role of Noise in Evaluating Interventions that Use Test Scores to Rank Schools
author: Kenneth Y. Chay, Patrick J. McEwan, Miguel Urquiola
context: This paper evaluates Chile’s P-900 program (1990–1992), which targeted roughly 900 low-performing fourth-grade schools across 13 regions using 1988 mean test-score cutoffs. Using school-level fourth-grade test scores from 1984, 1988, 1990, and 1992, the authors analyze program impacts on school-level gain scores, exploiting the cutoff-based assignment, regional cutoff variation, and school observables (enrollment, SES) to address selection and mean-reversion.

title: What Mean Impacts Miss: Distributional Effects of Welfare Reform Experiments
author: Marianne Bitler, Jonah Gelbach, Hilary Hoynes
context: This paper uses MDRC’s randomized Jobs First waiver experiment in Connecticut (random assignment April 1996–February 1997; follow‑up through December 2000) to estimate causal effects on quarterly earnings, cash welfare and Food Stamps for 4,803 welfare cases (women) in New Haven and Manchester. Jobs First implemented a 21‑month time limit, an expanded earnings disregard (earnings below the federal poverty line disregarded), work requirements, and sanctions; administrative UI and welfare records are the primary data.
identification_methods: RCT; PSM; event studies; other panel regressions

title: What Mean Impacts Miss: Distributional Effects of Welfare Reform Experiments
author: Marianne Bitler, Jonah Gelbach, Hilary Hoynes
context: This estimate uses MDRC’s randomized Jobs First waiver experiment in Connecticut (New Haven and Manchester), with random assignment April 1996–February 1997 and follow‑up through December 2000, for 4,803 welfare cases (women). The triplet refers specifically to the pre‑time‑limit period—the first seven post‑assignment quarters (before the 21‑month time limit can bind)—and estimates causal effects of Jobs First (including the expanded earnings disregard) on quarterly transfer income (cash welfare plus Food Stamps) using administrative UI and welfare records.
identification_methods: RCT; PSM; event studies; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 10217|debt relief agreements (brady plan)|us commercial bank stock prices
title: Is Debt Relief Efficient?
author: Serkan Arslanalp, Peter Blair Henry
context: The paper analyzes monthly country-level stock market indices for 10 Less-Developed Countries that signed Brady Plan debt-relief agreements (announcements 1989–1995) versus a control group of 16 emerging markets, and stock returns of major U.S. commercial banks with LDC exposure. It focuses on event windows (months before and after Brady announcements) within the broader Baker/Brady policy era, accounting for concurrent IMF programs and structural reforms, to evaluate causal effects of debt relief on valuations, net resource transfers, investment, and growth.

title: What do Aggregate Consumption Euler Equations Say about the Capital Income Tax Burden?
author: Casey B. Mulligan
context: This paper uses U.S. aggregate annual data (primarily 1947–1997) to study how capital income taxation affects the wedge between expected pre-tax returns on the capital stock and consumption growth. Units are national aggregates—BEA measures of pre-tax and after-tax capital returns, per-capita nondurable and services consumption, and capital-income tax revenue (federal, state, local). The analysis relies on consumption Euler equations, VARs to form expectations, and TSLS estimates to infer capital supply elasticity and tax incidence in the mid-to-late 20th century United States.

triplet_key: 10276|degree of dollarization|severity of reversal impact on growth
title: Thirty Years of Current Account Imbalances, Current Account Reversals and Sudden Stops
author: Sebastian Edwards
outcome: Severity of reversal impact on growth
context: Using annual country-year data for 157 countries over 1970–2001, this paper studies current-account imbalances, large deficit “reversals,” and “sudden stops.” The unit of observation is the country-year across six regions (industrial, Latin America/Caribbean, Asia, Africa, MENA, Eastern Europe). Analyses relate lagged external debt, reserves, short-term debt, and credit growth to reversal probability and estimate reversal impacts on GDP growth, contextualized by 1970s oil shocks, the 1980s debt crisis, 1990s currency crises, IMF programs, and varying exchange-rate/dollarization regimes.

title: Financial Openness, Sudden Stops and Current Account Reversals
author: Sebastian Edwards
context: Using an annual panel of 157 countries from roughly 1970–2001 (2,228 country-year observations), this paper studies how abrupt stops in capital inflows (“sudden stops”) and large current-account reversals affect national real GDP growth. Units are countries observed annually across world regions. Key institutional features include measures of financial openness (Quinn index), trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), reserve levels, external debt, short-term debt shares, and de-facto exchange-rate regimes; the analysis evaluates how capital-account restrictions and exchange-rate flexibility mediate crisis incidence and growth impacts.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Business Cycle Accounting
author: V.V. Chari, Patrick J. Kehoe, Ellen McGrattan
context: This paper analyzes U.S. aggregate business cycle episodes — principally the Great Depression (1929–39), the 1982 recession (1979–85), and the postwar period (1959:1–2004:3) — using national-level time series on output, labor, investment and government consumption. It maps frictions (credit, sticky wages, monopoly power) into four time-varying wedges (efficiency, labor, investment, government consumption) within a stochastic growth prototype, estimates a vector AR(1) process for wedges from macro data, and feeds measured wedges into calibrated structural models to assess their contributions to fluctuations.

title: Good Principals or Good Peers? Parental Valuation of School Characteristics, Tiebout Equilibrium, and the Effects of Inter-district Competition
author: Jesse Rothstein
context: Analyzes U.S. metropolitan school markets in the 1990s using student- and school-level data (NELS 8th graders surveyed 1988 with 1992 follow-up; SAT-takers from the 1994 cohort) and 1990 Census/MSA measures. Units are students, schools, and Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs). The institutional setting is local public school districts (Tiebout-style residential choice between districts), and the paper studies how district market structure (a district-concentration choice index) relates to within-MSA sorting of peer-group composition and unobserved school effectiveness, and implications for incentives from school competition.

triplet_key: 10930|medicaid market share|average price of a prescription
title: The Distortionary Effects of Government Procurement: Evidence from Medicaid Prescription Drug Purchasing
author: Mark Duggan, Fiona Scott Morton
context: This paper examines how U.S. Medicaid procurement rules affect pharmaceutical prices and product introductions using drug- and NDC-level data for the top 200 drugs in 1997 and 2002. It exploits variation across drugs in their Medicaid market share (Medicaid revenues divided by total U.S. revenues), merging IMS national sales with CMS State Drug Utilization files. Institutional context: Medicaid reimburses roughly 90% of AWP, applies a rebate (≈15.1% of AMP), beneficiaries face near-zero co-pays, and a CPI cap on price increases that firms can circumvent by introducing new NDCs.

title: An Investigation of the Effects of Alcohol Policies on Youth STDs
author: Michael Grossman, Robert Kaestner, Sara Markowitz
context: Using U.S. state- and large-MSA-level panel data from the 1980s through 2001, the paper estimates how alcohol policies affect STD outcomes for teenagers and young adults. It analyzes annual gonorrhea rates by state (ages 15–19 and 20–24) from 1981–2001 and AIDS incidence in 103 large MSAs (ages 20–29 and 30–34) from 1982–2001, exploiting cross‑state and over‑time variation in beer excise taxes, percent dry counties, and BAC/zero‑tolerance laws; models include area and year fixed effects, lag structures (an 8‑year lag for AIDS), and dynamic panel methods.

title: An Investigation of the Effects of Alcohol Policies on Youth STDs
author: Michael Grossman, Robert Kaestner, Sara Markowitz
context: Using state-level annual panel data for U.S. states from 1981–2001, the paper examines how the percentage of the state population living in dry (no-beer) counties affects reported gonorrhea incidence among teenagers and young adults. The evidence for this triplet focuses on male gonorrhea rates for ages 15–19 and 20–24. Models include state and year fixed effects, lag structures, and dynamic panel estimators (including first-difference two-stage least squares/FD2SLS) as well as OLS, with standard errors clustered by state.

triplet_key: 10990|equity market liberalization|ability to exploit global growth opportunities
title: Global Growth Opportunities and Market Integration
author: Geert Bekaert, Campbell R. Harvey, Christian Lundblad, Stephan Siegel
outcome: Ability to exploit global growth opportunities
context: This paper analyzes a panel of 50 countries (annual December observations broadly covering 1980–2002) using country-level units to test whether industry-weighted global price-earnings (P/E) ratios—combined with country industry weights (market cap or value-added)—predict five-year-ahead real GDP and investment growth. Data sources include Datastream, IFC, UNIDO and IMF. The authors exploit variation across countries and over time in financial openness (capital account, equity market, and banking liberalizations), include country fixed effects, and interact openness measures to assess whether openness enables exploitation of exogenous global growth opportunities.

author: Mark J. Garmaise, Tobias J. Moskowitz
treatment: Decrease in bank competition (via large bank mergers)
context: Using U.S. microdata from the 1990s (loan and merger data 1992–1999; crime scores 1990, 1995, 2000), the paper studies commercial real estate transactions across 11 states and DC and analyzes neighborhood (15-mile radius), census-tract, county, and state units. It exploits mergers of large, non‑failing banks (≥ $1bn) and state branching deregulation as plausibly exogenous shocks to local bank competition to trace effects on loan terms, construction, property prices, demographic change, and subsequent property crime (CAP Index and FBI UCR measures).

triplet_key: 11052|higher legal formalism|interest rates to rated borrowers
title: How Law and Institutions Shape Financial Contracts: The Case of Bank Loans
author: Jun Qian, Philip E. Strahan
context: This paper studies how laws and institutions shape bank loan contracts using a global sample of syndicated bank loans originated 1994–mid-2003 to large corporate borrowers in about 60 countries (excluding the U.S.). It links loan-level terms—interest spread over LIBOR, maturity, secured status—and ownership structure to country-level measures of creditor rights (disaggregated into ability to seize collateral vs. oust management), property rights, legal formalism, corruption, legal origin, and financial/economic development, controlling for borrower, loan, industry, and year effects.

title: Systemic Crises and Growth
author: Romain Ranciere, Aaron Tornell, Frank Westermann
context: Using cross-country annual data (primarily 1961–2000, sample of ~58 countries aggregated into non‑overlapping decades), the paper links systemic financial risk—proxied by skewness of real bank‑credit growth and multiple crisis indexes—to per‑capita GDP growth and investment. The unit of analysis is countries (and country‑decades); key institutional contrasts are financial liberalization (de facto and de jure indexes) and contract enforceability (1984 law‑and‑order index). The empirical tests exploit cross‑country and decade variation to evaluate a theoretical model where liberalization plus moderate enforceability and bailout guarantees induce systemic risk‑taking.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Did Iraq Cheat the United Nations?  Underpricing, Bribes, and the Oil for Food Program
author: Chang-Tai Hsieh, Enrico Moretti
context: This paper studies Iraq’s UN Oil-for-Food Program using weekly price and quantity data (primarily 1997–2002, with 1980–1996 as a pre-program baseline) and examines the September 2001 retroactive-pricing reform and a 1998 export-cap change. It analyzes Basrah Light and Kirkuk crude, spot-market prices, and contract-level buyer composition (firms and individual traders). Institutional context: UN-controlled escrow, Iraqi discretion to set monthly selling prices and choose buyers. The authors use price gaps versus close substitutes and spot prices to infer deliberate underpricing, rents, and bribe transfers.

triplet_key: 11478|mandatory disclosure requirements|cumulative abnormal excess stock returns
title: Mandated Disclosure, Stock Returns, and the 1964 Securities Acts Amendments
author: Michael Greenstone, Paul Oyer, Annette Vissing-Jorgensen
context: This empirical study analyzes U.S. firms (primarily 1,196 OTC firms and matched NYSE/AMEX firms) over 1963–1966, exploiting the passage (Aug 20, 1964) and implementation of the 1964 Securities Acts Amendments that extended mandatory disclosure to OTC firms above asset/shareholder thresholds. Using weekly stock returns, SEC registration/filing dates, and 1962–1966 accounting data, the paper compares affected OTC groups (e.g., firms newly required to file) to size and book-to-market matched listed firms to assess valuation and operating-performance effects of mandatory disclosure.
identification_methods: event studies; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

triplet_key: 11551|increased aid flows to developing countries|developing countries' incomes
title: The Marginal Product of Capital
author: Francesco Caselli, James Feyrer
treatment: Increased aid flows to developing countries
context: Cross-country analysis using a cross-section of about 53 countries circa 1996–2000 (Penn World Tables 6.1 for GDP/capital; World Bank wealth data ~2000; Bernanke & Gurkaynak capital shares). Unit of observation is the country. The authors assume competitive domestic goods and factor markets and Cobb–Douglas production, and they adjust for reproducible vs. natural capital and for domestic relative prices of investment goods. They perform structural counterfactual reallocations of the world reproducible-capital stock to assess deadweight losses from non‑equalized returns.

triplet_key: 11702|increased head start funding|child mortality rate (ages 5-9) from relevant causes
title: Does Head Start Improve Children's Life Chances? Evidence from a Regression Discontinuity Design
author: Jens Ludwig, Douglas L. Miller
outcome: Child mortality rate (ages 5-9) from relevant causes
context: This paper exploits a discontinuity generated by how the U.S. Office of Economic Opportunity (OEO) launched Head Start in 1965 by providing grant-writing assistance to the 300 poorest 1960 counties. Using county- and individual-level data on cohorts born in the 1960s–70s (mortality 1973–83, 1990 Census cohorts aged 18–24 born 1966–72, and NELS 1988 students), the authors compare outcomes across counties near the OEO poverty cutoff to estimate Head Start's impacts on child health and educational attainment.

title: Dams
author: Esther Duflo, Rohini Pande
context: This paper studies the impacts of large dam construction in India over roughly 1970–1999 (with crop analyses 1971–1987 and disease and rainfall panels through the 1990s). The analysis uses Indian districts as units, GIS-derived river and dam locations to classify upstream/catchment and downstream/command areas, and district-level panels on agricultural production, irrigation, rural consumption, poverty and wages. Context includes state-led dam planning/financing, the Land Acquisition Act (1894), documented displacement, and environmental impacts of reservoirs.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Foreign Direct Investment and Domestic Economic Activity
author: Mihir A. Desai, C. Fritz Foley, James R. Hines Jr.
context: This study examines U.S. manufacturing multinational parent firms using confidential BEA benchmark-survey panel data for 1982, 1989, 1994 and 1999 (covering changes between 1982–1999). The unit of observation is U.S. parent firms and their foreign affiliates located across many host countries. The institutional background is the U.S. multinational tax and investment environment and debates over outbound FDI. Identification leverages variation in host-country GDP growth interacted with each firm’s lagged geographic distribution of foreign investment.

title: Insurer-Provider Networks in the Medical Care Market
author: Katherine Ho
context: The finding refers to short-term hospital investment incentives in the paper's US managed-care setting (supply-side sample: 42 markets, excluding Maryland, with data through 2003). It concerns hospitals as the unit of observation and analyzes how high markups generated by insurer–hospital bargaining — under the paper's fixed-premium assumption and with hospital capacity assumed chosen prior to contracting — create an incentive to under-invest in inpatient bed capacity. The paper quantifies a median consumer benefit of about $331,890 per additional bed per year.

triplet_key: 11832|universal $5/day childcare program|child behavioral and health outcomes
title: Universal Childcare, Maternal Labor Supply, and Family Well-Being
author: Michael Baker, Jonathan Gruber, Kevin Milligan
context: Using the Canadian NLSCY (pre: 1994-95, 1996-97; post: 2000-01, 2002-03), this analysis focuses on children aged 0–4 in married/cohabiting (two-parent) families across provinces to evaluate Quebec’s phased 1997–2000 universal $5/day childcare reform. The reform rapidly expanded subsidized center and licensed family care and altered subsidy/tax treatment; the outcomes for this triplet are parent-reported child behavioral and health measures (e.g., hyperactivity, inattention, aggressiveness, motor/social skills, general health, illness). Estimates are reduced-form DiD comparisons of Quebec versus other provinces.

title: Universal Childcare, Maternal Labor Supply, and Family Well-Being
author: Michael Baker, Jonathan Gruber, Kevin Milligan
context: Using the Canadian National Longitudinal Survey of Children and Youth (pre: 1994-95, 1996-97; post: 2000-01, 2002-03), the paper examines children aged 0–4 and their married/cohabiting parents across provinces to evaluate Quebec’s phased 1997–2000 universal $5-per-day childcare (CPE) reform. The reform rapidly expanded subsidized center and licensed family care, altered subsidy and tax treatment, and applied by child age and province; the authors compare Quebec to other Canadian provinces to estimate effects on childcare use, maternal labor supply, and child and family outcomes.

title: Universal Childcare, Maternal Labor Supply, and Family Well-Being
author: Michael Baker, Jonathan Gruber, Kevin Milligan

The reform rapidly expanded subsidized center and licensed family care, altered subsidy and tax treatment, and applied by child age and province; the authors compare Quebec to other Canadian provinces to estimate effects on childcare use, maternal labor supply, and child and family outcomes.

title: The Polarization of the U.S. Labor Market
author: David H. Autor, Lawrence F. Katz, Melissa S. Kearney
context: For the specific causal link between minimum-wage changes and lower-tail wage inequality, the paper analyzes U.S. workers using CPS hourly wage data across 1973–2004 (with emphasis on 1979–1987 versus 1988–2004) and concludes that minimum-wage movements plausibly help explain lower-tail inequality trends only during 1979–1987, but do not account for the evolution of bottom-half wage inequality after 1987.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Democracy and Development: The Devil in the Details
author: Torsten Persson, Guido Tabellini
context: The paper uses annual country-year data on about 150 countries (main analyses 1960–2000; extended 1850–2000 for expectation analysis) to study effects of regime changes on log per-capita income and investment. It exploits episodes of democratization, autocracy, and economic liberalization (Polity2; Sachs–Warner liberalization), variation in forms of government and electoral rules (presidential vs parliamentary; majoritarian vs proportional), and controls for wars and former Soviet-bloc transitions; expected regime-change hazards (democratic capital) are modeled.

title: The Unequal Effects of Liberalization: Evidence from Dismantling the License Raj in India
author: Philippe Aghion, Robin Burgess, Stephen Redding, Fabrizio Zilibotti
context: This paper studies the effects of dismantling India’s “license raj” and concurrent trade liberalization on registered manufacturing across 16 Indian states over 1980–1997. Using state × three-digit industry-year observations from the Annual Survey of Industries, it exploits staggered nationwide delicensing waves (major waves in 1985 and 1991) and cross-state variation in labor-market institutions (state amendments to the 1947 Industrial Disputes Act) and tariffs to estimate how liberalization differentially affected output, employment, entry and investment.

triplet_key: 12254|introduction of medicare pps reform|adoption of high-tech medical technologies
title: Input and Technology Choices in Regulated Industries: Evidence From the Health Care Sector
author: Daron Acemoglu, Amy Finkelstein
context: Using AHA hospital panel data for fiscal years 1980–1986 (four pre and three post years), the paper studies U.S. hospitals (≈6,200 annually, excluding MA/NY/MD/NJ waivers) to assess how the October 1983 Medicare Prospective Payment System (PPS) — a shift from full cost to partial cost reimbursement (prospective payments for operating costs while capital remained cost‑reimbursed) — affected hospitals’ input mix, technology adoption, length of stay, and nurse skill composition. Identification leverages pre‑PPS Medicare share of patient days interacted with post‑PPS timing.

title: Big Business Stability and Economic Growth: Is What's Good for General Motors Good for America?
author: Kathy Fogel, Randall Morck, Bernard Yeung
context: This paper studies the relationship between stability of the largest firms/business groups and long-run national economic growth across 44 countries. It constructs employment-weighted top‑10 firm stability indices using Dun & Bradstreet lists for circa 1975 and 1996, then relates these indexes to country-level per‑capita GDP, capital accumulation, and TFP growth over 1990–2000. Analyses control for 1990 initial income, physical and human capital and institutional features (state-controlled firms, legal origin, financial development, government size, and openness). Units are countries.

title: First Do No Harm?: Tort Reform and Birth Outcomes
author: Janet Currie, W. Bentley MacLeod
context: This study examines how state-level tort reforms in the United States from 1989–2001 affected obstetric practice and birth outcomes. Using individual Vital Statistics natality records on millions of births (mothers and infants), the authors exploit within-state changes in laws (caps on non-economic/punitive damages, joint-and-several liability, collateral-source rules) across counties to estimate effects on C-sections, labor induction, complications, and APGAR scores. Many reforms were enacted or overturned in the 1990s, providing staggered policy variation across states.

author: Christopher House, Matthew D. Shapiro

Using quarterly BEA investment and price series matched to IRS/MACRS schedules for 36 capital-good types (panel 1959–2005, focus 2001–2005), it exploits cross-sectional eligibility (MACRS recovery ≤20 years) and time variation to identify effects on investment spending and observed investment prices in the U.S.

triplet_key: 12639|severance pay and extended benefits|subsequent job match quality
title: Cash-on-Hand and Competing Models of Intertemporal Behavior: New Evidence from the Labor Market
author: David Card, Raj Chetty, Andrea Weber
context: Using Austrian administrative social-security data on ~650,922 private-sector job-loss spells (1981–2001) for individuals aged ~20–50, this triplet exploits sharp eligibility discontinuities: a statutory severance lump-sum (two months’ pay) at prior tenure ≥36 months and a UI maximum duration discontinuity (20 vs 30 weeks) at 36 months worked in the past five years. The outcome is subsequent job match quality, measured by mean wages, duration of subsequent jobs, and other job-quality metrics; the paper finds little or no effect (e.g., mean wage change <1% at the 95% CI upper bound).

triplet_key: 12727|pre-negotiation import volume|magnitude of negotiated tariff concessions
title: What Do Trade Negotiators Negotiate About? Empirical Evidence from the World Trade Organization
author: Kyle Bagwell, Robert W. Staiger
context: Examines WTO accession tariff commitments for 16 countries that joined between 1995 and 2005 using country×6‑digit HS product observations. It links pre‑accession (unbound) ad‑valorem tariffs and import levels (imports averaged 1995–1999) to final bound tariffs negotiated at accession. The institutional setting is WTO accession bargaining (tariffication of NTBs, MFN bindings, reciprocity). The analysis assesses terms‑of‑trade versus commitment theories of trade agreements across industries and countries.

title: Economics and Politics of Alternative Institutional Reforms
author: Francesco Caselli, Nicola Gennaioli
context: The paper develops a static model of incumbents and outside entrepreneurs in economies with regulatory entry barriers and imperfect contract enforcement, focusing on developing and transition settings where licenses, firm control, and managerial talent matter. It tests model predictions using cross-country × industry data: country-level measures of contract enforcement and industry-level proxies for the importance of the market for control (e.g. M&A activity), with outcomes measured at the industry–country (firm/industry) level such as entry rates and average productivity.

title: Who Blows the Whistle on Corporate Fraud?
author: Alexander Dyck, Adair Morse, Luigi Zingales
context: This paper studies reported corporate frauds in large U.S. public firms (assets > $750M) from 1996–2004, using class-action filings, news and regulatory records to identify which external actors (analysts, short sellers, auditors, employees, SEC, media, regulators) reveal fraud and how timing and incentives (notably post-SOX reforms and qui tam awards in healthcare) affect detection. The unit of analysis is the firm/case, and the institutional setting emphasizes U.S. securities litigation, regulatory regimes, and industry-specific government exposure.

title: The Spike at Benefit Exhaustion: Leaving the Unemployment System or Starting a New Job?
author: David Card, Raj Chetty, Andrea Weber
treatment: Eligibility for 30 weeks of UI benefits
context: Using Austrian social security administrative records for involuntary job losses from 1981–2001, the paper analyzes about 92,969 individuals aged 20–50 to study exit hazards around unemployment insurance (UI) benefit exhaustion. Austria’s UI rules provide 20 or 30 weeks of benefits depending on months worked in the prior five years (36-month threshold), alongside severance pay rules and means-tested post-exhaustion assistance. The analysis contrasts registered-unemployment duration and time-to-next-job (including recalls) to assess whether spikes at benefit expiration reflect true re-employment or exits from the register.

title: Unbalanced Trade
author: Robert Dekle, Jonathan Eaton, Samuel Kortum
context: Using 2004 data for the world split into forty countries, this paper studies bilateral manufacturing trade flows and unilateral current-account imbalances. Units are countries and their bilateral manufacturing imports/exports. The authors calibrate a multi-country Eaton–Kortum gravity-style general-equilibrium model (manufactures vs nonmanufactures; parameters α, β, θ) and compute static counterfactual equilibria with all current accounts set to zero to quantify required relative-wage, price, welfare, and bilateral-trade adjustments.

title: Product Differentiation, Multi-product Firms and Estimating the Impact of Trade Liberalization on Productivity
author: Jan De Loecker
context: This paper studies Belgian textile firms over 1994–2002, using matched plant‑level and product‑level data (308 firms, 2,990 firm‑product observations) to estimate how EU textile quota liberalization affected firm productivity. It exploits product‑specific changes in quota protection (SIGL quota data) across segments, controlling for unobserved prices via an explicit demand system and addressing simultaneity and selection with Olley–Pakes style panel methods to separate true productivity gains from demand/markup effects.

title: Risk Shifting versus Risk Management: Investment Policy in Corporate Pension Plans
author: Joshua Rauh
context: This paper studies U.S. corporate defined‑benefit (DB) pension plans and their sponsoring firms using plan‑level IRS 5500 filings (primarily 1990–2003) and Pensions & Investments data matched to Compustat (1997–2004). Units of analysis are pension plans and firms in the U.S.; the institutional setting includes PBGC insurance, ERISA mandatory contribution rules, credit ratings, and observed plan terminations/bankruptcies. The paper examines how funding status, firm creditworthiness, and estimated default/termination probabilities relate to pension asset allocation.

title: Vertical Arrangements, Market Structure, and Competition An Analysis of Restructured U.S. Electricity Markets
author: James B. Bushnell, Erin T. Mansur, Celeste Saravia
context: Analyzes summer 1999 (June–Sept) hourly wholesale electricity markets in three restructured U.S. regions—California, New England, and PJM—using firm-hour observations of generation, retail obligations, and prices. Context: early post-restructuring regime with retail rate freezes and varied vertical arrangements (divestiture vs long-term contracts vs retained generation), ISOs operating spot markets, and price caps; authors reconstruct firm cost functions and simulate Cournot and competitive equilibria to estimate impact of vertical commitments on wholesale prices.

title: Do Political Parties Matter? Evidence from U.S. Cities
author: Fernando Ferreira, Joseph Gyourko
context: The paper studies causal effects of mayoral party (Democrat vs Republican) on municipal outcomes using a new panel of U.S. mayoral elections (1950–2005) and matched fiscal/crime data (1968–2004). The unit of observation is cities (≥25,000 pop.) and individual mayoral elections (4,543 elections; RD sample ≈1,993 close races). It examines impacts on government size, spending composition, employment, and crime within the institutional setting of partisan and nonpartisan local elections, varying term lengths, and municipal finance and FBI crime reporting.

title: Does Medicare Save Lives?
author: David Card, Carlos Dobkin, Nicole Maestas
context: Using California hospital discharge records (January 1992–December 2002) linked to death records, the paper studies individual unplanned emergency-room hospital admissions for “non-deferrable” diagnoses around the Medicare eligibility threshold. The unit is the patient admission for people just under and just over age 65 in California hospitals. It exploits the institutional rule that most U.S. workers become eligible for Medicare at 65 (sharp changes in primary coverage, supplemental coverage, and managed-care incidence) to estimate effects on treatment intensity, transfers, readmissions, and mortality.

title: Myth and Reality of Flat Tax Reform:  Micro Estimates of Tax Evasion Response and Welfare Effects in Russia
author: Yuriy Gorodnichenko, Jorge Martinez-Vazquez, Klara Sabirianova Peter
outcome: Consumption-income gap for private sector employees
context: This empirical study uses the Russian Longitudinal Monitoring Survey (household panel) for 1998 and 2000–2004 to evaluate Russia’s January 2001 personal income tax reform that replaced a progressive schedule with a 13% flat tax. The unit of analysis is households (and heads of household) across Russian regions; outcomes include reported income, consumption, and the consumption–income gap as a proxy for evasion. The paper exploits pre/post timing, tax-bracket thresholds, and regional administration/enforcement variation to identify voluntary compliance, productivity, and welfare effects.

title: Profiting from Government Stakes in a Command Economy: Evidence from Chinese Asset Sales
author: Charles Calomiris, Raymond Fisman, Yongxiang Wang
context: This paper studies market reactions to China’s 2001–2002 policy episodes (the July 24, 2001 announcement to sell remaining state-owned A shares and the June 23, 2002 cancellation) using firm-level stock returns. The analysis uses 107 publicly traded B‑share firms listed on Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges as the units, exploiting variation in government ownership, legal‑person shares, and senior managers’ prior government posts. The setting is China’s partial privatization with segmented A/B markets, non‑tradable state shares, SEZ heterogeneity, and strong local government discretion.

title: Moral Hazard vs. Liquidity and Optimal Unemployment Insurance

It exploits within‑state over‑time and cross‑state variation in state UI rules (e.g. maximum weekly benefits, ~26‑week benefit duration) and cross‑firm variation in lump‑sum severance payments to estimate effects on individual unemployment durations and to separate moral‑hazard from liquidity channels.

title: Big Business Stability and Social Welfare
author: Kathy Fogel, Randall Morck, Bernard Yeung
context: This paper performs a cross-country empirical analysis of big business stability using lists of the ten largest employers in 1975 and their persistence to 1996 for 43 countries. Persistence measures consolidate firms into business groups and sometimes include state-controlled and foreign firms. The authors relate 1975–1996 firm persistence to economic growth and social outcomes (public goods, inequality, labor power) using country-level regressions controlling for log per-capita GDP (averaged 1996–2000), with social indicators averaged mainly over 1996–2000.

title: Pricing and Welfare in Health Plan Choice
author: M. Kate Bundorf, Jonathan D. Levin, Neale Mahoney
treatment: Incorporating private health information into prices
context: This empirical study analyzes employer-sponsored health plan choice using administrative data from 11 small-to-mid sized employers in a single metropolitan area in the western United States during 2004–2005 (3,683 employee-years; 6,603 enrollee-years). title: Why Do Foreign Firms Leave U.S. Equity Markets?
author: Craig Doidge, G. Andrew Karolyi, René M. Stulz
context: This paper studies foreign firms’ deregistration from U.S. equity markets between 2002 and 2008, focusing on firms listed on major U.S. exchanges (including ADRs). The unit of analysis is firms and deregistration events; key institutional shocks are the Sarbanes–Oxley Act (SOX) passage in 2002 and the SEC’s Exchange Act Rule 12h-6 (adopted March 21, 2007, effective June 4, 2007) that eased foreign private issuers’ deregistration. The authors compare deregistering firms to other U.S.-listed foreign firms across financial, governance, and stock-return outcomes to test bonding versus loss-of-competitiveness mechanisms.
identification_methods: event studies; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Free Distribution or Cost-Sharing? Evidence from a Malaria Prevention Experiment
author: Jessica Cohen, Pascaline Dupas
context: This paper reports a 2007 randomized field experiment in 20 rural prenatal clinics in four districts of western Kenya (Busia, Bungoma, Butere, Mumias) that randomized clinic-level prices of long-lasting ITNs offered to pregnant women and measured baseline hemoglobin, take-up, and short-run home usage. The claim that “free distribution saves more child lives” is not from direct mortality measurement but from a five-year cost-effectiveness simulation: it combines the RCT’s demand/usage estimates with assumptions on community externalities (three threshold scenarios) and ITNs’ physical-barrier effectiveness to compute child lives saved under free versus cost-sharing.

title: Term Length and Political Performance
author: Ernesto Dal Bó, Martín Rossi
context: This paper examines effects of legislative term length on individual legislators' performance in Argentina using two natural experiments: the 1983 Chamber of Deputies random assignment of 2- vs 4-year initial terms after the restoration of democracy (analyzed 1984–1985), and the 2001 Senate random assignment of 2-, 4-, and 6-year terms (analyzed 2001–2003). Unit of observation is individual deputies and senators; outcomes include attendance, speeches, committee activity, bills introduced and passed; institutional context includes staggered renewal constitutional rules and low reelection rates.

title: The Value of School Facilities:  Evidence from a Dynamic Regression Discontinuity Design
author: Stephanie Riegg Cellini, Fernando Ferreira, Jesse Rothstein
context: This paper analyzes California school districts using bond referenda from 1987–2006 (with housing transactions through 1988–2005 and multi-year fiscal and test-score panels) to estimate causal effects. The unit is the school district; the institutional setting is California’s voter-approved general obligation school bonds (initially requiring a 2/3 supermajority, later sometimes 55%), whose revenues are legally restricted to capital projects. The design exploits close elections and repeated proposals to identify impacts on district finances, housing prices, and student achievement.
identification_methods: RDD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lottery
author: Brian A. Jacob, Jens Ludwig
context: Using a randomized Section 8 housing voucher wait-list lottery in Chicago (applications July 1997) and administrative data through 2005, the paper studies low-income applicant households (working-age, able-bodied adults) living in private-market housing. CHAC randomly assigned waiting-list positions and offered roughly 18,110 vouchers by May 2003. The institutional setting is means-tested housing vouchers (Fair Market Rent rules) interacting with TANF, Medicaid and Food Stamps; outcomes include employment, quarterly earnings and public-assistance receipt.
identification_methods: RCT; IV; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

triplet_key: 14595|growth slowdown in late 1970s–1980s|adoption of market-oriented policies
title: Learning the Wealth of Nations
author: Francisco J. Buera, Alexander Monge-Naranjo, Giorgio E. Primiceri
context: This paper studies the evolution of countries' choices between market-oriented and state-interventionist policies using a structural Bayesian learning model estimated on a panel of 133 countries over 1950–2001 (priors informed by 1900–1950 data). Units are countries; outcomes are Sachs–Warner-based binary market-orientation indicators and per-capita GDP growth. The institutional setting spans global regions and postwar policy regimes; key mechanisms are policymakers' Bayesian updating from own and geographically-weighted neighbors' growth experiences, used to infer policy adoption and simulate counterfactuals (e.g., Great Depression-sized shocks).

title: Private Equity and Long-Run Investment: The Case of Innovation
author: Josh Lerner, Morten Sørensen, Per Strömberg
context: This empirical study examines how private equity (primarily leveraged buyouts) affects firms’ long‑run innovative investment by linking Capital IQ transactions (closed 1980–2005) to U.S. USPTO patent grants observed through May 2007. The units are firms (495 private equity‑backed entities) and their patents (≈6,938 grants); analyses focus on event windows three years before to five years after a transaction. The setting evaluates changes in patent counts, citations, originality, generality, and portfolio concentration using patent‑class/year controls and firm/year fixed effects, while noting selection and causation limits.

title: Wages and Human Capital in the U.S. Financial Industry: 1909-2006
author: Thomas Philippon, Ariell Reshef
context: This paper analyzes the U.S. financial sector from 1909–2006, comparing firms' subsectors and individual workers to the nonfarm private sector. It uses Census, CPS, BEA industry accounts and matched longitudinal CPS to study wages, education, and task complexity for individuals (workers) and subsector aggregates across Credit Intermediation, Insurance, and Other Finance. The institutional setting focuses on regulatory regimes (Glass–Steagall, branching limits, interest-rate ceilings, bank–insurance separations), IPO waves, and credit-risk cycles as drivers of skill demand and wage dynamics.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Immigration and Inequality
author: David Card
context: This paper analyzes U.S. labor markets using city-level panels drawn from the 1980, 1990, and 2000 Censuses and 2005–2006 ACS across the 124 largest MSAs, focusing on individual wage outcomes (native and immigrant workers, by education and experience). It studies immigration inflows during roughly 1980–2000 (and early 2000s), exploiting country-of-origin settlement patterns and variation in immigrant skill composition to infer causal effects on within- and between-group wage inequality, under a one-sector CES demand framework and assumptions of elastically supplied capital and imperfect substitutability between groups.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Long-Run Impacts of Unions on Firms: New Evidence from Financial Markets, 1961-1999
author: David Lee, Alexandre Mas
context: This paper studies U.S. National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) representation elections from 1961–1999 by matching election outcomes to publicly traded firms’ monthly CRSP stock returns and Compustat quarterly accounting data. The unit of analysis is the firm/security experiencing an NLRB election; the institutional setting is NLRA secret‑ballot certification elections (and their closure dates). Long event windows (up to ~48 months pre/post) and firm‑level accounting series are used to infer how union victories affect market valuation and firm performance.
identification_methods: event studies; RDD; DiD; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Long-Run Impacts of Unions on Firms: New Evidence from Financial Markets, 1961-1999
author: David Lee, Alexandre Mas
context: For the total-assets analysis the authors use firm-level quarterly Compustat accounting data (restricted to 1973–1999) matched to U.S. NLRB representation elections. The unit is the firm and event time is measured in quarters around the NLRB case-closure (certification) date. Estimates are from an election×event-time panel with election fixed effects and a post × union-win interaction, comparing union-victory to union-loss firms' total assets.

title: Did the 2008 Tax Rebates Stimulate Spending?
author: Matthew D. Shapiro, Joel B. Slemrod
context: This paper studies U.S. households’ responses to the Economic Stimulus Act of 2008, which authorized roughly $152 billion of one-time tax rebates disbursed mainly by direct deposit in May and by mail May–July 2008. Using a February–June 2008 representative Survey of Consumers module and aggregate BEA series, the authors examine household plans and aggregate saving/credit behavior to infer marginal propensity to consume from rebates, with attention to eligibility rules, payment timing, rebate amounts, and heterogeneity by age and income.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other time-series regressions; others

triplet_key: 14759|restricting the choice set to three lowest cost options|welfare
title: Choice Inconsistencies Among the Elderly: Evidence from Plan Choice in the Medicare Part D Program
author: Jason T. Abaluck, Jonathan Gruber
treatment: Restricting the choice set to three lowest cost options
context: This paper studies Medicare Part D plan choices by U.S. Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+ around the 2006 rollout, using 2005–2006 Wolters Kluwer longitudinal prescription claims matched to CMS plan menus. It focuses on individual elders’ choices among dozens of stand-alone PDPs (typical county choice set ≈40 plans) under the newly privatized Part D benefit (premiums, deductibles, copays, and “donut hole” rules), excluding dual eligibles and employer-sponsored plans, to evaluate whether seniors optimize given premiums, expected out-of-pocket costs, and risk.

triplet_key: 14787|endogenous monetary policy|volatility of reset and actual inflation
title: Reset Price Inflation and the Impact of Monetary Policy Shocks
author: Mark Bils, Peter J. Klenow, Benjamin A. Malin
outcome: Volatility of reset and actual inflation
context: Using U.S. CPI microdata (CPI-RDB) for non-shelter consumer items and monthly price quote-lines from the three largest metropolitan areas, the paper constructs reset-price inflation series from January 1989 through May 2008. Units are individual item quote-lines across retail outlets, grouped into flexible and sticky goods. The study evaluates how aggregate monetary (and alternative productivity) shocks transmit to reset and actual prices by estimating impulse responses in the data and contrasting them with simulations from Calvo time-dependent and state-dependent DSGE pricing models (with/without strategic complementarities).

title: The Consequences of Radical Reform: The French Revolution
author: Daron Acemoglu, Davide Cantoni, Simon Johnson, James A. Robinson
context: The paper analyzes the long-run economic effects of institutional changes imposed by French Revolutionary and Napoleonic occupation (circa 1792–1815) across Europe, with detailed focus on sub-national German polities. Units are countries and polities (city/polity/country-level panels) using urbanization and Maddison GDP series spanning 1700–1910. It exploits exogenous variation in years and timing of French control—together with documented reforms (abolition of guilds, feudal dues, Napoleonic civil code)—to assess causal impacts on industrialization and growth while accounting for pre-trends and covariates.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other panel regressions

title: The Finnish Great Depression: From Russia with Love
author: Yuriy Gorodnichenko, Enrique G. Mendoza, Linda L. Tesar
context: This paper studies the Finnish Great Depression of the early 1990s (1990–1993) using national- and sector-level data and structural modeling. The setting is Finland’s collapse of barter-style five-year trade agreements with the USSR—large, specialized exports in exchange for subsidized energy—followed by a sudden loss of Soviet markets and a rise in energy prices. Units of analysis include aggregate macro series (GDP, consumption, investment), sectoral/industry value added and employment, and cross-industry export exposure; comparisons include Sweden and Eastern European transition economies and the 1974 oil shock.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Does Corporate Governance Matter in Competitive Industries?
author: Xavier Giroud, Holger M. Mueller
context: Using U.S. firm-year panel data (Compustat) from 1976–1995, the paper studies how staggered passage of 30 state business combination (BC) laws (primarily 1985–1991) affected firm performance. Units are U.S. firms defined by state of incorporation and state of location, with industry competition measured by HHIs (3-digit SIC). BC laws constrained hostile takeovers by moratoria on business combinations; identification exploits variation in adoption timing and incorporation-location mismatches, with event dates from contemporary newspaper reports.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: A New Test of Borrowing Constraints for Education
author: Meta Brown, John Karl Scholz, Ananth Seshadri
context: This paper studies U.S. parent–child pairs (siblings) to test borrowing constraints for higher education, combining a theoretical model with empirical analysis using Health and Retirement Study cohorts (parents born c.1931–1947; children with median 18th birthday 1977) and NLSY‑97 validation. Empirical data span HRS waves 1992–2004 with post‑college cash transfers aggregated 1998–2004 and a 1994 retrospective module; institutional context is U.S. federal financial aid (FAFSA/EFC rules and the MISAA 1978 expansion). Unit of observation is children within families, exploiting sibling birth‑spacing to proxy variation in EFC.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: International Trade and Labor Income Risk in the United States
author: Pravin Krishna, Mine Zeynep Senses
context: This paper analyzes U.S. manufacturing workers using three SIPP longitudinal panels (1993–1995, 1996–1998, 2001–2003). Unit of observation is individual workers assigned to manufacturing industries; industry-level import penetration (Imports/(Shipments−Exports+Imports)) measures trade exposure. The setting covers U.S. manufacturing across multiple two-digit SIC industries, exploiting within-industry changes over time to link import penetration to persistent idiosyncratic labor-income risk and to quantify welfare costs under incomplete-market, CRRA-preference assumptions.

title: Land and Credit: A Study of the Political Economy of Banking in the United States in the Early 20th Century
author: Raghuram G. Rajan, Rodney Ramcharan
context: The paper studies county-level banking and credit in the United States in the early 20th century (primarily 1890–1930, focusing on 1920 and 1930). The unit of observation is U.S. counties; outcomes include banks per capita, mortgage interest rates, indebtedness and mortgage lending. The setting leverages variation in agricultural land distribution (farm-size Gini), tenancy, crop-lien and chartering institutions (state vs. national banks, unit-banking laws) and geographic factors (rainfall, distance to state capital) to assess how landed interests shaped local financial development.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

triplet_key: 15180|more powerful supervisors (greater powers)|bank returns during crisis
title: Why Did Some Banks Perform Better During the Credit Crisis? A Cross-Country Study of the Impact of Governance and Regulation
author: Andrea Beltratti, René M. Stulz
context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in buy-and-hold stock returns for 98 large deposit-taking banks (assets >$50bn) across about 20 countries during the 2007–2008 global credit crisis (main window July 2007–Dec 2008 and the Lehman month Sep 12–Oct 10, 2008). It links pre-crisis (2006) bank-level governance (RiskMetrics/CGQ), ownership, balance-sheet and profitability metrics (Tier 1, deposits, loans, liquidity), and country-level regulation and institutions (Caprio/Barth/Levine indices, Kaufmann governance) to differential bank performance.

title: Why Did Some Banks Perform Better During the Credit Crisis? A Cross-Country Study of the Impact of Governance and Regulation
author: Andrea Beltratti, René M. Stulz
context: This result comes from a cross-sectional regression on a subset of the paper’s sample: 41 of the large deposit-taking banks (assets >$50bn) from 11 countries, using Bloomberg data on writedowns-to-assets observed during the 2007–2008 credit crisis (main window July 2007–Dec 2008). The regression relates pre-crisis (2006) bank-level governance (RiskMetrics/CGQ board index) and controls (Tier 1, log assets) plus country regulatory indices (power of supervisor, capital supervision, institutions, anti-director index) to writedowns.

title: Bank CEO Incentives and the Credit Crisis
author: Rüdiger Fahlenbrach, René M. Stulz
context: This paper examines whether bank CEO incentives and holdings measured at fiscal-year-end 2006 predict U.S. bank performance during the 2007–2008 credit crisis. The unit of observation is 95 large U.S. commercial and investment banks covered in ExecuComp; outcomes include buy-and-hold stock returns (typically July 1, 2007–Dec 31, 2008), quarterly ROA/ROE, and insider trades. The institutional setting is the U.S. banking industry amid the global financial crisis and policy responses (e.g., TARP); analyses control for firm size, book-to-market, past returns, and capital ratios.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other time-series regressions

triplet_key: 15321|recession during impressionable years|desire for government redistribution
title: Growing Up in a Recession: Beliefs and the Macroeconomy
author: Paola Giuliano, Antonio Spilimbergo
context: This paper studies how exposure to large macroeconomic shocks during “impressionable years” (ages 18–25) shapes individuals’ beliefs about luck versus effort and preferences for redistribution. Using US pooled cross-sections (GSS 1972–2010, regional per-capita income shocks or state unemployment), a longitudinal US cohort (NLS72, 1972–1986, state unemployment), and cross-country data (WVS linked to Barro et al. disaster dates), the unit is the individual across regions, states, and 37 countries. Identification exploits cohort, age, period, region/state timing of recessions, and extensive demographic and background controls.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 15323|performance-based bonus payments to teachers|variance of student test scores
title: Teacher Performance Pay: Experimental Evidence from India
author: Karthik Muralidharan, Venkatesh Sundararaman
context: This paper reports a randomized evaluation (2005–07) of teacher performance pay in rural Andhra Pradesh, India. The authors randomized 500 sampled government primary schools (focus on 300 schools: 100 group-incentive, 100 individual-incentive, 100 control) across 50 mandals, following baseline tests in June–July 2005 and endline tests in March–April 2006 and 2007. Units are pupils (grades 1–5), teachers, and schools. The intervention paid teacher bonuses (≈3% of annual pay) based on average student test-score improvements, in a setting with low learning, high teacher absence, and civil-service pay rules.

The authors randomized 500 sampled government primary schools (focus on 300 schools: 100 group-incentive, 100 individual-incentive, 100 control) across 50 mandals, following baseline tests in June–July 2005 and endline tests in March–April 2006 and 2007. Units are pupils (grades 1–5), teachers, and schools. The intervention paid teacher bonuses (≈3% of annual pay) based on average student test-score improvements, in a setting with low learning, high teacher absence, and civil-service pay rules.

triplet_key: 15362|rising home prices, declining rates, easier refinancing|systemic risk in financial system
title: Systemic Risk and the Refinancing Ratchet Effect
author: Amir E. Khandani, Andrew W. Lo, Robert C. Merton
treatment: Rising home prices, declining rates, easier refinancing
context: This paper analyzes the U.S. residential housing market using a calibrated simulation of individual homes/mortgages (vintages) over 1919–2008, with emphasis on the refinancing boom and subsequent price decline around 1999–2008 (notably June 2006–Dec 2008). It studies homeowners and mortgage lenders nationally, under institutional features like widespread cash‑out and rate/term refinancing, 30‑year fixed (often non‑recourse) mortgages, falling interest rates and rising home prices, to quantify how refinancing‑facilitated equity extraction amplified systemic mortgage losses.

title: Macroeconomic Effects from Government Purchases and Taxes
author: Robert J. Barro, Charles J. Redlick
context: This paper uses U.S. annual national macro data (roughly 1912–2006, with main samples starting 1917/1930/1939/1950 and ending 2006) to estimate causal effects of government purchases—especially federal defense spending tied to WWI, WWII and the Korean War—and changes in average marginal income‑tax rates on per‑capita real GDP and its components. Units are national annual observations (per‑capita GDP, defense and non‑defense purchases, AMTR). Identification leverages war-driven exogenous variation, Ramey’s defense‑news, Romer‑Romer narrative tax shocks, and time‑series IV regressions.

title: The Fundamental Law of Road Congestion: Evidence from US cities
author: Gilles Duranton, Matthew A. Turner
context: This analysis examines the effect of public transportation provision—measured as peak‑service large bus counts from Section 15 reports (circa 1984, 1994, 2004)—on vehicle‑kilometers traveled (VKT) across US metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) and their urbanized areas over the 1983–2003 period (with travel data from NPTS 1995 and 2001). For transit, the authors instrument bus supply (alongside historical road instruments) using the 1972 share of the Democratic vote to identify causal effects.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Paying a Premium on Your Premium? Consolidation in the U.S. Health Insurance Industry
author: Leemore Dafny, Mark Duggan, Subramaniam Ramanarayanan
context: This paper uses employer‑plan panel data from 1998–2006 covering over 10 million Americans across 139 U.S. local health‑insurance markets (3‑digit ZIP definitions) and analyzes employer‑market‑year plans and provider occupation‑MSA outcomes. Exploiting the 1999 Aetna–Prudential national merger (and DOJ‑required Texas divestitures) as plausibly exogenous shocks to local insurer HHI, the authors estimate effects of insurer consolidation on employer‑sponsored premiums and provider earnings and employment, controlling for plan design, demographics, local economic and hospital market conditions.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

title: Paulson's Gift
author: Pietro Veronesi, Luigi Zingales
context: This paper studies the immediate effects of the U.S. Treasury–FDIC intervention announced Oct 13–14, 2008 (the “Paulson Plan”) on the largest U.S. banks (nine–ten major commercial banks and surviving investment banks). Using firm-level market data (CDS, bond/implied debt values, common and preferred equity, derivatives) around Oct 10–14, 2008 and an extended Merton-style calibration, it evaluates changes in enterprise value, default probabilities, and taxpayers’ costs during the 2008 U.S. financial crisis.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Banking Deregulations, Financing Constraints, and Firm Entry Size
author: William Kerr, Ramana Nanda
context: Using the US Census Bureau Longitudinal Business Database (annual establishment-level data, 1976 onward; entrants roughly 1977–1996), the paper examines state-level interstate branch‑banking deregulations (staggered across US states, 1970s–mid‑1990s) and their effect on entry rates of private‑sector establishments. The triplet focuses on interstate (not intrastate) reforms and exploits event‑study/staggered‑adoption variation with annual lead/lag indicators (10 years before and after reform), controlling for state and year fixed effects, clustering standard errors by state, and weighting states by pre‑period startup employment.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 15499|interstate banking deregulations|time to reach max size
title: Banking Deregulations, Financing Constraints, and Firm Entry Size
author: William Kerr, Ramana Nanda
context: This empirical study uses US Census Bureau Longitudinal Business Database establishment-level employment data (annual, 1976 onward, focusing on entrants roughly 1977–1996) to examine how state-level intrastate and interstate branch-banking deregulations (1970s–mid-1990s), enacted at different times across US states, affected entry sizes and early growth of new private‑sector establishments (startups). The paper exploits staggered reform timing across states, conditions on four-year survival for some analyses, and controls for state and year effects to study financing-constraint effects on initial and near‑term firm size.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

title: Accountability and Flexibility in Public Schools: Evidence from Boston's Charters and Pilots
author: Atila Abdulkadiroglu, Joshua Angrist, Susan Dynarski, Thomas J. Kane, Parag Pathak
context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of attending Boston charter and pilot public schools using student-level administrative and MCAS test-score data from roughly 2001–2008. The unit of analysis is individual Boston Public School students (elementary, middle, and high school cohorts). The study exploits randomized admissions lotteries at over-subscribed charter and some pilot schools as a quasi-experiment, compares lottery-based IV estimates to observational regressions with baseline scores and demographics, and situates findings in Massachusetts’ charter/pilot institutional setting (union rules, charter caps).

title: Banking System Control, Capital Allocation, and Economy Performance
author: Randall Morck, M. Deniz Yavuz, Bernard Yeung
context: This cross-country empirical study examines 44 countries’ banking systems circa 2001 and economy-level outcomes mainly over 1993–2003 (with some analyses using 1963–2003 or up to 2004 and crisis counts through 2008). The unit of observation is countries; the authors measure fractions of large banks controlled by tycoons/families, the state, or widely held investors and relate these to capital-allocation efficiency (Wurgler elasticities), nonperforming loans, banking crises, GDP per capita and TFP growth, inequality, and entry barriers. Data come from The Banker/Bankscope, UNIDO, WDI, and Penn World Tables.

title: Compulsory Licensing - Evidence from the Trading with the Enemy Act
author: Petra Moser, Alessandra Voena
context: The paper studies U.S. chemical invention across 8,422 USPTO subclasses from 1875–1939, exploiting the 1917 Trading with the Enemy Act (TWEA) and subsequent confiscation/licensing of German enemy patents (licenses mainly issued 1919–1921) as an exogenous shock. Using yearly patent counts by domestic inventors (subclass-year units) and firm-level (DuPont) patents, it assesses how compulsory licensing of enemy-owned patents affected domestic innovation in the U.S. chemical industry.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other panel regressions

title: Criminal Recidivism after Prison and Electronic Monitoring
author: Rafael Di Tella, Ernesto Schargrodsky
context: Analyzes male detainees (age <40) in the Province of Buenos Aires, Argentina, using administrative records from 1998–2007. Compares recidivism (re-arrest) for individuals released under electronic monitoring versus prison; EM was used mainly for pre-trial detainees, limited to 300 simultaneous bracelets, and allocation depended on judges on duty (assigned by lottery) exhibiting large ideological variation. Units are individual offenders; key institutional features include limited EM capacity, judges’ exogenous rotation, and matches on age, crime, prior imprisonment, and detention timing.

title: Adjustment Costs, Firm Responses, and Micro vs. Macro Labor Supply Elasticities: Evidence from Danish Tax Records
author: Raj Chetty, John N. Friedman, Tore Olsen, Luigi Pistaferri
context: Using Danish administrative tax and matched employer–employee registers from 1994–2001, the paper studies individual wage earners (and the self-employed) in Denmark to examine labor-supply responses to a three-bracket non-linear income tax (notably a large top‑bracket kink) and to smaller tax reforms. The institutional setting features occupation-level collective bargaining, pension deductibility, and mechanically indexed bracket cutoffs; analysis uses bunching at kinks, cross-year reforms, and occupational variation to study how search frictions and firm-set hours constraints shape micro versus macro elasticities.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Earnings Determination and Taxes: Evidence from a Cohort Based Payroll Tax Reform in Greece
author: Emmanuel Saez, Manos Matsaganis, Panos Tsakloglou
context: This paper studies the long-run effects of a 1992 Greek pension/payroll-tax reform that, from Jan 1, 1993, raised the monthly earnings cap for social security contributions for new entrants. Using full-population administrative IKA data on individuals who first entered 1988–1997 and linked March 2004–2007 job records (workers and firms), the authors compare cohorts just before and after the cutoff to estimate how employer and employee payroll taxes affected gross/posted/net earnings, labor supply, turnover, and pay growth.

title: Earnings Determination and Taxes: Evidence from a Cohort Based Payroll Tax Reform in Greece

title: Globalization, Markups and U.S. Welfare
author: Robert C. Feenstra, David E. Weinstein
context: This paper studies how globalization affected firm markups and U.S. welfare between 1992 and 2005, using U.S. import and domestic production data at HS 4-digit and firm-country-product (HS-10) levels. The setting is the U.S. merchandise market; units of observation include exporting countries, individual firms/products, and manufacturing sectors. Under a monopolistic-competition framework with translog preferences, the authors exploit changes in import shares, Herfindahl concentration, and product entry/exit to structurally estimate demand elasticities, markups, and welfare impacts.

title: The Influence of the Home Owners' Loan Corporation on Housing Markets During the 1930s
author: Price V. Fishback, Alfonso Flores-Lagunes, William Horrace, Shawn E. Kantor, Jaret Treber
context: This paper studies the Home Owners’ Loan Corporation’s (HOLC) mortgage purchases and refinancing during 1933–1936 across the United States, using county-level nonfarm housing data (≈2,854 counties) from 1920–1940. It examines how HOLC interventions—buying ~1 million troubled mortgages, replacing toxic lender assets with government bonds and refinancing borrowers—affected county-level owned-home values, rents, and counts of homeowners and renters. The setting is U.S. nonfarm housing markets during the New Deal, with variation in HOLC lending and office proximity exploited for causal inference.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: How University Endowments Respond to Financial Market Shocks: Evidence and Implications
author: Jeffrey Brown, Stephen G. Dimmock, Jun-Koo Kang, Scott Weisbenner
context: This paper studies U.S. doctoral university endowments using annual NACUBO/IPEDS panel data from roughly 1986–2009, exploiting variation in endowment returns (and shocks normalized by endowment-to-cost ratios) across university‑years — including the 2001–02 tech‑bubble collapse and 2008–09 financial crisis. The unit is the doctoral university/endowment‑year; institutional context includes multi‑year smoothing payout policies, state UMIFA/UPMIFA rules, public vs. private status, and presidents’ tenure benchmarks; identification leverages within‑university and state-by-year-by-public/private comparisons.

title: Targeting the Poor: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Indonesia
author: Vivi Alatas, Abhijit Banerjee, Rema Hanna, Benjamin A. Olken, Julia Tobias
context: This paper reports a randomized field experiment in 640 sub‑villages across North Sumatra, South Sulawesi, and Central Java, Indonesia, conducted in late 2008–early 2009. Units are households within sub‑villages; a baseline household consumption survey (Nov–Dec 2008) defined poverty (≈PPP$2/day). The Indonesian Central Statistics Bureau and an NGO implemented a one‑time Rp30,000 cash transfer using three randomized targeting regimes—proxy‑means test, community ranking, and a hybrid—to compare targeting accuracy, elite capture, effort, and satisfaction.

title: The Impact of Competition on Management Quality: Evidence from Public Hospitals
author: Nicholas Bloom, Carol Propper, Stephan Seiler, John Van Reenen
context: This empirical study (management survey conducted in 2006) examines public acute hospitals (hospital trusts) in England—100 hospitals, 161 interviews in cardiology and orthopaedics—and links management scores to clinical and financial outcomes (AMI mortality, MRSA, waiting lists) using 2005–2006 outcomes. Identification exploits England’s centralized NHS and political incentives: the share of marginal Labour constituencies (1997) within 30km is used as an instrument for the number of nearby public hospitals during a mid-1990s–early-2000s consolidation period when closures were centrally decided by the Department of Health.

title: Assessing the Incidence and Efficiency of a Prominent Place Based Policy
author: Matias Busso, Jesse Gregory, Patrick M. Kline
context: This paper evaluates Round I (1993) federal urban Empowerment Zones in six U.S. cities (Atlanta, Baltimore, Chicago, Detroit, New York City, Philadelphia/Camden) over the 1990s using tract-level microdata (1990 and 2000 Decennial Census) and the Longitudinal Business Database. Units are census tracts, firms, and workers (using Journey-to-Work to separate resident vs. commuter employment). The policy combined employment tax credits (from 1994) and $100M SSBG block grants. Identification compares treated tracts to rejected and later-round applicant tracts with DiD and reweighting/matching-based checks.

title: Assessing the Incidence and Efficiency of a Prominent Place Based Policy
author: Matias Busso, Jesse Gregory, Patrick M. Kline
context: Focuses on Round I (1993) Empowerment Zones in six U.S. cities over the 1990s, using 1990 and 2000 Decennial Census microdata (Journey-to-Work) at the census-tract and individual-worker level. For this triplet the outcome is individual hourly wages of zone-resident workers who work inside the zone (log hourly wages, with composition-adjusted and unadjusted estimates). Treatment is EZ designation (employment tax credits from 1994 plus SSBG block grants); causal estimates compare treated tracts to rejected/later-round applicant tracts using DiD with propensity-score trimming and regression reweighting.

title: Intellectual Property Rights and Innovation: Evidence from the Human Genome
author: Heidi L. Williams
context: This empirical study examines how Celera’s short-term intellectual property on human genes affected subsequent scientific research and product development. It uses gene-level observations (≈27,882 genes; ≈1,682 initially held by Celera) linked to publications, genotype–phenotype links, and diagnostic-test availability. Identification exploits timing variation in sequencing and public re-sequencing around 1999–2003 (outcomes observed through 2009) in the Human Genome Project vs. Celera institutional setting, where Celera used contract-based IP that lapsed once public resequencing occurred.
identification_methods: event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Public Monopoly and Economic Efficiency: Evidence from the Pennsylvania Liquor Control Board's Entry Decisions
author: Katja Seim, Joel Waldfogel
context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s state-run retail monopoly for wine and spirits (the Pennsylvania Liquor Control Board) using store-level daily and weekly sales, wholesale costs, and geocoded store locations for 2005, linked to 2000 Census tract demographics. Units of observation include PLCB stores, Census tracts (consumers over 21), and store-week/day sales. The PLCB sets uniform retail prices via markup rules and controls entry and store locations; the authors estimate a structural spatial demand model and simulate profit- and welfare-maximizing store networks and counterfactual private-entry equilibria.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Short-Run Subsidies and Long-Run Adoption of New Health Products: Evidence from a Field Experiment
author: Pascaline Dupas
context: Field experiment in Busia District, Western Kenya (2007–2008) with 1,120 rural households offered vouchers for Olyset long-lasting insecticidal nets. Phase 1 randomly assigned subsidy levels (0–250 Ksh) with short-run follow-up (~2 months); Phase 2 delivered an unannounced uniform-priced offer (150 Ksh) about 12 months later. Malaria-endemic setting with pre-existing subsidized nets; units are households (and geographically-defined neighbors) redeeming vouchers at local retailers. The study focuses on a new LLIN (Olyset) that is demonstrably more comfortable (lower non-monetary usage cost) than the status-quo nets, a feature the paper argues drove positive long-run adoption from short-run subsidies.

title: Firm Dynamics, Job Turnover, and Wage Distributions in an Open Economy
author: A. Kerem Coşar, Nezih Guner, James Tybout
context: The paper analyzes Colombian manufacturing plants (establishments with ≥10 workers) using annual plant-level panel data primarily from 1981–1990 (pre-reform) and evaluates post-1990s outcomes following trade liberalization and 1991 labor-market reforms. It studies how reductions in trade frictions (tariffs and iceberg costs), falling firing costs (dismissal costs), and increased global integration affected firm dynamics, job turnover, unemployment, and cross-firm wage distributions via a calibrated dynamic general-equilibrium model.

title: Beyond GDP? Welfare across Countries and Time
author: Charles I. Jones, Peter J. Klenow
context: This paper constructs a consumption-equivalent welfare metric combining consumption, leisure, mortality, and inequality to compare living standards across countries and over time. It uses household microdata for 13 countries (e.g., US, France, UK, Italy, Spain, China, India, Brazil, Indonesia, Mexico, Russia, South Africa, Malawi) spanning mainly the 1980s–2000s and broader macro datasets (Penn World Tables, UNU-WIDER, WHO) for roughly 150+ countries. The unit of observation is individuals/households; the authors apply an expected-utility (Rawlsian) framework, calibrate preference parameters (β, Frisch elasticity, value of life), and compute equivalent-variation welfare levels and growth rates.

title: How Does Your Kindergarten Classroom Affect Your Earnings? Evidence From Project STAR
author: Raj Chetty, John N. Friedman, Nathaniel Hilger, Emmanuel Saez, Diane Whitmore Schanzenbach, Danny Yagan
context: This paper evaluates long-term impacts of Project STAR, a randomized classroom-size and teacher-assignment experiment conducted in 79 Tennessee schools during 1985–1989 among a cohort born ~1979–1980. It links K–3 STAR test scores and randomized classroom/teacher assignments to administrative tax and 1098-T records from the late 1990s through 2007 to measure outcomes (college attendance, college quality, earnings at ages ~25–27, home ownership, retirement savings). Units are individual students randomized within schools to small (≈15) or regular (≈22) classes.
identification_methods: RCT; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; event studies

title: How did increased competition affect credit ratings?
author: Bo Becker, Todd Milbourn
outcome: Correlation of ratings with bond yields
context: This empirical study examines how the rise of Fitch as a third credit rater affected incumbent ratings quality in the U.S. corporate bond market, using issuer- and issue-level data from roughly 1995–2006 (with discussion through 2007). Units of observation include individual bond ratings, firm (issuer) ratings, bond trades, and 2-digit NAICS industry-year cells. Identification exploits cross-industry variation in Fitch’s market share, industry/year and bond/firm fixed effects, and institutional details such as the issuer‑paid revenue model, NRSRO status, and Fitch’s 2005 inclusion in the Lehman index.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Estimating the Cream Skimming Effect of School Choice
author: Joseph G. Altonji, Ching-I Huang, Christopher R. Taber
outcome: Public school stayers' high school graduation rate
context: The paper analyzes U.S. secondary-school students using the NELS:88 cohort to assess how a private-school voucher/school-choice program would affect public-school “stayers.” Authors measure student and school characteristics, estimate a private-school entry model and peer effects on high-school graduation, and simulate voucher-induced selection (cream-skimming) under alternative assumptions. The setting is national (U.S.) student cohorts and school peer groups, focusing on policy-driven student reallocation and resulting peer externalities on graduation outcomes.

triplet_key: 16665|fair housing act of 1968|black likelihood of suburban residence
title: Race and Home Ownership from the Civil War to the Present
author: William J. Collins, Robert A. Margo
context: This triplet refers to an analysis of CPS microdata testing whether the U.S. Fair Housing Act of 1968 changed black households’ likelihood of suburban residence. The paper examines a structural-break around 1968 in household-level suburban residence in U.S. metropolitan areas using CPS panels/pooled cross-sections.

title: Consumer Spending and the Economic Stimulus Payments of 2008
author: Jonathan A. Parker, Nicholas S. Souleles, David S. Johnson, Robert McClelland
context: This paper studies U.S. household-level responses to the 2008 Economic Stimulus Act payments (mid-2008), using Consumer Expenditure Survey interviews June 2008–March 2009. title: Trade Induced Technical Change? The Impact of Chinese Imports on Innovation, IT and Productivity
author: Nicholas Bloom, Mirko Draca, John Van Reenen
context: This paper uses firm- and plant-level panels (up to half a million firms; IT establishment sample ≈160,000) across 12 European countries over 1996–2007 to estimate how rising Chinese import competition — especially after China’s 2001 WTO accession and the phased abolition of textile/apparel quotas (MFA reforms in 2002 and 2005) — affected firm innovation (patents, R&D), IT adoption, TFP, employment and survival. Identification exploits differential industry exposure to quota removal and pre-existing Chinese export shares, with firm and industry fixed effects and trend controls.
