title: Capital-Market Effects of Securities Regulation: Prior Conditions, Implementation, and Enforcement
author: Hans B. Christensen, Luzi Hail, Christian Leuz
context: The paper studies firm-level stock-market liquidity across 26 European countries from 2001–2011 (about 112,260 firm-quarter observations, ~4,846 firms), exploiting staggered national transposition dates of two EU directives—the Market Abuse Directive (MAD) and the Transparency Directive (TPD)—that tightened insider-trading and disclosure rules. It analyzes regulated versus unregulated market listings, constructs a liquidity factor from bid-ask spreads, price impact, zero returns, and trading costs, and examines heterogeneity by prior regulatory quality, supervisory powers, penalties, and regulator staffing while controlling for other FSAP directives.

title: Limits to Arbitrage and Hedging: Evidence from Commodity Markets
author: Viral V. Acharya, Lars A. Lochstoer, Tarun Ramadorai
outcome: Producer share of futures risk premium
context: This paper studies U.S. oil and gas commodity markets from 1980–2006, combining a two-period theoretical model with empirical tests using quarterly NYMEX futures and spot prices, U.S. inventory data, and firm-level Compustat/EDGAR data on 525 oil and gas producers. Units include commodity futures contracts, aggregate U.S. inventories, and producer firms (hedging disclosures, default-risk proxies Zmijewski, naive EDF, 3‑year returns). title: The U.S. Left Behind: The Rise of IPO Activity Around the World
author: Craig Doidge, G. Andrew Karolyi, René M. Stulz
context: This empirical study analyzes 29,361 IPOs across 89 countries from January 1990 through December 2007, using firm-level IPO observations aggregated to country-year measures (domestic and global IPO counts and proceeds). It examines how home‑country institutions (legal origin, investor protection, disclosure, enforcement), economic and financial development, and global market conditions influenced firms’ decisions to pursue domestic versus global IPOs during a period of rapid financial globalization and liberalization.

title: Reshaping Institutions: Evidence on Aid Impacts Using a Pre-Analysis Plan
author: Katherine Casey, Rachel Glennerster, Edward Miguel
context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of the GoBifo community-driven development (CDD) program in Sierra Leone using a village-level randomized rollout. Baseline surveys were collected in 2005, the program ran 2006–2009 (block grants of roughly $4,667 per village with participation requirements for women and youth), and follow-up and structured community activities were fielded in 2009 across 236 villages and about 2,832 households in Bombali and Bonthe districts. Units of analysis are villages and households in a post-conflict decentralization setting where VDCs, bank accounts, and participatory rules were introduced to reshape local institutions.

title: Intermittency and the Value of Renewable Energy
author: Gautam Gowrisankaran, Stanley S. Reynolds, Mario Samano
context: This paper evaluates the social costs of large-scale solar PV in the Tucson, Arizona electricity grid (Tucson Electric Power) using hourly and 15-minute data from May 2011–April 2012. Units are hours (system operator decisions), generation units, and 58 geographically dispersed PV sites. The context includes Arizona’s Renewable Portfolio Standard and exogenous solar capacity scenarios (10–20% penetration). The authors use day‑ahead weather forecasts, generator cost and outage data, and a structural welfare-maximizing model to quantify intermittency, reserve operations, and CO2 impacts.

triplet_key: 17121|debt and deposit guarantees|exposures to global, us and domestic factors
title: Global Crises and Equity Market Contagion
author: Geert Bekaert, Michael Ehrmann, Marcel Fratzscher, Arnaud J. Mehl
outcome: exposures to global, US and domestic factors
context: This paper analyzes transmission of the 2007–09 global financial crisis using weekly US-dollar returns for 415 country-sector equity portfolios across 55 countries from Jan 1995–Mar 2009 (crisis defined Aug 7, 2007; robustness to Lehman Sept 15, 2008). It contrasts US, global financial-sector, and domestic factors, exploiting cross-sectional variation in banking exposures, trade and financial openness, macro fundamentals (e.g., current account, budget balance, political risk), and crisis-era banking policies (debt/deposit guarantees, capital injections) to explain heterogeneity in contagion.

triplet_key: 17149|collective government bailout guarantee|financial sector market value
title: Too-Systemic-To-Fail: What Option Markets Imply About Sector-wide Government Guarantees
author: Bryan T. Kelly, Hanno Lustig, Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh
context: This paper analyzes US equity option and stock data for S&P500 firms and SPDR sector ETFs from January 2003 to June 2009, with emphasis on the financial‑crisis period (August 2007–June 2009) and key policy dates (e.g., TARP). Units are individual bank stocks, sector ETFs (financials versus non‑financials), and their option contracts traded on the CBOE. The institutional setting is the US financial sector facing government bailout programs and sector‑wide guarantees that can truncate aggregate tail losses.

title: The Oregon Health Insurance Experiment: Evidence from the First Year
author: Amy Finkelstein, Sarah Taubman, Bill Wright, Mira Bernstein, Jonathan Gruber, Joseph P. Newhouse, Heidi Allen, Katherine Baicker, The Oregon Health Study Group
context: This paper studies the causal impact of expanding Medicaid via Oregon’s 2008 Medicaid lottery. From Jan–Sep 2008 the state randomly selected low-income adults (ages 19–64) on a waiting list for the opportunity to apply for OHP Standard; outcomes are measured through Sept 2009 (~16 months). The units are uninsured low-income adults/households (below ~100% FPL) who signed up for the lottery. The institutional setting includes Medicaid eligibility rules (citizens/legal immigrants, six months uninsured, income/assets limits) and uses administrative and survey data.

triplet_key: 17317|accommodative monetary policy|negative effects of fiscal volatility shocks
title: Fiscal Volatility Shocks and Economic Activity
author: Jesús Fernández-Villaverde, Pablo A. Guerrón-Quintana, Keith Kuester, Juan Rubio-Ramírez
outcome: Negative effects of fiscal volatility shocks
context: Analyzes how time-varying uncertainty about U.S. fiscal policy affects macro activity. The authors use quarterly consolidated government data (federal, state, local) on spending and average tax rates (labor, capital, consumption) from 1970:Q1–2010:Q2 to estimate fiscal rules with stochastic volatility. They then simulate a calibrated New Keynesian DSGE model to compute impulse responses. The setting emphasizes elevated deficits and public debt after the 2008–09 financial crisis and uncertainty over the timing and mix of fiscal consolidation; units are aggregate fiscal instruments and macro aggregates.

triplet_key: 17338|fiscal stimulus payments (tax rebates)|nondurable consumption in receiving quarter
title: A Model of the Consumption Response to Fiscal Stimulus Payments
author: Greg Kaplan, Giovanni L. Violante
context: The paper studies U.S. household consumption responses to one‑off fiscal stimulus (tax rebate) episodes, with emphasis on the 2001 tax rebate (checks mailed Jul–Sep 2001; average ~$480–$500) and discussion of 2008–09 episodes. Units are households observed in consumption panels (CEX), balance‑sheet cross‑sections (SCF) and earnings panels (PSID). The institutional setting is lump‑sum federal rebates with randomized timing by Social Security number, under liquidity constraints, heterogeneous liquid versus illiquid wealth, borrowing limits, and the macro backdrop around the 2001 downturn.
identification_methods: RCT; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

triplet_key: 17347|50 percentage point tariff increase in chemicals|government welfare
title: Trade Wars and Trade Talks with Data
author: Ralph Ossa
treatment: 50 percentage point tariff increase in chemicals
context: This triplet refers to a counterfactual 50 percentage-point increase in the US import tariff on chemicals simulated in the paper's calibrated 2007 multi-country, multi-industry general-equilibrium model (regions: Brazil, China, EU, India, Japan, US, Rest of World; 33 sectors). The model uses CES demand elasticities estimated from UN trade data (1994–2008) and GTAP8/Market Access Map/TRAINS tariff data; the unit of observation is country-industry bilateral trade flows and the outcome is US government welfare (real-income measure) implied by the GE counterfactual.

title: Trade Wars and Trade Talks with Data
author: Ralph Ossa
context: This paper uses 2007 industry-level data covering seven regions (Brazil, China, EU, India, Japan, US, Rest of World) and 33 agriculture/manufacturing sectors, estimating CES demand elasticities from UN trade data (1994–2008) and calibrating to GTAP8 and tariff sources (Market Access Map, TRAINS). Units are bilateral industry-region trade flows, prices, and tariffs. These observables are embedded in a multi-country, multi-industry general-equilibrium model with iceberg trade costs, monopolistic competition, and political-economy weights to simulate optimal, Nash, and cooperative tariff and welfare counterfactuals.

triplet_key: 17417|oil and gas revenue per capita|deforestation rate (short-run)
title: The Political Economy of Deforestation in the Tropics
author: Robin Burgess, Matthew Hansen, Benjamin A. Olken, Peter Potapov, Stefanie Sieber
treatment: Oil and gas revenue per capita
context: This paper uses annual MODIS-derived forest-cover data (2001–2008) across Indonesia’s main forest islands (Sumatra, Kalimantan, Sulawesi, Papua), aggregated to district and province units, combined with administrative changes following post-Soeharto decentralization (district splits 1998–2008), staggered introduction of direct district head elections (from 2005), and district oil-and-gas revenue-sharing data to study how local political incentives affect legal and illegal logging across Forest Estate zones (production, conversion, protection, conservation).

title: Payout Taxes and the Allocation of Investment
author: Bo Becker, Marcus Jacob, Martin Jacob
context: Using an international firm-level panel from 1990–2008 (7,661 non-financial firms, 81,222 firm‑year observations) across 25 countries, the paper exploits extensive dividend and capital‑gains payout tax variation (including ~15 major reforms and dozens of discrete rate changes) to study how payout taxation affects firms’ investment allocation. Firms are the unit of observation; the setting compares cash‑rich versus cash‑poor firms within country‑years under varying tax regimes (dividend/capital gains, imputation systems) to infer how taxes alter internal vs. external equity costs and investment decisions.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: The Real Effects of Hedge Fund Activism: Productivity, Asset Allocation, and Labor Outcomes
author: Alon Brav, Wei Jiang, Hyunseob Kim
context: This analysis examines the effect of hedge fund activism on IT investment at U.S. manufacturing plants targeted by activists (identified primarily via SEC Schedule 13D filings). Because the CMF/ASM report computer capital only from 2001, the IT-investment tests focus on plant observations from 2001–2009 (about 36.6% of the full sample), matching activism events (mainly 1994–2007) to plant-level Census data and firm databases, with outcomes measured as plant IT capital expenditures (scaled by lagged capital) and related productivity.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions; others

title: The Real Effects of Hedge Fund Activism: Productivity, Asset Allocation, and Labor Outcomes
author: Alon Brav, Wei Jiang, Hyunseob Kim
context: This paper studies the long-term real effects of hedge‑fund activism in the United States by matching activism events (mainly 1994–2007, with plant data through 2009) to plant‑level manufacturing data (Census CMF/ASM/LBD) and firm databases (Compustat/CRSP). The units are manufacturing plants and their parent firms targeted by activists (identified primarily via SEC Schedule 13D filings). The institutional setting is U.S. capital markets and disclosure rules; activists typically hold concentrated stakes and push strategic, asset‑sale, governance, and capital‑allocation changes, with outcomes measured in plant TFP, asset reallocation, employment, wages, and IT investment.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions; others

title: The Welfare Economics of Default Options in 401(k) Plans
author: B. Douglas Bernheim, Andrey Fradkin, Igor Popov
context: This triplet pertains to a theoretical analysis within the paper's behavioral models of 401(k) default contribution options. The authors evaluate how varying penalties for passive choice, alone and combined with defaults, affect aggregate worker welfare using the Bernheim–Rangel behavioral welfare framework and calibrated structural models of employer-sponsored U.S. 401(k) plans with employer matching and contribution caps. The analysis is model-based rather than directly estimated from the three firms' administrative distributions.

triplet_key: 17714|making consumer bankruptcy more costly|volume of credit and insurance
title: Human Capital Risk, Contract Enforcement, and the Macroeconomy
author: Tom Krebs, Moritz Kuhn, Mark L. J. Wright
context: This paper develops a theoretical model of risky, non-pledgeable human capital with limited contract enforcement and empirically documents life-cycle under-insurance using U.S. household microdata (Survey of Consumer Finances, pooled 1992–2007) and U.S. life-table mortality data (1991–2000). The unit of analysis is households; the institutional setting is U.S. private life-insurance markets and consumer bankruptcy (Chapter 7) rules. The model is calibrated to U.S. earnings and mortality profiles and used to simulate effects of bankruptcy-code reforms on credit, insurance, and growth.

triplet_key: 17776|increased state transfers|black teenagers' school enrollment and educational quality
title: Valuing the Vote:  The Redistribution of Voting Rights and State Funds Following the Voting Rights Act of 1965
author: Elizabeth U. Cascio, Ebonya L. Washington
outcome: Black teenagers' school enrollment and educational quality
context: The paper analyzes U.S. southern counties (1957–1982), exploiting the 1965 Voting Rights Act’s removal of literacy tests in seven "treatment" states versus comparison states (Arkansas, Florida, Tennessee, Texas). Using county-level real per-capita state transfers (largely for education) and election turnout, it documents that counties which experienced increases in state transfers after the VRA also saw increases in the share of black teenagers enrolled in school and in measures of black children’s educational quality.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; IV; PSM; other panel regressions

title: Aiding Conflict: The Impact of U.S. Food Aid on Civil War
author: Nathan Nunn, Nancy Qian
context: This paper uses annual country‑year data for 134 non‑OECD countries from 1972–2006 to estimate how U.S. wheat food aid (PL‑480/USDA shipments) affects civil conflict incidence, onset and duration. Identification exploits exogenous year‑to‑year variation in U.S. wheat production (weather‑driven) interacting with each country's historical propensity to receive U.S. food aid, controlling for country fixed effects and region‑year shocks. Outcomes are civil and interstate armed conflict (UCDP/PRIO); analyses include panel regressions, IV 2SLS, and hazard models for conflict duration.

title: The Long-Term Effects of Unemployment Insurance Extensions on Employment
author: Johannes F. Schmieder, Till M. von Wachter, Stefan Bender
context: This paper uses administrative social-security records for Germany (1975–2004) to study unemployment insurance (UI) duration effects for workers who became unemployed 1987–1999. The unit of observation is individual employees (ages 40–43 meeting experience criteria). Policy variation arises from a statutory discontinuity at age 42 that increased maximum UI from 12 to 18 months for eligible claimants. Outcomes are days on UI benefits and days in nonemployment up to five years after the initial UI claim, with dynamic effects analyzed around the RD cutoff.

title: The Causal Effects of an Industrial Policy
author: Chiara Criscuolo, Ralf Martin, Henry Overman, John Van Reenen
context: This paper studies the causal effects of the UK Regional Selective Assistance (RSA) investment-subsidy program on manufacturing plants and firms in Britain over 1986–2004. Using administrative panel data on plants, firms and program applicants (SAMIS, IDBR, ARD), and area aggregates at ward and Travel-to-Work levels, it exploits exogenous EU-driven changes to area eligibility (maps in 1993 and 2000) and Net Grant Equivalent rates to estimate impacts on employment, investment, productivity, firm entry/exit, and local unemployment.

title: The Causal Effects of an Industrial Policy
author: Chiara Criscuolo, Ralf Martin, Henry Overman, John Van Reenen
context: This triplet refers specifically to plant-level effects for plants belonging to small firms (defined in the paper as firms with under 150 employees) in UK manufacturing over 1986–2004. Using administrative panel data (SAMIS, IDBR, ARD) and ward-level Net Grant Equivalent instruments derived from exogenous EU-driven changes to assisted-area maps (notably 1993 and 2000), the paper estimates plant fixed-effects instrumental-variables models (Table 2, Panel B, column 8) to identify the causal impact of the RSA investment subsidy on employment for small firms.

title: The Causal Effects of an Industrial Policy
author: Chiara Criscuolo, Ralf Martin, Henry Overman, John Van Reenen
context: This paper studies the causal effects of the UK Regional Selective Assistance (RSA) investment-subsidy program on manufacturing plants and firms in Britain over 1986–2004. Using administrative panel data on plants, firms and program applicants (SAMIS, IDBR, ARD), and area aggregates at ward and Travel-to-Work levels, it exploits exogenous EU-driven changes to area eligibility (maps in 1993 and 2000) and Net Grant Equivalent rates to estimate impacts on employment, investment, productivity, firm entry/exit, and local unemployment.

title: Winning by Losing: Evidence on the Long-Run Effects of Mergers
author: Ulrike Malmendier, Enrico Moretti, Florian S. Peters
context: This empirical study uses contested takeover bids from Thomson One merged with CRSP/Compustat/I/B/E/S, primarily covering U.S. public bidders from 1985–2012 (and an international extension). The unit is bidding firms (winners and losers) in contests with multiple public bidders; the authors focus on long-duration (close) contests and track stock and accounting outcomes three years before and after deal completion to estimate the causal effect of winning a merger, using losing bidders as within-contest counterfactuals.

title: The Anatomy of a Credit Crisis: The Boom and Bust in Farm Land Prices in the United States in the 1920s.
author: Raghuram Rajan, Rodney Ramcharan
context: This paper studies U.S. county-level farmland markets and local banking from roughly 1900–1960, focusing on the WWI-era commodity boom (1917–1920) and the subsequent 1920s collapse. Units are counties (with banks, farms, and bank failures as sub-units). It uses Census and Department of Agriculture land-price and farm data plus county bank counts/deposits and exploits state banking regulations (unit vs branch banking), deposit insurance, Federal Reserve district and state borders, and pre-boom bank presence to isolate exogenous variation in local credit availability and its effects on prices, indebtedness, bank failures, and long-run outcomes.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Bankruptcy as Implicit Health Insurance
author: Neale Mahoney
context: This paper studies U.S. households (primarily 1996–2005 survey waves) across states to assess how state-level bankruptcy asset exemption laws (Chapter 7) and hospital credit provision (EMTALA/charity care practices) create implicit “bankruptcy” health insurance. Using household-level data (MEPS, SIPP, PSID) and a web survey of uninsured adults, the author exploits cross-state and within-state variation in exemption rules to analyze uninsured households’ out-of-pocket medical payments and insurance take-up, and calibrates a 2005 microsimulation to evaluate policy welfare implications.

triplet_key: 18125|elimination of rent control|price of never-controlled housing
title: Housing Market Spillovers: Evidence from the  End of Rent Control in Cambridge Massachusetts
author: David H. Autor, Christopher J. Palmer, Parag A. Pathak
context: This paper studies the causal effect of the abrupt 1995 elimination of stringent rent control in Cambridge, Massachusetts (statewide referendum Nov 1994, effective Jan 1995) on residential property values and spillovers. Using assessor data (1994, 2004), transaction data (1988–2005), building permits, and voter-registration panels, the unit of analysis is individual residential units and transactions (houses and condominiums) and small-area neighborhoods. Cambridge’s pre-1995 ordinance covered non-owner-occupied units built before 1969 (no vacancy decontrol); the study exploits spatial variation in nearby controlled-unit share to identify effects.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Is Conflicted Investment Advice Better than No Advice?
author: John Chalmers, Jonathan Reuter
context: The paper studies Oregon University System employees participating in the Optional Retirement Plan (ORP) from October 1996 through December 2009, exploiting a November 2007 regime change that removed a broker-sold provider (HIGH). Using matched administrative and account-level data on individual ORP participants (contributions, holdings, demographics) across providers (HIGH with brokers, LOW, NEW with target-date funds), the authors analyze how access to broker recommendations (limited to asset allocation and fund selection) affects fees, risk exposures, and after-fee returns.

title: Competition and Ideological Diversity: Historical Evidence from US Newspapers
author: Matthew Gentzkow, Jesse M. Shapiro, Michael Sinkinson
context: This study analyzes US daily newspaper markets centered on 1924 (with historical panel data back to the late 19th century), covering ~1,910 city markets and 12,188 hinterland towns. Units include newspapers (entry, political affiliation, prices), households (town-level circulation/readership), and advertisers. The institutional setting is early twentieth-century US print media—radio nascent and newspapers the main daily news source—where many papers declared Republican or Democratic affiliations. The paper combines audited 1924 circulation by town, directories, readership surveys, and cost/revenue records to estimate a structural two-sided market model.

title: Endogenous Skill Acquisition and Export Manufacturing in Mexico
author: David Atkin
context: This paper studies how the 1986–2000 expansion of export-oriented manufacturing in Mexico, driven by trade liberalization (GATT 1986, NAFTA 1994) and Maquiladora growth, affected schooling of youth cohorts. Using 1985–1999 IMSS firm-level employment data matched to a 10% sample of the 2000 Mexican census, the author analyzes non-migrant cohorts (focus on age-16 exposure) across 1,808 commuting zones, exploiting the legal factory-work age (16), compulsory schooling norms (grade 9), and formal-sector IMSS coverage to identify cohort schooling responses to local export job shocks.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Policy Intervention in Debt Renegotiation: Evidence from the Home Affordable Modification Program
author: Sumit Agarwal, Gene Amromin, Itzhak Ben-David, Souphala Chomsisengphet, Tomasz Piskorski, Amit Seru
context: For this triplet the analysis is at the regional (zip-code) level over July 2008–June 2012, exploiting variation in exposure to servicers’ HAMP implementation intensity (the share of local loans serviced by intermediaries with high pre-program renegotiation activity). Using OCC Mortgage Metrics merged with zip-level Equifax consumer-credit data and CoreLogic house prices, the paper tests whether stronger local HAMP implementation reduced non-targeted consumer debt delinquencies, foreclosures, and durable spending.
identification_methods: DiD; RDD; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Policy Intervention in Debt Renegotiation: Evidence from the Home Affordable Modification Program
author: Sumit Agarwal, Gene Amromin, Itzhak Ben-David, Souphala Chomsisengphet, Tomasz Piskorski, Amit Seru
context: This paper evaluates the US Home Affordable Modification Program (HAMP) introduced in March 2009, using loan-level monthly panel data (OCC Mortgage Metrics and a Treasury application sample) from July 2008–June 2012 covering ≈23–34 million mortgages serviced by large banks. Units are individual first-lien mortgages and servicers/zip regions. HAMP's institutional rules (owner-occupancy, $729,750 balance cap, NPV test, trial-to-permanent modifications) and servicer incentives ($1,000 upfront plus pay-for-success) provide quasi-experimental variation to estimate effects on modifications, foreclosures, delinquencies, house prices, and spending.
identification_methods: DiD; RDD; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

The unit is modified first‑lien mortgages (observations confined to loans that were modified); the outcome is an indicator for redefault within six months of renegotiation. The authors estimate a difference‑in‑differences panel specification analogous to the main regressions.
identification_methods: DiD; RDD; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Wages and Informality in Developing Countries
author: Costas Meghir, Renata Narita, Jean-Marc Robin
context: This paper develops and estimates a structural equilibrium search and wage-posting model of formal and informal labor in Brazil, using Pesquisa Mensal de Emprego (PME) rotating-panel data from six large metropolitan regions (2002–2007). Units are low-educated individuals (age 23–65) and heterogeneous firms deciding sector and posted wages. The institutional setting includes minimum wage, payroll and corporate taxes, employer social security contributions, severance pay, unemployment insurance (funded by labor taxes), and monitoring/fines for informality; counterfactuals simulate policy changes to informality costs and labor regulations.

title: Germs, Social Networks and Growth
author: Alessandra Fogli, Laura Veldkamp
context: This triplet is derived from the paper’s theoretical network-diffusion model and simulations: agents are nodes on a ring (ring-lattice/small-world). The paper shows analytically and by illustration (Result 3 and Figure 2) that purely collectivist networks (high clustering/mutual ties) have longer average path lengths and thus slower technology diffusion than individualist networks. The unit of analysis for this result is simulated network nodes, not country-level empirical data.

title: Early and Late Human Capital Investments, Borrowing Constraints, and the Family
author: Elizabeth M. Caucutt, Lance Lochner
treatment: Increase public spending on early investments
outcome: Early investment by low-education parents' children
context: Using U.S. data (Children of the NLSY79, NLSY79 cohort and CPS benchmarks, samples through ~2010 and 2006), the paper studies children and households to assess how parental resources and credit constraints shape early (ages 0–11) versus late (ages 12–23) human capital investments and later earnings. The institutional setting emphasizes U.S. schooling (public K–12, subsidized postsecondary education and student loans), young parents’ labor income profiles, and imperfect credit markets. The authors combine reduced-form regressions with a calibrated dynastic lifecycle model to evaluate policy interventions (subsidies, loans, transfers).

title: Wrongful Discharge Laws and Innovation
author: Viral V. Acharya, Ramin P. Baghai, Krishnamurthy V. Subramanian
context: This study analyzes the effect of state-level wrongful discharge laws (public-policy, implied-contract, and especially the good-faith exception) on firm innovation and entrepreneurship in the U.S. using firm-year data from 1971–1999. title: Long Run Impacts of Childhood Access to the Safety Net
author: Hilary W. Hoynes, Diane Whitmore Schanzenbach, Douglas Almond
treatment: Food Stamp Program exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-run effects of childhood exposure to the U.S. Food Stamp Program, exploiting county-by-county rollout from 1961–1975. Using individuals born 1956–1981 observed in the PSID, it links county-of-birth month/year to adult health and economic outcomes measured through 2009, focusing on disadvantaged families (parent < high school). The institutional setting is the federal Food Stamp/SNAP expansion, with treatment defined as the share of months exposed between conception and age five.

author: Hilary W. Hoynes, Diane Whitmore Schanzenbach, Douglas Almond
treatment: Food Stamp Program exposure in early childhood
context: This estimate uses the full PSID sample of individuals born 1956–1981 and exploits the county-by-county rollout of the U.S. Food Stamp Program (1961–1975). Identification for this triplet comes from a triple-difference specification that interacts FSP exposure with group-level participation rates (yielding treatment-on-the-treated); adult self-reported health is measured in PSID interviews through 2009.

title: Vulnerable Banks
author: Robin Greenwood, Augustin Landier, David Thesmar
context: This paper develops and calibrates a fire‑sale contagion model and applies it to the European banking sector during the 2010–2011 sovereign‑debt crisis. Using harmonized balance‑sheet exposures from the European Banking Authority’s July 2011 stress tests for 90 large EU banks and equity return data (Dec 2009–Sep 2011), the authors quantify bank‑level vulnerability, systemicness, and spillovers from GIIPS sovereign write‑downs under leverage‑targeting and linear price‑impact assumptions, and simulate policy counterfactuals (capital injections, mergers, size caps, re‑nationalization, Eurobonds).

title: Active vs. Passive Decisions and Crowdout in Retirement Savings Accounts: Evidence from Denmark
author: Raj Chetty, John N. Friedman, Soren Leth-Petersen, Torben Nielsen, Tore Olsen
context: This paper uses administrative registers for the population of Denmark (≈4 million individuals, 1995–2009, ages 18–60) to study how retirement saving responds to policy changes. It exploits variation in employer-defined-contribution pension rates from job switches, a 1998 Mandatory Savings Plan that imposed a 1% income-tied contribution with an income cutoff, and a 1999 top‑bracket tax deduction reduction for capital pensions. The institutional setting includes tax‑favored capital and annuity pension accounts, collective‑bargained employer contributions, and third‑party reported flows into retirement and taxable accounts.
identification_methods: event studies; RDD; DiD; IV; other panel regressions

title: Analyzing the Effects of Insuring Health Risks: On the Trade-off between Short Run Insurance Benefits vs. Long Run Incentive Costs
author: Harold L. Cole, Soojin Kim, Dirk Krueger
context: This paper studies U.S. working‑age households (panel data from the PSID, calibrated to circa 1999) to evaluate policies that ban employer wage discrimination by health status (ADA/ADAAA) and prohibit insurer pricing on prior conditions (HIPAA/PPACA, with 2014 implementation). Using a life‑cycle, dynamic structural model of health, medical spending, and labor earnings for cohorts aged roughly 24–65, the authors estimate health transition and expenditure processes and simulate short‑run insurance gains versus long‑run incentive costs of these regulatory reforms.

title: Market-Based Emissions Regulation and Industry Dynamics
author: Meredith Fowlie, Mar Reguant, Stephen P. Ryan
context: Using kiln- and plant-level data for the U.S. Portland cement industry over 1980–2006, the paper estimates a structural dynamic oligopoly model of regional cement markets (firms/kilns/markets) to simulate effects of market-based carbon regulation. It evaluates auctioning (carbon tax), grandfathering, output-based permit rebating, and border tax adjustments, accounting for firm entry, exit, investment, production responses, trade exposure, emissions leakage, and pre-existing market power to quantify dynamic welfare impacts of alternative permit-allocation designs.

title: Modest, Secure and Informed: Successful Development in Conflict Zones
author: Eli Berman, Joseph Felter, Jacob N. Shapiro, Erin Troland
context: Using panel data from Feb 2004–Dec 2008/Feb 2009 on Iraq’s reconstruction, this paper studies effects of U.S. development spending across districts (N≈103) observed in half‑year intervals. It links IRMS project‑level spending (CERP, USAID CAP/CSP, large vs. small projects), troop strength (maneuver battalions), and Provincial Reconstruction Team presence to SIGACT insurgent‑incident rates, exploiting variation in timing, project size, local security, and implementation modality to evaluate when aid reduced violence during the Iraq war and U.S. counterinsurgency operations.

title: Equilibrium Labor Market Search and Health Insurance Reform
author: Naoki Aizawa, Hanming Fang
context: This study models U.S. labor and health‑insurance markets using worker and establishment data (SIPP 1996, MEPS 1997–99, RWJ‑EHI 1997) to estimate an equilibrium search model of heterogeneous firms and risk‑averse workers facing medical shocks. Units are non‑elderly workers and private employers; key institutional features include employer‑sponsored insurance, HIPAA rules, and 2010 Affordable Care Act provisions (individual mandate, employer mandate, exchanges, subsidies, with major changes phased in by 2014). The paper uses structural estimation to evaluate the ACA and counterfactual reforms.

title: Are Government Spending Multipliers Greater During Periods of Slack?  Evidence from 20th Century Historical Data
author: Michael T. Owyang, Valerie A. Ramey, Sarah Zubairy
context: This empirical study uses newly constructed quarterly national data for the U.S. (1890–2010) and Canada (1921–2011) on GDP, the GDP deflator, government spending and unemployment to study fiscal multipliers across major wars and the Great Depression. The unit of observation is country-quarter, and identification exploits a narrative “military news” series (changes in the expected present value of military spending) as exogenous shocks to government purchases. The paper compares responses across slack versus non-slack states defined by unemployment thresholds (6.5% U.S., 7% Canada) using local-projection impulse responses.

title: Long-Term Neighborhood Effects on Low-Income Families: Evidence from Moving to Opportunity
author: Jens Ludwig, Greg J. Duncan, Lisa A. Gennetian, Lawrence F. Katz, Ronald C. Kessler, Jeffrey R. Kling, Lisa Sanbonmatsu
context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

title: Long-Term Neighborhood Effects on Low-Income Families: Evidence from Moving to Opportunity
author: Jens Ludwig, Greg J. Duncan, Lisa A. Gennetian, Lawrence F. Katz, Ronald C. Kessler, Jeffrey R. Kling, Lisa Sanbonmatsu
context: Using the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Moving to Opportunity randomized experiment (1994–98 enrollment, 10–15 year follow-up surveys in 2008–10), the paper studies low-income public-housing families with children in five U.S. cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York). Families were randomly assigned to an Experimental voucher (restricted to low-poverty tracts plus counseling), a regular Section 8 voucher, or control; outcomes for adults and youth are analyzed using survey, administrative, and neighborhood-tract data.

title: Profitability of Fertilizer: Experimental Evidence from Female Rice Farmers in Mali
author: Lori A. Beaman, Dean Karlan, Bram Thuysbaert, Christopher R. Udry
context: In spring 2010 an RCT in Bougouni district, southern Mali randomly assigned 383 women rice farmers (one per household) to receive no, half, or full recommended fertilizer per hectare; follow-ups were conducted in August and December 2010. Rice is rainfed, broadcast-farmed on small, women-controlled plots; fertilizer is officially recommended but under-used despite government subsidies. The study evaluates how exogenous fertilizer grants affect input choices, output, and profits under typical (no intensive monitoring) extension conditions.

title: Growth Forecast Errors and Fiscal Multipliers
author: Olivier J. Blanchard, Daniel Leigh
context: This paper studies whether forecasters underestimated fiscal multipliers by relating two-year real GDP growth forecast errors to planned changes in the structural fiscal balance across countries. The baseline is April 2010 IMF WEO forecasts for 26 European advanced economies, with extensions to all advanced and emerging economies and panel vintages covering 2009–2012 (and 1997–2008 as precrisis). Unit of observation is the national economy over two-year intervals. The setting is the post‑2008 financial crisis, featuring large multiyear consolidation plans, liquidity‑trap/zero‑lower‑bound conditions, and elevated sovereign and banking stress.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; IV

triplet_key: 18799|attending a lower-ranked university|probability of graduating with a science degree
title: University Differences in the Graduation of Minorities in STEM Fields: Evidence from California
author: Peter Arcidiacono, Esteban M. Aucejo, V. Joseph Hotz
outcome: Probability of graduating with a science degree
context: The paper uses individual-level administrative data on applicants, enrollees, and graduates across University of California campuses for freshmen cohorts entering 1995–1997 (when race-conscious admissions were legal) to analyze how student preparation and campus sorting affect STEM persistence and degree completion. The setting is California’s UC system; units are individual students (minorities: African American, Hispanic, Native American; non-minorities: white and Asian). The analysis exploits variation in campus selectivity, SAT/GPA measures, intended major, and admissions/application patterns to adjust for selection and simulate counterfactual reallocations.

title: Firm Size Distortions and the Productivity Distribution: Evidence from France
author: Luis Garicano, Claire LeLarge, John Van Reenen
context: Using administrative French firm-level data (FICUS, with some checks in DADS) for roughly 2002–2007, the paper studies firms (primarily manufacturing, units = firms) across employment sizes to quantify the equilibrium and welfare effects of size-contingent labor regulations that sharply increase costs at the 50-employee threshold. It exploits the break in the firm-size and productivity distributions around 50 employees, estimates a structural Lucas-style model (with production-function panel estimations and counterfactual simulations), and compares flexible versus downwardly rigid wage scenarios.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: How Elastic Are Preferences for Redistribution? Evidence from Randomized Survey Experiments
author: Ilyana Kuziemko, Michael I. Norton, Emmanuel Saez, Stefanie Stantcheva
context: This paper uses randomized online survey experiments conducted in the United States between January 2011 and August 2012 (with one-month follow-ups and additional rounds) to study how customized, interactive information about U.S. income inequality, historical top tax rates and economic growth correlations, and estate-tax incidence affects individual preferences for redistribution. The unit of observation is U.S. adult respondents recruited primarily via Amazon Mechanical Turk (≈10,000 participants across rounds, with some supplemental panel recruitment). The institutional background is contemporary U.S. tax and transfer policy amid rising top income/wealth shares.

title: Sales Force and Competition in Financial Product Markets: The Case Of Mexico’s Social Security Privatization
author: Justine S. Hastings, Ali Hortaçsu, Chad Syverson
context: The paper examines Mexico’s 1997 privatization of the social security pension system, using administrative data on all individual account-holders, contributions, opening balances, fund-manager fees, and registered sales agents from 1997–2007. Units are individual workers/accounts (linked to municipalities) and 17 private fund managers (Afores). The institutional setting features nationally set flow and balance fees, tightly regulated portfolios under CONSAR, extensive sales-agent recruitment at launch, difficult switching, and centralized processing—enabling analysis of how local sales forces affected demand, price sensitivity, and equilibrium fees.

triplet_key: 18885|preferential tax scheme for high-earning foreigners|pre-tax earnings
title: Migration and Wage Effects of Taxing Top Earners: Evidence from the Foreigners' Tax Scheme in Denmark
author: Henrik Jacobsen Kleven, Camille Landais, Emmanuel Saez, Esben Anton Schultz
treatment: Preferential tax scheme for high-earning foreigners
context: Using population-wide matched employer–employee administrative data for Denmark (1980–2006), the paper studies individual-level migration, duration, and wage responses to the Danish foreigners’ tax scheme introduced in 1991 (applied retroactively mid-1991). The scheme granted a preferential flat tax (~30%) for up to 3 years to recruited high‑earning non-residents who met an annual earnings threshold (≈99th percentile) and a 3‑year non‑residency rule. Identification exploits entry timing, the earnings eligibility notch, and the 3‑year duration kink across foreign and native workers.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Can Self-Help Groups Really Be Self-Help"?"
author: Brian Greaney, Joseph P. Kaboski, Eva Van Leemput
context: This paper evaluates, via a randomized field experiment conducted c.2009–2011, a delivery innovation to Catholic Relief Services' SILC self-help group microfinance program in Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda. The intervention randomized trained local agents (≈276) across multiple districts to remain NGO‑paid Field Agents (FAs) or become privatized Private Service Providers (PSPs) who charge membership fees. Data include eight quarters of group accounting for >5,700 SILC groups, semiannual agent surveys, and household panel surveys in 192 villages (~1,910 households), measuring membership, savings, credit, business activity, and consumption.
identification_methods: RCT; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; event studies

title: Innovation, Reallocation and Growth
author: Daron Acemoglu, Ufuk Akcigit, Harun Alp, Nicholas Bloom, William R. Kerr
context: This paper combines a structural quality‑ladder model with firm‑level estimation using US microdata (1987–1997). It studies continuously‑innovative US manufacturing firms and product lines, using Census LBD and CMF, NSF RAD R&D surveys and NBER patent data, focusing on skilled‑labor allocation, firm entry/exit, R&D intensities and selection between high/low innovative types. The setting considers industrial policies (R&D subsidies, taxes/subsidies on incumbent operations) and evaluates their counterfactual impacts on reallocation, innovation and welfare.

title: Female Labour Supply, Human Capital and Welfare Reform
author: Richard Blundell, Monica Costa Dias, Costas Meghir, Jonathan M. Shaw
context: This triplet refers to structural life-cycle model simulations for UK women (aged ≈19–50) using BHPS 1991–2008 and a tax–benefit microsimulation, evaluating cohort-specific exposure to major UK reforms (notably the Working Families’ Tax Credit reforms c.1999–2002). Results are conditional on the observed family-formation processes and the institutional details (hours-based eligibility 16/30h, childcare and housing benefits) and contrast lone versus partnered mothers.
identification_methods: DiD; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

title: Female Labour Supply, Human Capital and Welfare Reform
author: Richard Blundell, Monica Costa Dias, Costas Meghir, Jonathan M. Shaw
context: This study analyzes UK women’s education, labor supply, human-capital accumulation and savings using 1991–2008 data (BHPS panel and Labour Force Survey), exploiting cohort variation and major UK tax and welfare reforms—notably the Working Families’ Tax Credit and Income Support changes implemented c.1999–2004 (large reforms 1999–2002). The unit is individuals/households (women aged ≈19–50, with focus on lone mothers vs partnered mothers), set in the detailed UK tax-credit, childcare, housing-benefit and income-tax institutional environment with eligibility hours (16/30h) and cohort-specific policy exposure.
identification_methods: DiD; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

title: Vehicle Scrappage and Gasoline Policy
author: Mark R. Jacobsen, Arthur A. van Benthem
context: This paper analyzes U.S. used-vehicle registrations (Polk VIN-prefix counts) and NADA used-vehicle valuations over roughly 1999–2009 (with vintages up to 19 years), at the make-model-trim-age-year level. It exploits differential gasoline-price shocks across vehicle MPG to generate demand-driven used-price variation, estimates how changes in used-car values affect owners’ scrappage decisions, and applies those elasticities in a calibrated U.S. vehicle-fleet simulation to quantify leakage from tighter CAFE fuel-economy standards (41.7 MPG target in 2020).

title: Juvenile Incarceration, Human Capital and Future Crime: Evidence from Randomly-Assigned Judges
author: Anna Aizer, Joseph J. Doyle, Jr.
context: Using linked administrative records (Chicago Public Schools 1990–2006, Cook County Juvenile Court 1991–2006, Illinois Dept. of Corrections 1993–2008), the paper studies over 35,000 juveniles (age 10–16 at first offense, mostly birth cohorts 1974–1982) in Chicago, IL. It exploits quasi-random assignment of cases to judges across neighborhood calendars and a weapons calendar to instrument juvenile incarceration, estimating effects on high-school completion and adult incarceration by age 25. Institutional context: juvenile detention, probation alternatives, and judge sentencing variation.

title: Juvenile Incarceration, Human Capital and Future Crime: Evidence from Randomly-Assigned Judges
author: Anna Aizer, Joseph J. Doyle, Jr.
context: Using linked administrative records from Chicago Public Schools (1990–2006), Cook County Juvenile Court (1991–2006) and the Illinois Department of Corrections (1993–2008), the paper studies over 35,000 juveniles (age 10–16 at first offense, birth cohorts roughly 1974–1982) in Chicago. It exploits quasi-random assignment of cases to judges across neighborhood calendars (and a weapons calendar) to instrument for juvenile incarceration, estimating causal effects on high‑school completion, juvenile recidivism, and adult incarceration by age 25 within the Illinois juvenile‑justice institutional setting.

title: Adverse Selection and an Individual Mandate: When Theory Meets Practice
author: Martin B. Hackmann, Jonathan T. Kolstad, Amanda E. Kowalski
context: This paper studies the 2006 Massachusetts health reform — notably the individual mandate, Commonwealth Choice exchange, and existing community-rating and guaranteed-issue rules — and its effect on the individual health insurance market. Using insurer- and state-level data from 2004–2011 (pre:2004–05; implementation:2006–07; post:2008–11), the authors compare Massachusetts to other U.S. states to estimate impacts on coverage, premiums, and average claims for non-elderly adults (18–64) in the non-poor individual market (>$300% FPL), combining SNL insurer-year data and NHIS coverage rates.

title: Marginal Tax Rates and Income: New Time Series Evidence
author: Karel Mertens, José L. Montiel Olea
context: Using U.S. postwar annual data from 1946–2012, the paper studies tax-unit–level reported market incomes across the income distribution (top 1%, top 5%, bottom 90%, etc.) and aggregate macro outcomes. It constructs average marginal tax rate series (income and payroll taxes) and narrative scores of exogenous federal tax reforms (Romer–Romer style) to isolate surprise marginal-rate innovations, and estimates dynamic effects of these shocks on reported incomes, GDP, unemployment, and income sources.

title: Public Health Insurance, Labor Supply, and Employment Lock
author: Craig Garthwaite, Tal Gross, Matthew J. Notowidigdo
context: This paper studies the causal effect of Tennessee’s 2005 TennCare disenrollment on labor supply using individual- and state-level data from roughly 2000–2007 (with robustness checks into later years). The analysis focuses on non-elderly adults (ages 21–64), especially childless adults, comparing Tennessee to other Southern states with CPS March ASEC data, BRFSS, monthly LAUS employment series, and Google Trends. The policy abruptly removed expansion-category public Medicaid coverage (≈170,000 adults) without income-based eligibility, allowing estimation of how loss of public insurance affected employment, hours, and private employer coverage.

triplet_key: 19226|exposure to import competition from china|earnings per year of employment
title: Trade Adjustment: Worker Level Evidence
author: David H. Autor, David Dorn, Gordon H. Hanson, Jae Song
treatment: Exposure to import competition from China
context: This empirical study evaluates how rising import competition from China during 1991–2007 affected U.S. workers. Using a 1% longitudinal Social Security sample of individual earnings and employer SIC codes, the authors analyze manufacturing workers’ earnings, employment, job-to-job flows, and SSDI receipt across the United States as China’s exports (and U.S. import penetration) surged—notably after WTO accession in 2001. The setting exploits industry-level variation in exposure to Chinese import growth and instruments supply-driven Chinese export gains using import growth in other high-income countries.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Family Welfare Cultures
author: Gordon B. Dahl, Andreas Ravndal Kostol, Magne Mogstad
context: This paper studies Norway's Disability Insurance (DI) system using linked administrative registers (1967–2010) and appeals data (1989–2005) for parent–child pairs. It focuses on parents (≤55) who appealed an initial DI denial and their adult children (18+), exploiting randomized assignment of Administrative Law Judges in Oslo to the written appeal process. The institutional setting features centralized, non-specialized judges whose leniency variation exogenously shifts parental DI awards, enabling causal estimates of parental DI receipt on children’s DI application, receipt, labor, and education outcomes.

title: Unemployment and Business Cycles
author: Lawrence J. Christiano, Martin S. Eichenbaum, Mathias Trabandt
context: Using U.S. quarterly post‑war macro data (approximately 1951Q1–2008Q4), the paper studies aggregate responses of households and firms (GDP, hours, unemployment, vacancies, wages, inflation, investment, etc.). It embeds search-and-matching labor market frictions and alternating-offer or Nash wage bargaining into a New Keynesian DSGE, and estimates parameters by matching model impulse responses to VAR-identified shocks (monetary policy, neutral TFP, investment‑specific TFP). The analysis includes policy experiments on unemployment benefits and scenarios with the zero lower bound.

context: Analyzes the long-run causal effects of the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) using a county-level panel from ~1900–2000, emphasizing heavy federal investment 1934–1958 and the post-1959 self-financing period. title: The Twilight Zone: OTC Regulatory Regimes and Market Quality
author: Ulf Brüggemann, Aditya Kaul, Christian Leuz, Ingrid M. Werner
context: This paper studies U.S. over-the-counter (OTC) equity markets from January 2001 through October 2010 using a panel of ≈10,583 domestic firms (firm-months ≈955,716). It exploits variation across OTC trading venues (NASDAQ Bulletin Board, OTC Pink tiers, Grey Market), firms’ SEC filing status, inclusion in securities manuals, and state “blue sky” merit-review regimes — including several Pink Sheets regulatory changes — to estimate effects on market quality (liquidity and crash risk). Identification leverages within-firm venue and reporting-status transitions, regulatory reforms, and cross-state institutional differences.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; PSM; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 19441|attending a private school|average test scores across subjects
title: The Aggregate Effect of School Choice: Evidence from a Two-stage Experiment in India
author: Karthik Muralidharan, Venkatesh Sundararaman
context: This paper evaluates a two-stage randomized school voucher experiment implemented in Andhra Pradesh, India, with baseline tests in March–April 2008 and follow-up assessments roughly 2.3 and 4 years later. The study covers primary-school students (and villages/markets) across 180 rural villages in five districts. Villages were randomized to treatment/control and eligible applicants within treatment villages were randomized to receive vouchers that covered private school fees and materials; private schools participated voluntarily and were not allowed to select students. The analysis estimates individual impacts, market-level spillovers, and cost-effectiveness.

title: Private Equity, Jobs, and Productivity

title: Advance Refundings of Municipal Bonds
author: Andrew Ang, Richard C. Green, Yuhang Xing
context: This paper analyzes US municipal bond advance-refundings over 1996–2009 (MSRB/Bloomberg trades), covering 148,961 CUSIPs (~$454 billion par, ~51% of traded pre-refunded bonds). Unit of observation: CUSIP-level bonds and issuer "deals" across the 50 U.S. states. Using institutional details of municipal call provisions, SLGS escrow rules, and balanced-budget restrictions, the authors model option values (Vasicek/Hull-White), quantify option value and fees lost to issuers, and relate cross-sectional variation (e.g., state governance) to losses.

title: Relationship and Transaction Lending in a Crisis
author: Patrick Bolton, Xavier Freixas, Leonardo Gambacorta, Paolo Emilio Mistrulli
context: This paper analyzes bank–firm lending in Italy around the 2008 financial crisis (2007–2010), using Bank of Italy credit-registry data on about 72,000 firms and 179 banks at the bank–firm loan level. It treats the Lehman Brothers collapse (Sept 2008) as an exogenous aggregate shock, comparing pre-crisis (2007:q2) and post-crisis (2010:q1) lending, interest rates, and defaults while classifying relationship vs. transaction banks by geographic proximity and controlling with firm and bank fixed effects.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Relationship and Transaction Lending in a Crisis
author: Patrick Bolton, Xavier Freixas, Leonardo Gambacorta, Paolo Emilio Mistrulli
context: This triplet is estimated using Italian Bank of Italy credit-registry data at the firm–bank loan level. The treatment is each firm’s share of transactional loans measured in 2008:q2 (pre-Lehman), and the outcome is the firm default probability observed over the six quarters following Lehman’s collapse (2008:q3–2010:q1). Units are firms (the sample covers many tens of thousands). Relationship vs. transaction banks are classified by geographic proximity. The main specification is a marginal probit (cross-sectional) with bank and industry fixed effects; robustness adds firm controls.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 19473|higher public basic research investment|private applied research investment
title: Back to Basics: Basic Research Spillovers, Innovation Policy and Growth
author: Ufuk Akcigit, Douglas Hanley, Nicolas Serrano-Velarde
context: This paper combines a theoretical multi-industry general-equilibrium model with structural estimation using French firm-level R&D data (basic vs. applied) for 2000–2006 (13,708 firm-year observations), matched balance-sheet and multi-industry ownership data (EAE, LIFI), and U.S. patent citation data (1974–2006). Units are firms (product lines, industries); the institutional setting is France’s R&D policy environment (public basic-research funding ≈0.5% of GDP and existing corporate R&D subsidies ≈10%). The analysis targets spillovers, technology diffusion, and innovation policy counterfactuals.

title: Back to Basics: Basic Research Spillovers, Innovation Policy and Growth
author: Ufuk Akcigit, Douglas Hanley, Nicolas Serrano-Velarde
context: This triplet refers to a counterfactual, model-based evaluation within the paper’s estimated multi-industry general-equilibrium framework calibrated to French firm-level R&D data (13,708 firm-year observations, 2000–2006). The policy is a hypothetical type-dependent subsidy applied to private firms’ research (separate rates for basic vs. applied research), and welfare effects are reported in consumption-equivalent terms for the French economy; the model’s optimal rates are a 50% subsidy for basic research and 14% for applied research.

triplet_key: 19484|card act fee regulations|interest charges for new accounts
title: Regulating Consumer Financial Products: Evidence from Credit Cards
author: Sumit Agarwal, Souphala Chomsisengphet, Neale Mahoney, Johannes Stroebel
context: This paper evaluates causal effects of the 2009 U.S. Credit Card Accountability Responsibility and Disclosure (CARD) Act — signed May 2009 with key provisions effective Feb 22, 2010 and Aug 22, 2010 — using monthly account-level data from March 2008–December 2011 covering ~160 million consumer and small-business credit card accounts issued by eight large U.S. banks. Treatment is consumer cards (subject to CARD rules) versus small-business cards (exempt); outcomes include fees, interest charges, repayment behavior, and credit volume, with heterogeneity by origination FICO score.

title: Regulating Consumer Financial Products: Evidence from Credit Cards
author: Sumit Agarwal, Souphala Chomsisengphet, Neale Mahoney, Johannes Stroebel
context: This estimate measures the effect of the CARD Act fee regulations on total fees for accounts with origination FICO < 660, using monthly account-level data (March 2008–December 2011) on consumer and small-business credit cards from eight large U.S. banks. Identification compares consumer cards (subject to CARD fee limits) to small-business cards (exempt) over the implementation phases (Feb 22, 2010 and Aug 22, 2010); the outcome is total fees expressed as an annualized percent of average daily balance.

title: Regulating Consumer Financial Products: Evidence from Credit Cards
author: Sumit Agarwal, Souphala Chomsisengphet, Neale Mahoney, Johannes Stroebel
context: This analysis evaluates the causal effect of the 2009 U.S. CARD Act (key provisions effective Feb 22, 2010 and Aug 22, 2010) on interest charges using monthly account-level data (March 2008–December 2011) from ~160 million consumer and small-business credit card accounts at eight large U.S. banks. Treatment compares consumer cards (subject to CARD rules) to small-business cards (exempt); the baseline interest-charge analysis focuses on accounts with origination FICO score below 660 and examines anticipatory and phased responses.

title: In the Shadow of a Giant: Medicare's Influence on Private Physician Payments
author: Jeffrey Clemens, Joshua D. Gottlieb
context: This empirical paper uses US Medicare and private insurance claims from 1995–2002 to estimate how administrative Medicare payment reforms affect private physician reimbursements. It exploits two exogenous shocks: the 1998 merger of surgical and non‑surgical conversion factors (affecting 2,194 HCPCS services) and the 1997 consolidation of geographic payment localities (210→89 regions). Analysis is at the service-by-state and service-by-payment‑area levels, linking a 5% Medicare Part B sample to MarketScan private claims.

title: Predatory Lending and the Subprime Crisis
author: Sumit Agarwal, Gene Amromin, Itzhak Ben-David, Souphala Chomsisengphet, Douglas D. Evanoff
context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of Illinois’ HB4050 anti‑predatory mortgage counseling pilot (implemented Sep 2006–Jan 2007) on mortgage origination and 18‑month default outcomes using loan‑level HMDA and CoreLogic LoanPerformance data covering 2005–2007. The setting is ten predominantly minority South Side Chicago zip codes (treated) versus comparable Chicago zip codes; units are loans/borrowers, lenders, and zip codes. HB4050 required HUD‑certified counseling for low‑FICO or risky‑product applicants from state‑licensed lenders and imposed penalties, producing a localized exogenous shock to lending practices.
identification_methods: DiD; synthetic control; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Recall and Unemployment
author: Shigeru Fujita, Giuseppe Moscarini
context: Using US microdata (SIPP panels spanning 1990–2008 with outcomes observed through ~2011) and CPS flow series, the paper studies individual workers’ separations, unemployment spells, and recalls across the United States. The unit of observation is the worker and employer–worker match; key outcomes are recall rates, unemployment duration, occupation switching, and wage changes. The setting includes the institutional measurement of Temporary Layoffs (TL) and Permanent Separations (PS), aggregate labor-market series (JOLTS/Help-Wanted), and the Great Recession. The paper also calibrates a search-and-matching model with a recall option.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; other time-series regressions; others

title: Recall and Unemployment
author: Shigeru Fujita, Giuseppe Moscarini

The analysis compares unadjusted hires to measures that exclude recalls (and exclude TL from the unemployment denominator), computing standard deviations of the adjusted vs. unadjusted series to assess how recalls affect volatility of the job-finding probability.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; other time-series regressions; others

title: Efficiency and Foreclosure Effects of Vertical Rebates: Empirical Evidence
author: Christopher T. Conlon, Julie Holland Mortimer
context: Using detailed sales and contract data from a single U.S. vending operator (Mark Vend Company) and Mars’ All-Units Discount (AUD) terms, the paper studies vending-machine product assortments, retailer restocking effort, and manufacturer rebates. The panel spans Jan 2006–Feb 2009 with a field experiment (product removals) run June 2007–Sept 2008 across 66 office-building machines (Chicago-area/high-income offices). The setting exploits exogenous product removals plus structural demand and dynamic restocking models to trace efficiency and foreclosure effects of Mars’ rebate program.

triplet_key: 19796|adjacency to shale gas well pads|piped-water-dependent property values
title: The Housing Market Impacts of Shale Gas Development
author: Lucija Muehlenbachs, Elisheba Spiller, Christopher Timmins
treatment: Adjacency to shale gas well pads
context: Using property transaction data from January 1995–April 2012, this paper studies how shale gas development (Marcellus formation) in Pennsylvania — with bordering New York counties under a fracking moratorium — affected housing prices. The unit of observation is individual property transactions (repeat-sale and hedonic samples). Analyses contrast homes on private groundwater versus public piped-water service areas to isolate perceived groundwater contamination risk, adjacency disamenities, and broader vicinity (boom-town) effects, leveraging variation in well permits, drilled well pads, production, and visibility.

title: The Housing Market Impacts of Shale Gas Development
author: Lucija Muehlenbachs, Elisheba Spiller, Christopher Timmins
context: Using individual property transaction data from January 1995–April 2012 for Pennsylvania (Marcellus shale) and bordering New York counties, this triplet concerns the broader “vicinity” effect of newly drilled wellbores — measured by counts of wellbores/well pads within radii (up to about 20 km) — on housing prices. The unit of observation is individual property sales (repeat-sale and hedonic samples); estimates track changes in prices as new wellbores are drilled nearby while accounting for property fixed effects and county×year shocks to isolate local economic (boom-town) capitalization.

title: Every Breath You Take - Every Dollar You'll Make: The Long-Term Consequences of the Clean Air Act of 1970
author: Adam Isen, Maya Rossin-Slater, W. Reed Walker
outcome: Fraction at bottom of earnings distribution
context: This empirical study exploits the 1970 Clean Air Act Amendments in the United States, using LEHD administrative data on ~5.7 million individuals born in 1969–1974 in 148 counties (24 states). It compares cohorts born before versus after nonattainment-driven reductions in county-level total suspended particulates (TSP) and measures labor-market outcomes around ages 29–31 (observed 1998–2007), combining county TSP monitors, natality records, county fixed effects, and state×year controls to isolate early-life pollution exposure effects.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; event studies; RDD; other panel regressions

title: Central Clearing and Collateral Demand
author: Darrell Duffie, Martin Scheicher, Guillaume Vuillemey
context: Using the DTCC Trade Information Warehouse snapshot of single-name CDS as of December 30, 2011 (184 reference entities, 885 anonymized counterparties and 14 dealers), the paper simulates counterfactual clearing and margin arrangements and finds that client clearing reduces system-wide collateral demand only conditional on dealers being able to re-use a sufficiently large share of collateral received from clients (rehypothecation) and when investors’ portfolios are large enough to realize netting and diversification benefits.

triplet_key: 19890|increase in number of clearing members|total collateral demand
title: Central Clearing and Collateral Demand
author: Darrell Duffie, Martin Scheicher, Guillaume Vuillemey
treatment: Increase in number of clearing members
context: This paper empirically analyzes how expanded margin requirements and central clearing affect collateral demand in the single-name CDS market using a global DTCC Trade Information Warehouse snapshot as of December 30, 2011. The unit of observation is bilateral CDS exposures across 184 reference entities (primarily European and American financial and sovereign names) and 885 anonymized counterparties (distinguishing 14 dealers and customers). It evaluates counterfactual clearing and margining arrangements—including dealer-to-dealer initial margins, CCP proliferation, and client clearing—under post‑Lehman G20 regulatory reforms.

title: In with the Big, Out with the Small: Removing Small-Scale Reservations in India
author: Leslie A. Martin, Shanthi Nataraj, Ann Harrison
context: This paper studies India’s dismantling of small-scale industry (SSI) product reservations (major de-reservation 1997–2008) and its effects on manufacturing establishments and districts using the Annual Survey of Industries panel (primarily 2000–2007) and NSS unorganized-manufacturing rounds (2000, 2005). Units are establishments (product-level incumbents and entrants), products, and districts. The institutional setting is the national SSI reservation regime (eligibility tied to a plant-and-machinery cap around Rs.10 million) that restricted certain product lines to small firms; de-reservation varied by product timing, generating differential local exposure.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: The Sad Truth About Happiness Scales
author: Timothy N. Bond, Kevin Lang
context: This paper combines theoretical analysis with empirical re-examination of happiness survey evidence from: the Moving-to-Opportunity randomized housing experiment (long-term follow-up of low-income U.S. public-housing families); U.S. General Social Survey repeated cross-sections (1972–2006) comparing male and female subjective well-being; national time-series of U.S. per-capita real disposable income for the Easterlin paradox; and cross-country World Values Survey (circa 2005). Units are individuals/households reporting 3-point happiness categories; key institutional settings include voucher eligibility, neighborhood poverty, income growth and changing inequality.
identification_methods: RCT; other cross-sectional regressions; other time-series regressions

title: One Fundamental and Two Taxes: When Does a Tobin Tax Reduce Financial Price Volatility?
author: Yongheng Deng, Xin Liu, Shang-Jin Wei
context: This paper analyzes the causal effect of changes in China’s stock trading stamp duty on price volatility and trading by exploiting 53 firms cross-listed as A‑shares (Mainland) and H‑shares (Hong Kong) around seven discrete stamp‑duty events from 1996–2008. Using daily firm‑level A/H pairs (240‑day windows before/after events), it leverages binding Chinese capital controls that segment markets (preventing arbitrage), different timing of transaction‑cost changes across markets, and variation in institutional ownership over time (1990s retail‑dominated to post‑2006 institutional growth).
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other panel regressions

title: Why Do Banks Practice Regulatory Arbitrage? Evidence from Usage of Trust Preferred Securities
author: Nicole Boyson, Rüdiger Fahlenbrach, René M. Stulz
context: This analysis uses an unbalanced panel of U.S. publicly traded bank holding companies (FR Y-9C filers). The z-score regressions cover 1996–2011 at the BHC-year level and regress distance-to-default (z-score, computed as (ROA + market-capitalized equity/asset ratio) divided by the six-year quarterly SD of ROA) on TPS / Tier 1 and standard bank controls.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Why Do Banks Practice Regulatory Arbitrage? Evidence from Usage of Trust Preferred Securities
author: Nicole Boyson, Rüdiger Fahlenbrach, René M. Stulz
context: This paper analyzes U.S. publicly traded bank holding companies (FR Y-9C filers, sample 1996–2012, ~857 BHCs) to study use of trust preferred securities (TPS) as regulatory arbitrage. The institutional setting includes the Fed’s 1996 allowance of TPS in Tier‑1, FASB deconsolidation (2003) and FRB guidance (2004), and the Dodd‑Frank Collins amendment and TPS phase‑out (2010–2013). Analyses cover issuance timing, TPS share of Tier‑1, concurrent ABCP conduit use, and impacts during the 2007–09 crisis (TARP take‑up, stock and operating performance).
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Democracy Does Cause Growth
author: Daron Acemoglu, Suresh Naidu, Pascual Restrepo, James A. Robinson
context: This paper uses an annual panel of up to 175 countries from 1960–2010 to estimate the causal impact of transitions between nondemocracy and democracy on log GDP per capita at the country-year level. It constructs a consolidated dichotomous democracy indicator from Freedom House, Polity IV and other sources, controls for country and year fixed effects and rich GDP dynamics (lagged GDP), and exploits regional waves of democratization, instrumental variables, GMM panel estimators, and propensity-score–based semi-parametric matching to address endogeneity and measurement error.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; PSM; IV; event studies

title: Can Taxes Shape an Industry? Evidence from the Implementation of the “Amazon Tax”
author: Brian Baugh, Itzhak Ben-David, Hoonsuk Park
context: This analysis examines whether U.S. states that implemented laws requiring Amazon to collect sales tax (19 treated states with staggered adoptions in 2012–2015) experienced different GDP growth. The unit of observation is state-quarter: the authors collect five-year GDP growth data in a window around each state’s implementation year and regress state-level GDP growth on indicators for treated states after implementation, including state and time fixed effects and weights, comparing treated states to control states that did not change Amazon tax status.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: The Long Term Impact of Cash Transfers to Poor Families
author: Anna Aizer, Shari Eli, Joseph P. Ferrie, Adriana Lleras-Muney
context: This triplet concerns male children born 1900–1925 whose mothers applied (before 1930) to state Mothers’ Pension programs (1911–1935) across 11 U.S. states. Administrative application records (accepted vs. rejected) are linked to the 1940 U.S. Census — when these men were roughly ages 20–45 — to measure adult income, with additional linkage to WWII enlistment and death records. The analysis focuses on income outcomes for boys raised in female-headed poor households receiving modest monthly pensions.

triplet_key: 20109|divestiture (end of cost-of-service regulation)|price paid for coal
title: When Does Regulation Distort Costs? Lessons from Fuel Procurement in U.S. Electricity Generation
author: Steve Cicala
context: This paper studies U.S. coal- and gas-fired electricity generating plants in the lower 48 states over 1990–2009, examining plant- and unit-level responses to state-level electricity restructuring that ended cost-of-service regulation (divestiture) in the late 1990s–early 2000s. Using restricted EIA/FERC/MSHA data, it compares divested versus nearby non-divested IOU, municipal, and cooperative plants to assess changes in fuel procurement prices, coal sourcing (mine characteristics and in-/out-of-state sourcing), and sulfur-abatement choices under different regulatory regimes.

triplet_key: 20112|new high school curriculum|skepticism of market economy
title: Curriculum and Ideology
author: Davide Cantoni, Yuyu Chen, David Y. Yang, Noam Yuchtman, Y. Jane Zhang
context: This paper studies the causal effect of China’s 8th Curriculum Reform—new high-school Politics and Economic Life textbooks—rolled out staggered across provinces between 2004–2010 on students’ beliefs. It analyzes survey responses from about 2,000 Peking University undergraduates (cohorts entering high school roughly 2006–2010) drawn from 29 provinces. The institutional setting includes gaokao-aligned curriculum changes, provincially staggered textbook adoption, and cohort exposure variation used to identify impacts on political participation, trust in government, ethnic attitudes, market views, and environmental preferences.

triplet_key: 20172|cash or credit incentive for migration|re-migration rate in subsequent years
title: Under-investment in a Profitable Technology: The Case of Seasonal Migration in Bangladesh
author: Gharad Bryan, Shyamal Chowdhury, Ahmed Mushfiq Mobarak
treatment: Cash or credit incentive for migration
context: This paper evaluates randomized incentives to seasonal out-migration during the 2008 ‘monga’ lean season in Rangpur (Kurigram and Lalmonirhat districts), northwestern Bangladesh. Between August 2008 and follow-ups through 2009 and 2011, 1,900 poor households in 100 villages (selected by low landholding and prior meal loss) were randomly assigned to cash (Tk600+bonus), credit, information, or control arms via local NGOs. Outcomes include household migration, consumption, earnings, savings, and re-migration in a setting of seasonal famine and constrained local labor markets.

triplet_key: 20256|board gender quota|female share in top quartile earnings
title: Breaking the Glass Ceiling? The Effect of Board Quotas on Female Labor Market Outcomes in Norway
author: Marianne Bertrand, Sandra E. Black, Sissel Jensen, Adriana Lleras-Muney
outcome: Female share in top quartile earnings
context: This paper uses linked Norwegian administrative data (population ages 16–64) from the late 1990s through 2014 to evaluate the causal effects of Norway’s 2003 board‑gender quota law (made compulsory by 2006, enforced by 2008) requiring roughly 40% representation of each gender on public limited companies’ (ASA) boards. Analyses examine firms, business groups and individual workers (board appointees, highly qualified cohorts, recent graduates), exploiting cross‑firm differences in pre‑2003 female board shares and cohort timing to study labor‑market and top‑echelon outcomes.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions

author: Marianne Bertrand, Sandra E. Black, Sissel Jensen, Adriana Lleras-Muney
outcome: Early career outcomes for young business graduates
context: This analysis uses Norwegian linked administrative data (late 1990s–2014) to evaluate Norway’s 2003 board‑gender quota (made compulsory by 2006, enforced by 2008). For the young‑graduate margin, the unit is individual recent graduates (graduate business programs, defined within three years of degree). The authors construct four cohorts—1994–98, 1999–2003 (pre‑reform), 2004–08 (reform), and 2009–13 (post‑reform)—and follow each cohort’s early career outcomes for five years, comparing business graduates (and sometimes natural‑science graduates as a control); they also report a student survey from the Norwegian School of Economics.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions
