title: Breaking the Glass Ceiling? The Effect of Board Quotas on Female Labor Market Outcomes in Norway
author: Marianne Bertrand, Sandra E. Black, Sissel Jensen, Adriana Lleras-Muney
outcome: Outcomes for highly qualified non-board women
context: This analysis uses linked Norwegian administrative data to study highly qualified non-board women around Norway's 2003 board‑gender quota (made compulsory in 2006 and enforced by 2008). It defines four cohorts (1993, 1998, 2003, 2008) of individuals aged 35–55 selected either by a board‑membership propensity (board‑specific human capital) index or by holding a business degree and being in the top 98th earnings percentile; each cohort is followed for five years. Outcomes are individual earnings and the probability of occupying top positions (e.g., top‑five earners) in Norwegian businesses, excluding women who became board members.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Decriminalizing Indoor Prostitution: Implications for Sexual Violence and Public Health
author: Scott Cunningham, Manisha Shah
context: This study exploits an unexpected 2003 Rhode Island District Court ruling that de facto decriminalized indoor prostitution from 2003–2009. Using state- and city-level panels (Rhode Island and Providence versus other US jurisdictions) together with newspaper, website, arrest, and CDC STI data on individuals and aggregate counts, the paper estimates effects on the indoor sex market, reported forcible rape offenses, and gonorrhea incidence within the US institutional setting where street prostitution remained criminalized.

title: Who Benefits from State Corporate Tax Cuts? A Local Labor Markets Approach with Heterogeneous Firms
author: Juan Carlos Suárez Serrato, Owen Zidar
context: The paper analyzes U.S. state corporate tax changes (statutory rates and apportionment rules) over roughly 1980–2012 using a panel of ~490 county-groups (aggregated from over 3,000 counties) and matched firm–establishment data. Units include establishments, local populations, wages and housing rents. Identification exploits state-level variation in corporate and personal tax rules — including external tax changes via apportionment — to estimate impacts on establishment entry, wages, rents and profits within a spatial-equilibrium framework with heterogeneous firms.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; event studies; others

title: Do Larger Health Insurance Subsidies Benefit Patients or Producers? Evidence from Medicare Advantage
author: Marika Cabral, Michael Geruso, Neale Mahoney
context: For this triplet the analysis uses U.S. county-year averages of Medicare Advantage plan benefits (physician and specialist copayments) constructed from plan-level data available 2000–2003 — with 2001 treated as the first post-reform year after BIPA (March 2001). The difference-in-differences estimates evaluate how BIPA's urban/rural payment floors, which raised county MA capitation payments, affected mean copayments by 2003 under the pre-2004 demographic risk-adjustment regime.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: The Role of Dynamic Renegotiation and Asymmetric Information in Financial Contracting
author: Michael R. Roberts
context: This empirical paper studies renegotiation of privately placed U.S. bank loans using hand-collected “loan paths” from SEC filings merged with Dealscan, covering the electronic-EDGAR era (primarily mid-1990s through about 2010). The unit of analysis is loan-tranche events and borrower firms (114 firms, 501 loan paths, 1,773 renegotiations). The institutional setting is the U.S. commercial and syndicated bank lending market with covenant-rich contracts; the paper exploits variation in loan events, borrower financial health, and market conditions to study timing and terms of renegotiation.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

triplet_key: 20492|adoption of essential drug list|speed of drug launch
title: Patents and the Global Diffusion of New Drugs
author: Iain M. Cockburn, Jean O. Lanjouw, Mark Schankerman
context: Using a newly constructed dataset of 642 new chemical entities and their launch dates across up to 76 countries from 1983–2002, the paper studies drug-country launch timing. It exploits cross-country and over-time variation in patent regimes (product vs. process, patent duration), pharmaceutical price‑control regimes, and health‑policy institutions (e.g., national formularies, WHO essential medicines lists) to estimate how these institutional and market factors affect the speed and geographic diffusion of new drugs.

title: Debt Relief and Debtor Outcomes: Measuring the Effects of Consumer Bankruptcy Protection
author: Will Dobbie, Jae Song
context: This paper studies first-time U.S. consumer bankruptcy filers who filed between 1992 and 2005 (analysis draws on court dockets 1992–2009 but requires 5+ years post‑filing), linking individual Chapter 13 filings across federal bankruptcy offices to Social Security Administration tax records and county-level DataQuick foreclosure data. The setting exploits blind rotation judge assignment within bankruptcy offices (pre‑2005 reform) that produces cross‑judge variation in confirmation rates; outcomes analyzed at the individual level include earnings, employment, mortality, and home foreclosure over the subsequent five years.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; PSM; other panel regressions; others

title: Debt Relief and Debtor Outcomes: Measuring the Effects of Consumer Bankruptcy Protection
author: Will Dobbie, Jae Song

Identification exploits blind-rotation judge assignment within bankruptcy offices. Job-tenure estimates are at the individual level over the five years after filing and are restricted to filers with at least one year of employment in both the pre- and post‑filing periods; results also examine variation by state garnishment rules.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; PSM; other panel regressions; others

title: Debt Relief and Debtor Outcomes: Measuring the Effects of Consumer Bankruptcy Protection
author: Will Dobbie, Jae Song
context: This analysis examines first-time U.S. Chapter 7 consumer bankruptcy filers who filed between 1992 and 2005 (restricted to ensure 5+ years of post‑filing outcomes), matched to Social Security Administration tax records and county-level DataQuick foreclosure data; it compares filers granted Chapter 7 protection to dismissed filers using an event‑study design to estimate effects on individual annual earnings and employment over the subsequent five years.

triplet_key: 20544|prohibition of underwriter board representation|average interest rate
title: Investment Banks as Corporate Monitors in the Early 20th Century United States
author: Carola Frydman, Eric Hilt
context: The paper studies U.S. NYSE-listed railroads (with industrial firms as a comparison group) over 1905–1929, exploiting the implementation of Section 10 of the Clayton Antitrust Act (passed 1914, enforced Jan 1, 1921). In this early-20th-century U.S. setting, railroads relied on bond finance and securities underwriters often held board seats; the law exogenously disrupted underwriter–board relationships and thus delegated monitoring, allowing the authors to examine effects on firm value, investment, leverage, and borrowing costs.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; event studies; PSM; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: The Market Impacts of Pharmaceutical Product Patents in Developing Countries:  Evidence from India
author: Mark Duggan, Craig Garthwaite, Aparajita Goyal
context: This paper studies how India’s 2005 implementation of TRIPS-compliant pharmaceutical product patents affected prices, quantities, and market structure using IMS MIDAS retail sales and a novel dataset of Indian patent grants for single-molecule products between 2003–2012. Analysis is at the molecule-quarter level (≈1,000 molecules, ≈6,000 products), exploiting variation in timing and strength of patent grants (including pre/post-1995 priority differences and pending/denied decisions) within the institutional setting of Section 3(d), compulsory licensing, and mailbox uncertainty.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: In Search of Labor Demand
author: Paul Beaudry, David A. Green, Benjamin M. Sand
context: This paper uses U.S. Census microdata from 1970–2000 decennial censuses and the 2007 ACS, analyzing roughly 152 U.S. metropolitan areas at the industry–city (industry×city) and aggregate city level over decadal intervals. The authors study how local wages, labor-market tightness, and population affect employment—emphasizing search frictions and entrepreneurial entry—using national industry shocks, initial city industry shares, immigration-weighted population predictors, and climate/amenity variation to isolate causal effects across traded and non-traded sectors.

title: Inspecting the Mechanism: Leverage and the Great Recession in the Eurozone
author: Philippe Martin, Thomas Philippon
context: This triplet refers to country‑level counterfactual simulations for euro‑area member states (particularly Greece, Ireland and Spain) over 2000–2012, assessing employment outcomes during the 2008–2012 bust under a “conservative fiscal policy” implemented by shutting down a modeled political‑economy spending bias in the paper’s structural heterogeneous‑household macro model. Simulations compare actual versus counterfactual trajectories for employment, public debt and spreads within the euro monetary union under sudden‑stop and credit‑cycle scenarios.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Inspecting the Mechanism: Leverage and the Great Recession in the Eurozone
author: Philippe Martin, Thomas Philippon
context: This triplet refers to a structural counterfactual simulation for four euro‑area periphery countries (Spain, Greece, Ireland, Portugal) over 2000–2012. The experiment assumes macro‑prudential policies prevent the private leverage boom (predicted private debt held constant), uses a cross‑sectional regression to map reduced private debt into lower bank recapitalization needs (0.25€ per €), and reports effects on country‑level employment (partial stabilization, notably in Ireland).
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

triplet_key: 20653|establishment of community health centers|age-adjusted mortality rate (50+)
title: The War on Poverty's Experiment in Public Medicine: Community Health Centers and the Mortality of Older Americans
author: Martha J. Bailey, Andrew Goodman-Bacon
context: This paper studies the rollout of Community Health Centers (CHCs) in the U.S. from 1965–1974 and evaluates effects on county-level age-adjusted mortality using mortality data through 1959–1988 (and to 1998 for a subset). The unit of analysis is county-year mortality (with focus on adults 50+ and cause-specific deaths). Identification exploits administrative variation in CHC establishment timing under the Office of Economic Opportunity within the War-on-Poverty policy environment, supplemented by county covariates and program survey evidence.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; PSM; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 20653|establishment of community health centers|medicare spending per-enrollee
title: The War on Poverty's Experiment in Public Medicine: Community Health Centers and the Mortality of Older Americans
author: Martha J. Bailey, Andrew Goodman-Bacon
context: Focusing on county-year outcomes, this triplet examines whether the 1965–1974 rollout of Community Health Centers affected county-level Medicare spending per enrollee. The analysis uses county-level Medicare administrative spending (Medicare per-enrollee expenditures available from 1966 onward, with total per-capita medical spending available 1959–1988) and exploits cross-county, staggered timing of CHC establishment under the Office of Economic Opportunity during the War-on-Poverty period.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; PSM; other panel regressions

title: The Effects of High-Skilled Immigration Policy on Firms: Evidence from Visa Lotteries
author: Kirk Doran, Alexander Gelber, Adam Isen
context: Using U.S. administrative data from USCIS H-1B lotteries (mainly FY2006–FY2007, with additional FY2008 and 2003–2008 analyses) matched to IRS firm-level tax records and USPTO patent data, the paper studies causal impacts of marginal H-1B visa allocations on U.S. firms (EINs). The institutional setting is the capped H-1B program (Regular and Advanced Degree Exemption) with lotteries when caps bind; outcomes include quarterly employment, patents, R&E tax credit claims, payroll per employee, and firm profits.
identification_methods: RCT; IV; RDD; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: The Effects of High-Skilled Immigration Policy on Firms: Evidence from Visa Lotteries
author: Kirk Doran, Alexander Gelber, Adam Isen
context: This estimate comes from the randomized USCIS H-1B lotteries in FY2006–FY2007, using firm-level (EIN) observations matched to IRS quarterly employment. The reported effect is the intent-to-treat impact in the year after the lottery (Q1–Q4) for firms applying on the cap day: for firms with ≤10 employees an extra chance H-1B win crowds out about 1.5 other workers (similar patterns hold for ≤30 and the full sample).

title: What are the Headwaters of Formal Savings? Experimental Evidence from Sri Lanka
author: Michael Callen, Suresh De Mel, Craig McIntosh, Christopher Woodruff
context: This paper reports a cluster-randomized field experiment in rural Sri Lanka (2010–2013) in Bandarawela and Mahiyangana districts. The unit is unbanked adult individuals (household respondents) living 5–15 km from National Savings Bank branches (n≈783; 389 treated, 406 control). The bank offered weekly door-to-door POS deposit collection (5% interest accounts); surveys ran monthly pre-treatment (Aug–Nov 2010) and high-frequency for 13 months post-treatment with follow-ups to Jan 2013. The study also randomized lockbox collection and seetu (rotating savings group) saturation to examine labor, income, transfers and savings.
identification_methods: RCT; DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Transition to Clean Technology
author: Daron Acemoglu, Ufuk Akcigit, Douglas Hanley, William Kerr
treatment: Delay of optimal policy by 50 years
context: This paper combines a micro-founded endogenous-growth model with firm-level estimation using U.S. energy-sector microdata from 1975–2004. It analyzes 1,576 innovative firms (6,228 firm-period observations) using Census LBD and Economic Census records, NSF R&D survey data, and USPTO patents to study firm R&D, patents, production, employment, entry/exit, and emissions. The structural model contrasts clean versus fossil-fuel (dirty) technologies, is calibrated to carbon-cycle dynamics, and evaluates policy counterfactuals (carbon taxes and clean-research subsidies) for production, innovation direction, and welfare.

title: Transition to Clean Technology
author: Daron Acemoglu, Ufuk Akcigit, Douglas Hanley, William Kerr

triplet_key: 20767|increasing rural residency positions|quality of rural residents
title: An Empirical Model of the Medical Match
author: Nikhil Agarwal
context: Analyzes the U.S. family medicine residency market (approximately 430 programs and ~3,000 residents per year) over 2003–2010 (with 2011–12 out-of-sample validation). Units are individual residents and residency programs participating in the centralized NRMP clearinghouse (pairwise-stable, many-to-one matching) where salaries are pre-set. The paper studies antitrust concerns about low resident wages and policy interventions to increase rural training, leveraging program and resident characteristics, Medicare-based reimbursement variation, and final-match data to estimate preferences and run counterfactual simulations.

title: Sequential Markets, Market Power and Arbitrage
author: Koichiro Ito, Mar Reguant
context: This study examines hourly bidding, scheduled quantities, and production by firms in the Iberian wholesale electricity market (Spain and Portugal) using plant- and bid-level data from OMIE and REE for January 2010–December 2012. Units of observation are firm/plant-hour-day bids and positions (with emphasis on wind farms and four dominant firms holding ~68% market share). Institutional features include a centralized day-ahead auction plus up to seven intra-day markets, no virtual bidding, capacity limits and regulatory constraints that restrict arbitrage. Analyses combine reduced-form and structural approaches.

triplet_key: 20787|multilateral reductions concentrated among wealthy countries|welfare of poorest countries
title: Universal Gravity
author: Treb Allen, Costas Arkolakis, Yuta Takahashi
treatment: multilateral reductions concentrated among wealthy countries
context: This result is computed using CEPII bilateral trade data for about 201 countries and WTO accession between 1995–2005 as an observed shock. Using a general-equilibrium gravity framework, the authors structurally estimate gravity constants from pre/post trade-flow changes and then compute welfare-maximizing multilateral reductions in trade frictions under the simplifying assumption that all frictions are equally costly to reduce. Units of analysis are country-pair trade flows and country-level incomes/welfare; the specific finding concerns welfare effects on the poorest countries when reductions are concentrated among wealthy countries.

title: Charters Without Lotteries: Testing Takeovers in New Orleans and Boston
author: Atila Abdulkadiroğlu, Joshua D. Angrist, Peter D. Hull, Parag A. Pathak
context: This triplet pertains specifically to middle-school students in New Orleans’ Recovery School District (RSD) who were affected by charter “takeovers” (traditional public legacy schools closed and reopened as charters with incumbent students guaranteed seats). The analysis focuses on roughly grades 4–8, with takeovers opening 2008–2013 and student outcomes measured on LEAP/iLEAP tests in 2011–2014. The unit is the individual student, using administrative enrollment, demographic, and standardized-test records; identification exploits grandfathering eligibility as an instrumental-variables source of variation in takeover attendance.
identification_methods: IV; DiD; event studies; RCT; others

title: How Much Energy Do Building Energy Codes Really Save? Evidence from California
author: Arik Levinson
context: This paper empirically evaluates whether California’s stringency building energy codes (first adopted in 1978 and tightened over subsequent decades) reduced residential energy use. It uses household-level data—California Energy Commission RASS monthly billing (2003, 2009) matched to NOAA temperatures and national RECS repeated cross-sections (1993–2009)—covering single-family homes built from pre‑1940 through 2005–08 in California and other US states, controlling for occupant, dwelling, climate‑zone, and temperature‑shock variation.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Can Health Insurance Competition Work? Evidence from Medicare Advantage
author: Vilsa Curto, Liran Einav, Jonathan Levin, Jay Bhattacharya
context: This paper analyzes Medicare Advantage in the United States using administrative data on all Medicare beneficiaries and plans from roughly 2006-2011 (FFS claims through 2010), studying county-level markets and plan-county-year choices. Units are Medicare beneficiaries, MA plans, and counties. It exploits institutional rules—county benchmarks, plan bids, risk adjustment, and the 75% rebate rule under post-2006 competitive bidding and periodic rebasing (2005/2007/2009) and the 2011 ACA benchmark freeze—to estimate how competition, selection, and bidding affect enrollee surplus, plan costs, and taxpayer payments.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; other panel regressions; others

title: Medicaid as an Investment in Children: What is the Long-Term Impact on Tax Receipts?
author: David W. Brown, Amanda E. Kowalski, Ithai Z. Lurie
context: This triplet uses U.S. administrative tax and Social Security death records to study cohorts born 1981–1984. Childhood Medicaid eligibility is simulated from birth to age 18 (using parental IRS information) and instrumented; adult mortality is measured as cumulative mortality from age 18 through age 28 using SSA death records. The sample excludes children who died before age 18.

triplet_key: 20838|jobs first welfare reform|welfare take-up among low-earning workers
title: Bounding the Labor Supply Responses to a Randomized Welfare Experiment: A Revealed Preference Approach
author: Patrick Kline, Melissa Tartari
context: This paper evaluates Connecticut’s Jobs First welfare reform using the MDRC randomized experiment (random assignment Jan 1996–Feb 1997) on low-income single-parent women, focusing on welfare take-up among low-earning workers in the first seven quarters after random assignment. It compares AFDC to Jobs First (100% earnings disregard up to the federal poverty line, stricter work requirements, sanctions, and time limits) using administrative UI earnings, welfare, and Food Stamp records.

triplet_key: 20889|nih funding|patents with different primary disease affiliation

author: Pierre Azoulay, Joshua S. Graff Zivin, Danielle Li, Bhaven N. Sampat
outcome: Patents with different primary disease affiliation
context: This paper studies the causal effect of U.S. NIH public R&D on private-sector patenting in the biopharmaceutical industry, linking NIH grants (1980–2005) to patents through 2012. The unit of analysis is a disease–science–year (DST) pairing and private-sector patents. Identification exploits NIH institutional features—chartered study sections, within-study “science” ranks, and Institute-level “rank-of-ranks” payline funding rules—across NIH Institutes and study sections in the U.S.

title: Regulating Innovation with Uncertain Quality:  Information, Risk, and Access in Medical Devices
author: Matthew Grennan, Robert Town
context: Study of coronary stent markets 2004–2013 in the US and six EU countries (France, Germany, Italy, Spain, UK), using monthly product-hospital-level data on prices and quantities and hand-collected clinical trial information. triplet_key: 21033|increase in chinese imports per worker|average log weekly wage
title: IV Quantile Regression for Group-level Treatments, with an Application to the Distributional Effects of Trade
author: Denis Chetverikov, Bradley Larsen, Christopher Palmer

context: This paper estimates how increases in Chinese import competition from roughly 1990–2007 affected average log weekly wages across U.S. local labor markets. Using individual wage microdata (Census/IPUMS and ACS) aggregated to commuting‑zone × decade groups (1990–2000 and 2000–2007), it estimates the effect on mean CZ log weekly wages via 2SLS, instrumenting imports per worker with a shift‑share instrument based on changes in Chinese imports to other countries to address endogeneity.

title: IV Quantile Regression for Group-level Treatments, with an Application to the Distributional Effects of Trade
author: Denis Chetverikov, Bradley Larsen, Christopher Palmer

Identification uses a difference‑in‑differences style design with state and year effects and the grouped quantile regression (group-by-group quantiles then linear second stage) allowing for group-level unobservables.

title: Tax Cuts For Whom? Heterogeneous Effects of Income Tax Changes on Growth and Employment
author: Owen M. Zidar
context: This paper uses U.S. federal income and payroll tax changes from the postwar period (1950–2011, with state analyses mainly 1980–2007) and state-level variation in income distributions to construct regional tax shocks by income group (bottom 90% vs top 10%). Units are state-year observations aggregated from individual tax returns via NBER TAXSIM; main outcomes are state employment, GDP, payrolls, wages and consumption. The analysis focuses on exogenous federal tax changes (per Romer & Romer 2010), exploiting cross-state exposure heterogeneity and panel variation while controlling for social-insurance policy changes.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions

title: Ghost-House Busters: The Electoral Response to a Large Anti Tax Evasion Program
author: Lorenzo Casaburi, Ugo Troiano
context: This paper studies the electoral impact of Italy’s nationwide “Ghost Buildings” anti–tax-evasion program (identification waves 2007–2010, registration deadline April 2011) using municipal-level data on detected and registered unlisted buildings and municipal elections (unit: Italian towns/municipalities, ~7,720 towns). The program used aerial imagery and land-registry overlays administered by the Agenzia del Territorio; local governments implemented registrations and collected additional local tax revenue. Analysis compares incumbent reelection and electoral competitiveness before and after program onset.

triplet_key: 21291|county-specific pigovian vmt taxes on electric and gasoline miles|deadweight loss
title: Environmental Benefits from Driving Electric Vehicles?
author: Stephen P. Holland, Erin T. Mansur, Nicholas Z. Muller, Andrew J. Yates
treatment: County-specific Pigovian VMT taxes on electric and gasoline miles
context: This paper (Holland et al., 2015) estimates the environmental benefits of battery electric vehicles across the contiguous United States by integrating a discrete-choice model of new-vehicle purchases, plant-level econometric estimates of marginal emissions using hourly 2010–2012 power-plant data (1,486 plants) and regional load across nine NERC regions, and the AP2 air-pollution damage model based on a 2011 emissions inventory. Units of analysis include power plants, counties (3,110), MSAs/states and prospective new-vehicle buyers; the policy context includes the $7,500 federal EV purchase credit and CAFE rules.

title: Subsidy Design in Privately-Provided Social Insurance: Lessons from Medicare Part D
author: Francesco Decarolis, Maria Polyakova, Stephen P. Ryan
context: Using their estimated structural demand-and-supply model for the U.S. Medicare Part D stand-alone PDP market (34 geographic markets), the authors simulate counterfactual flat voucher subsidies in 2010 with the MA–PD outside option held fixed. They compute equilibrium outcomes and total welfare for vouchers ranging $0–$1,500 (in $100 increments), reporting results such as total surplus peaking at an $800 voucher and remaining positive until $1,300.

title: Subsidy Design in Privately-Provided Social Insurance: Lessons from Medicare Part D
author: Francesco Decarolis, Maria Polyakova, Stephen P. Ryan
context: Using 2007–2010 U.S. Medicare data, the paper studies individual Medicare beneficiaries’ choices and insurers’ pricing across 34 Part D geographic markets (plan-level and 20% sample claimant data). It analyzes how CMS’s bid-based premium subsidy formula, risk-adjustment payments, LIS (low-income subsidy) random assignment rules, and the Medicare Advantage (MA–PD) outside option shape insurer incentives, consumer sorting by health-risk type, equilibrium prices, and welfare. The authors estimate a structural demand and supply model and run counterfactual subsidy simulations.

title: The Value of Medicaid: Interpreting Results from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment
author: Amy Finkelstein, Nathaniel Hendren, Erzo F.P. Luttmer
context: This paper evaluates the welfare value of Medicaid using the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment: a randomized Medicaid expansion in Oregon (implemented via a public lottery in 2008) that offered low-income, uninsured adults the opportunity to enroll. The authors combine randomized-assignment estimates from surveys and administrative data with welfare valuation frameworks to quantify Medicaid’s benefits per dollar of government spending, health and financial outcomes for recipients, and transfers to providers and implicit insurers within the U.S. Medicaid policy setting.

title: The Rise of Domestic Outsourcing and the Evolution of the German Wage Structure
author: Deborah Goldschmidt, Johannes F. Schmieder
context: This paper studies domestic outsourcing in Germany using the universe of social-security administrative records (IEB) mainly from the mid-1970s through 2009 (with some analyses starting 1979 or 1999 for specific codes). It analyzes individual workers, jobs and establishments (West Germany focus in some tests) in food, cleaning, security and logistics occupations during a period of deregulation, rising temp-agency use and declining collective-bargaining coverage, exploiting observed worker flows, on-site spin-offs, and establishment histories to identify wage and rent changes.
identification_methods: event studies; PSM; other panel regressions

title: Clearing Up the Fiscal Multiplier Morass: Prior and Posterior Analysis
author: Eric M. Leeper, Nora Traum, Todd B. Walker
context: This paper uses U.S. quarterly macro data (1955q1–2014q2, with pre-Volcker and Great Moderation subperiods) to estimate a monetary DSGE model augmented with fiscal details—government consumption and transfers, maturity structure of nominal government debt, and steady-state distortionary taxes—to quantify government spending multipliers under two monetary–fiscal regimes (active monetary/passive fiscal and active fiscal/passive monetary). title: Diversification through Trade
author: Francesco Caselli, Miklós Koren, Milan Lisicky, Silvana Tenreyro
context: This paper studies how declining trade costs since the early 1970s through 2007 affected GDP volatility across a multi-country sample (24 core countries plus a rest-of-world aggregate). The authors combine sector-level data (24 sectors), bilateral trade flows, and country-year observations with a calibrated multi-sector stochastic trade model that features country- and sector-specific productivity shocks and ex-ante labor allocation. Using the model and data, they back out trade costs and shocks and run counterfactuals to quantify trade’s causal impact on aggregate volatility.

title: State Taxes and Spatial Misallocation

title: University Innovation and the Professor's Privilege
author: Hans K. Hvide, Benjamin F. Jones
context: The paper analyzes Norway’s January 1, 2003 reform that ended the “professor’s privilege,” transferring majority commercialization rights to universities and creating TTOs. Using linked Norwegian administrative data on university-employed PhDs, national firm incorporation records (startup analysis 2000–2007), and patent applications (1995–2010), the authors compare individual-level entrepreneurship and patenting before and after the reform against various control groups to estimate the policy’s causal effects on startup and patenting rates and quality.

title: Temporary Migration and Endogenous Risk Sharing in Village India
author: Melanie Morten
context: This paper studies temporary migration and endogenous informal risk sharing in semi-arid rural India using the ICRISAT VLS2 household panel (2001–2004). The institutional setting features limited-commitment informal insurance networks, predominantly temporary (short-term) male migration, and policy counterfactuals including reduced migration costs and an NREGA-like rural employment guarantee.

title: Volatility and the Gains from Trade

title: Potential Unemployment Insurance Duration and Labor Supply: The Individual and Market-Level Response to a Benefit Cut
author: Andrew C. Johnston, Alexandre Mas

title: Do Government Audits Reduce Corruption? Estimating the Impacts of Exposing Corrupt Politicians
author: Eric Avis, Claudio Ferraz, Frederico Finan
context: This triplet is based on a structural counterfactual simulation calibrated to Brazil’s municipal model estimated for mayors audited in the 1.5 years prior to the 2012 mayoral elections. The simulation raises ego rents (value of holding office) — e.g., increasing mayoral wages by the average rents extracted — while holding the pool of potential candidates constant. The predicted outcome is first-term corruption measured as log acts of corruption, which falls about 3% (4.02 → 3.99).

title: Does Career Risk Deter Potential Entrepreneurs?
author: Joshua D. Gottlieb, Richard R. Townsend, Ting Xu
context: This analysis exploits Canada’s 2001 reform that extended job‑protected parental leave for births on/after Dec 31, 2000, using individual‑level Canadian microdata (primarily the 2006 long‑form Census) to measure self‑employment about five years after the cutoff among parents (first‑time singleton mothers versus fathers) across provinces. For this triplet the paper reports heterogeneous effects concentrated on individuals who had been wage‑employed in industries with a high wage penalty for leaving and then returning from entrepreneurship.

title: Does Career Risk Deter Potential Entrepreneurs?
author: Joshua D. Gottlieb, Richard R. Townsend, Ting Xu
context: This paper examines whether reducing career risk raises entrepreneurship by exploiting Canada’s 2001 reform that extended job-protected parental leave (income-replacement also increased) for births on or after Dec 31, 2000. Using individual-level Canadian microdata (primarily the 2006 long-form Census and administrative sources), the analysis focuses on parents—especially first-time singleton mothers versus fathers—across provinces, measuring self-employment about five years after the cutoff and leveraging provincial heterogeneity in leave increases.

title: Does Career Risk Deter Potential Entrepreneurs?
author: Joshua D. Gottlieb, Richard R. Townsend, Ting Xu
context: This paper examines whether reducing career risk raises entrepreneurship by exploiting Canada’s 2001 reform that extended job-protected parental leave (income-replacement also increased) for births on or after Dec 31, 2000. Using individual-level Canadian microdata (primarily the 2006 long-form Census and administrative sources), the analysis focuses on parents—especially first-time singleton mothers versus fathers—across provinces, measuring self-employment about five years after the cutoff and leveraging provincial heterogeneity in leave increases.

title: Does Career Risk Deter Potential Entrepreneurs?
author: Joshua D. Gottlieb, Richard R. Townsend, Ting Xu
context: This analysis examines whether Canada’s 2001 extension of job-protected parental leave (eligible for births on/after Dec 31, 2000) affected incorporated entrepreneurship. It uses individual-level 2006 long-form Census data on parents—focusing on first-time singleton mothers around the birth-date cutoff (with fathers as a control group)—and measures outcomes five years after the cutoff, finding increases in incorporated firms that hire employees. The design exploits provincial heterogeneity in leave changes and a regression-discontinuity / difference-in-discontinuities framework around the birth-date cutoff.

title: The Morale Effects of Pay Inequality
author: Emily Breza, Supreet Kaur, Yogita Shamdasani
context: A month-long randomized field experiment (14 one-month rounds in 201?–2016) with 378 male seasonal manufacturing workers in semi-rural Odisha, India (near Bhubaneswar). Workers were organized into 3-person production units making local goods, received a two-week training baseline, then units were randomized to wage structures (equal vs. unequal flat daily wages) with post-treatment observation for 20 days. Outcomes include daily attendance, individual output, endline cooperative games, and surveys; pay secrecy and task observability were experimentally varied.

triplet_key: 22589|allocating 100% seats to tier-specific quotas|minority representation at payton
title: The Efficiency of Race-Neutral Alternatives to Race-Based Affirmative Action: Evidence from Chicago's Exam Schools
author: Glenn Ellison, Parag A. Pathak
context: Using 2013–14 Chicago Public Schools applicant data (≈16,818 applicants), this paper evaluates CPS’s 2010–2012 shift from race-based racial quotas to a race-neutral, place-based tier admissions system for selective exam high schools (notably Walter Payton and Northside). The analysis is set against the 1980s consent decree and post-2007 Supreme Court limits on race-based affirmative action. The authors simulate counterfactual admissions under alternative race-neutral and race-conscious rules to quantify efficiency and diversity trade-offs across students as the unit of observation.

triplet_key: 22637|rising import competition from china|election of liberal democrat
title: Importing Political Polarization? The Electoral Consequences of Rising Trade Exposure
author: David Autor, David Dorn, Gordon Hanson, Kaveh Majlesi
context: This paper studies the political consequences of rising import competition from China following China’s WTO accession, focusing on the 2000s and extending to 2016. It uses U.S. commuting zones, county-by-congressional-district cells, counties, and individual Pew survey respondents to link CZ-level changes in Chinese import penetration to shifts in voter ideology (Pew, 2004–2014/15), congressional DW-NOMINATE scores (elections 2002–2010) and presidential county vote shares (2000–2016). The setting exploits heterogeneous industry exposure to a concentrated manufacturing shock amid local job and plant losses.

title: Incarceration, Recidivism and Employment
author: Manudeep Bhuller, Gordon B. Dahl, Katrine V. Løken, Magne Mogstad
context: Using linked Norwegian administrative registers for non-confession district-court defendants (cases decided 2005–2009; judge-stringency instrument constructed using 2005–2014), the analysis follows individuals up to five years. This triplet is specific to defendants who were not working prior to incarceration: for this subgroup, incarceration increases participation in job-training and raises the employment rate within five years by about 36 percentage points. The unit is the individual defendant and the setting exploits random assignment of cases to judges and Norway’s rehabilitative prison system (open prisons, training and re-entry programs).
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Incarceration, Recidivism and Employment
author: Manudeep Bhuller, Gordon B. Dahl, Katrine V. Løken, Magne Mogstad
context: Using linked administrative registers for Norway, the paper studies defendants in district court criminal cases decided mainly 2005–2009 (instrument constructed using 2005–2014) and follows individuals for up to five years. The unit is individual defendants (non-confession cases) with complete criminal, prison, and labor-market records. The setting exploits Norway’s random assignment of court cases to judges (no plea bargaining) and a rehabilitative prison system (open prisons, training programs, average served sentence ≈6 months) to estimate effects of incarceration on recidivism and employment.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Long-Range Growth: Economic Development in the Global Network of Air Links
author: Filipe Campante, David Yanagizawa-Drott
context: Global empirical study of 819 major international airport cities (≈200 countries) using city‑pair, airport, grid‑cell and firm‑level units to measure effects on night‑lights, population, passenger flows, and foreign ownership. It exploits air‑route data (1989–2014), night‑lights growth (1992–2010) and firm ownership/events (Orbis/GDELT, snapshot through 2016). Identification hinges on a regulatory/technological discontinuity at 6000 miles (crew duty‑time rules and the introduction of Boeing 747‑400 and Airbus A330/A340), which exogenously shifted likelihood of direct long‑haul flights.

title: Stealing Deposits: Deposit Insurance, Risk-Taking and the Removal of Market Discipline in Early 20th Century Banks
author: Charles W. Calomiris, Matthew S. Jaremski
context: This paper studies the causal impact of state-level deposit insurance laws in the United States during 1900–1930 (with key policy adoptions 1908–1918 and outcomes through the early 1920s), exploiting staggered, state-by-state adoption concentrated in Midwestern and Plains states and the WWI agricultural price boom. The unit of analysis is banks (biennial bank-level and annual state-level aggregates: insured state banks, uninsured state banks, and national banks) with county economic controls; institutional details include mandatory vs. voluntary insurance and national banks’ exclusion from systems.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Self-Fulfilling Debt Crises: A Quantitative Analysis
author: Luigi Bocola, Alessandro Dovis
context: This triplet concerns Italy’s sovereign bond market and the ECB’s 2012 Outright Monetary Transactions (OMT) announcements. The paper models OMT as a bond-price-floor implemented by a deep-pocketed central bank and, using a structural sovereign-debt model calibrated to pre-OMT Italian data, constructs a counterfactual (no-rollover-crises) spread series and compares it to the actual Italian spread observed after the OMT announcements (September 2012). Units are macro/sovereign (Italian central-government aggregates and aggregate bond-market spreads) within the Euro-area institutional setting.

title: Credit Expansion and Neglected Crash Risk
author: Matthew Baron, Wei Xiong
context: This paper analyzes quarterly data from 1920–2012 for 20 developed countries, using country-level bank-sector observations (past three-year change in bank credit-to-GDP, bank equity indices, dividend yields) to study how credit expansions predict subsequent bank equity crash risk and mean excess returns. The institutional setting focuses on bank lending to households and non-financial firms (BIS and Schularick & Taylor loan series), spans major historical episodes (Great Depression, 1990s crises, 2007–08), and uses country-quarter observations aggregated to non-overlapping 1–3 year horizons with standard market controls.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; event studies; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Securing Property Rights
author: A. Patrick Behrer, Edward L. Glaeser, Giacomo A.M. Ponzetto, Andrei Shleifer
context: Combining a theoretical model with county‑level empirical analysis, the paper studies U.S. water quality around the 1972 Clean Water Act. It uses EPA/STORET monitor data aggregated to county‑year units (1962–1985) to measure pollution in rivers and lakes and exploits the CWA’s shift from common‑law liability to permit‑based regulation (point‑source vs non‑point source) and state‑level heterogeneity (e.g., judicial corruption). The empirical setting evaluates how regulatory enforcement changed pollution and enforcement effectiveness across industrial and agricultural counties.

title: Subsidy Policies and Insurance Demand
author: Jing Cai, Alain de Janvry, Elisabeth Sadoulet
context: This paper uses a two-year (2010–2011) randomized field experiment in Jiangxi province, China, with ~3,500 rice-farming households across 134 villages to study how subsidy policies affect demand for a newly introduced area-index weather insurance. The product (actuarially fair premium ≈12 RMB/mu) faced randomized village-level first-year subsidies (free vs 70% subsidized) and household-level varied second-year prices (40–90% subsidies). The setting included large first-year payouts (~59% of insured) and rich social-network data; results are validated with administrative data and multi-year simulations (2010–2014).
identification_methods: RCT; IV; RDD; other panel regressions; others

triplet_key: 22711|removal of chapter 13 bankruptcy flag|implied probability of default
title: Bad Credit, No Problem? Credit and Labor Market Consequences of Bad Credit Reports
author: Will Dobbie, Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham, Neale Mahoney, Jae Song
context: This empirical study analyzes U.S. individual bankruptcy filers (aged ~23–47 at filing) who filed between 1995–2004, exploiting that credit bureaus voluntarily remove Chapter 13 bankruptcy flags after seven years but Chapter 7 flags after ten. Using Equifax CCP credit-bureau panels (1999–2015) and matched Social Security administrative earnings records (available through 2014), the authors estimate how flag removal (roughly 2002–2011 removals) affects credit scores, credit-card and mortgage borrowing, and formal labor-market outcomes within the FCRA credit-reporting institutional setting.

title: Minimum Payments and Debt Paydown in Consumer Credit Cards
author: Benjamin J. Keys, Jialan Wang
context: This empirical study uses account-month data from the CFPB Credit Card Database covering February 2008–December 2013 for large U.S. general-purpose credit card issuers (roughly 25% of market; a 1% sample yields ≈40 million account-months). The unit is individual credit‑card accounts; the paper exploits issuer changes to minimum‑payment formulas (four increases, one decrease) and the CARD Act (Feb 2010) three‑year payoff disclosure (with regulatory exemption rules) to identify causal effects on repayment behavior in the U.S. credit‑card market.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Identifying the Benefits from Homeownership: A Swedish Experiment
author: Paolo Sodini, Stijn Van Nieuwerburgh, Roine Vestman, Ulf von Lilienfeld-Toal
context: This paper studies the causal effects of homeownership using a quasi-experiment in Stockholm, Sweden from 1999–2007. It follows tenants (households) of municipally owned rental apartment buildings that underwent co-op privatization: a 2002 law (Stopplag) introduced quasi-random County Board approvals/denials of pending conversions, creating exogenous variation in ownership. Using linked administrative registers on demographics, income, detailed balance sheets, housing transactions, and building characteristics, the authors measure impacts on wealth, consumption, mobility, and portfolio choice.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other panel regressions

title: The Long-Run Effects of Childhood Insurance Coverage: Medicaid Implementation, Adult Health, and Labor Market Outcomes
author: Andrew Goodman-Bacon
context: This triplet examines how early-childhood Medicaid eligibility from the original U.S. Medicaid rollout (state implementation 1966–1970) affected adult disability transfer receipt for birth cohorts roughly 1936–1975. Outcomes are measured for native-born individuals aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race, using Census/ACS disability and program-participation data (primarily 2000–2007). Identification exploits the federal categorical-eligibility linkage to AFDC and cross-state differences in pre‑Medicaid AFDC rates to construct migration‑adjusted cumulative childhood exposure; effects on disability transfers are driven primarily by whites.

title: The Long-Run Effects of Childhood Insurance Coverage: Medicaid Implementation, Adult Health, and Labor Market Outcomes
author: Andrew Goodman-Bacon
context: This paper studies long-run effects of childhood Medicaid eligibility from the original U.S. Medicaid rollout (state implementation 1966–1970) by comparing birth cohorts (roughly 1936–1975, followed into 1980–2014) across U.S. states. The unit is individuals aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race. For the extensive-margin employment triplet, effects are reported primarily for white individuals and are driven by early-childhood exposure (roughly ages 0–10). Identification leverages the federal categorical-eligibility mandate tied to AFDC, cross-state differences in pre-existing AFDC rates, and migration-adjusted cumulative childhood exposure using event-study and IV/DiD designs.

title: The Long-Run Effects of Childhood Insurance Coverage: Medicaid Implementation, Adult Health, and Labor Market Outcomes
author: Andrew Goodman-Bacon
context: This estimate examines the effect of early-childhood Medicaid eligibility on adult self-reported disability (ambulatory difficulty), using native-born individuals age 25–64 born before 1976 observed in the 2000–2007 Census/ACS. Observations are aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race. Identification exploits 1966–1970 state Medicaid rollout and cross-state differences in initial AFDC rates, using event-study specifications and IV (predicted cumulative eligibility); age-specific effects are tested (split at age 10 for whites and age 4 for nonwhites).

title: The Long-Run Effects of Childhood Insurance Coverage: Medicaid Implementation, Adult Health, and Labor Market Outcomes
author: Andrew Goodman-Bacon

The outcome here—adult total income—is measured using the 2000–2014 Census and ACS at the individual level aggregated by birth state, birth year, and race; the income analyses in this section focus primarily on white adults. Identification leverages the federal categorical-eligibility rule tied to AFDC, cross‑state differences in pre‑Medicaid AFDC rates, and migration‑adjusted cumulative exposure.

triplet_key: 22903|first-time homebuyer credit (fthc)|default rates of policy-period buyers
title: Stimulating Housing Markets
author: David Berger, Nicholas Turner, Eric Zwick
context: The paper examines default rates for homebuyers who purchased during the FTHC policy (2009–2010) using DataQuick deed-level transaction data for the U.S. ZIP-sample. The analysis restricts to buyers with FHA-insured mortgages and follows cohorts (2009, 2010 policy cohorts) for up to 36 months after purchase, comparing their distress-transition rates to pre-policy cohorts (2006–2008) and a 2011 post-policy cohort. It measures defaults via chronological distress classifications (short sale, foreclosure, REO) recorded in transaction histories.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Stimulating Housing Markets
author: David Berger, Nicholas Turner, Eric Zwick
context: This paper studies the U.S. First-Time Homebuyer Credit during the Great Recession (2008–2010, focusing on the Feb 2009–Jul 2010 expansions), exploiting ZIP-code–level monthly housing transactions and de-identified individual tax records for ≈9,000 ZIPs (≈69% of the 2007 U.S. population). Units include individual homebuyers, tax filers, and ZIP-level housing markets and prices. The policy was a refundable $8,000 credit (first-time buyer and income eligibility rules) enacted to spur demand amid housing inventory overhang, coinciding with expanded FHA low-down-payment lending and high foreclosure activity.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Optimal Taxation and R&D Policies
author: Ufuk Akcigit, Douglas Hanley, Stefanie Stantcheva
treatment: Linear corporate taxes + nonlinear R&D subsidy
context: The paper studies U.S. firms using longitudinal firm-level administrative and accounting data matched to USPTO patent citation data (Census LBD/Census of Manufacturers and COMPUSTAT samples). It models firms (the unit of observation) undertaking R&D under the U.S. patent/IPR environment with technological spillovers, private stochastic research-productivity types, and observable R&D expenditures versus unobservable effort. The analysis uses structural estimation and calibrated parameters to quantify optimal corporate tax and R&D subsidy policies and counterfactual reforms in this setting.

triplet_key: 22985|mortgage boom (increase in mortgage debt)|reallocation of mortgage debt toward low-income borrowers
title: Cross-Sectional Patterns of Mortgage Debt during the Housing Boom: Evidence and Implications
author: Christopher L. Foote, Lara Loewenstein, Paul S. Willen
outcome: Reallocation of mortgage debt toward low-income borrowers
context: This paper examines the U.S. housing credit cycle in the early 2000s (roughly 1999–2007, with emphasis on 2001–2006), using household- and community-level data to study stocks and gross flows of mortgage debt. Units include households (Survey of Consumer Finances), individual credit histories (Equifax CCP), zip codes merged with IRS income, Core Based Statistical Areas (CBSAs), and loan-level CoreLogic securitization records. The institutional setting is the U.S. mortgage market during rapidly rising house prices, expanded subprime/Alt-A securitization, and changing lending standards.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Political Determinants of Competition in the Mobile Telecommunication Industry
author: Mara Faccio, Luigi Zingales
context: This paper uses cross-country and operator-level data on 148 countries in the mobile telecommunications industry over roughly 2003–2014 (with price bundles concentrated in 2010–2014 and some series into 2015). Units include country-year (and country-quarter) regulatory and market outcomes and firm-level operator metrics (market shares, ARPU, EBITDA, capex). It exploits variation in ITU-coded national rules (number portability, VoIP, spectrum tradability, foreign ownership, regulator independence), spectrum auctions, and measures of political connections to study effects on concentration, prices, quality, and investments.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Imperfect Markets versus Imperfect Regulation in U.S. Electricity Generation
author: Steve Cicala
context: This paper analyzes hourly generation and demand across U.S. Power Control Areas (PCAs) and individual generating units from 1999–2012, using a nationwide panel of PCA-date-hour and unit-hour observations. It exploits the staggered creation and expansion of centralized wholesale electricity markets (market-based dispatch) under FERC reforms, comparing PCAs that switched to market dispatch to those that did not. The study measures allocative efficiency changes—out-of-merit operating costs and gains from interarea trade—holding capacities, fuel and emissions prices, and demand fixed.

triplet_key: 23101|increase in corporate taxes on largest firms|retail cost of living inflation (bottom 20%)
title: Firm Heterogeneity in Consumption Baskets: Evidence from Home and Store Scanner Data
author: Benjamin Faber, Thibault Fally
treatment: Increase in corporate taxes on largest firms
context: Using U.S. AC Nielsen home and retail scanner microdata from 2006–2014, this paper studies how heterogeneous firms’ sizes and qualities are embodied in household consumption baskets. The analysis links on average ~59,000 households and ~27,000 retail stores per half-year across 1,000+ disaggregated product modules (brands within modules) and compares consumption by income quintile. It estimates demand and supply parameters, then calibrates a structural GE model to quantify policy counterfactuals—e.g., progressive income-tax reform, closing corporate-tax loopholes, and trade integration—affecting prices and real-income inequality.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: The (Dis)Advantages of Clearinghouses Before the Fed
author: Matthew S. Jaremski
context: Using annual bank-level data from 1880–1910 across seven U.S. states (Indiana, Michigan, Minnesota, New York, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Wisconsin), the paper studies city-level private clearinghouse entry and bank membership during the National Banking Era. Units are individual national and state commercial banks (bank-year observations), and the setting features private, city-based clearinghouses that selectively provided emergency liquidity during panics (e.g., 1893, 1907). The institutional environment includes interbank correspondent networks, unit banking, and heterogeneous membership affecting systemic liquidity and bank closures.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: The Macroeconomic Effects of Government Asset Purchases: Evidence from Postwar US Housing Credit Policy
author: Andrew Fieldhouse, Karel Mertens, Morten O. Ravn
context: This paper studies the macroeconomic effects of federal housing agencies' mortgage portfolio purchases in the United States from the postwar era with emphasis on January 1967–December 2006 (and extensions through the 2007–08 crisis). The institutional setting is US GSE policy (capital requirements, portfolio caps, conforming loan limits, Treasury/Fed interventions); identification exploits ex‑ante narrative policy events and GSE stock‑market news.

title: Recovery from the Great Depression: The Farm Channel in Spring 1933
author: Joshua K. Hausman, Paul W. Rhode, Johannes F. Wieland
context: The paper studies the U.S. recovery focusing on March–July 1933 (and the April 19, 1933 devaluation) across national commodity markets and subnational units (states and counties). Units include daily spot and futures farm prices, state- and county-level new auto sales, farm product values, and bank deposits. The institutional setting is the Roosevelt administration’s departure from the gold standard, ensuing dollar devaluation, Agricultural Adjustment Administration actions, widespread farm mortgage distress, and banking crises during the Great Depression.
identification_methods: event studies; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: The Returns to Nursing: Evidence from a Parental Leave Program
author: Benjamin U. Friedrich, Martin B. Hackmann
context: This paper studies Denmark's 1994 parental leave expansion using linked employer-employee, hospital patient, and death-register data for 1990–2000. It analyzes individual workers (nurses, nursing assistants, doctors), health providers (hospitals and nursing homes) and county-level markets, exploiting variation in eligibility by youngest-child age and county-sector exposure to a sudden 1994 reform that allowed up to one year leave for children ≤8 (job-protected, government-funded). The paper examines effects on nurse employment, hospital readmissions, and nursing-home mortality across counties and sectors.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Impulsive Consumption and Financial Wellbeing: Evidence from an Increase in the Availability of Alcohol
author: Itzhak Ben-David, Marieke Bos
context: This triplet refers to the Swedish February 2000 policy experiment that opened state retail liquor stores (Systembolaget) on Saturdays in six treatment counties. It concerns individuals aged 20–25 (the legally eligible buyers) and measures the change in total credit balances (pawn plus mainstream credit) using individual-level pawnbroker records and credit-bureau snapshots around 1999–2001, comparing treated and control counties in a pre/post framework.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Are Larger Banks Valued More Highly?
author: Bernadette A. Minton, René M. Stulz, Alvaro G. Taboada
context: This paper studies U.S. publicly traded bank holding companies (171 banks, 1987–2015, with emphasis on the pre‑crisis window 1987–2006) using FRY‑9C and CRSP/Compustat data to assess how market valuation (Tobin’s q and market-to-book) varies with bank asset size—particularly above the Dodd‑Frank enhanced‑supervision threshold ($50 billion in 2010 dollars). The analysis contrasts pre‑ and post‑Global Financial Crisis regulatory regimes (Basel revisions, Dodd‑Frank) and uses cross‑sectional and within‑bank variation to probe TBTF‑related valuation effects.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Government Debt and Corporate Leverage: International Evidence
author: Irem Demirci, Jennifer Huang, Clemens Sialm
context: Using firm- and country-level panel data for 40 countries over 1990–2014 (38,776 firms; 343,403 firm-year observations), the paper studies how changes in government debt—especially domestically held debt—affect corporate leverage. It exploits cross-country and time variation, compares pre- and post-EMU integration (pre-1999 vs post-1999) for European Monetary Union members, and leverages military-spending shocks as instruments. Units are firms (and aggregated country-year panels) operating in diverse institutional settings with varying equity-market development and bank dependence.

title: Disability Benefits, Consumption Insurance, and Household Labor Supply
author: David Autor, Andreas Ravndal Kostol, Magne Mogstad, Bradley Setzler
context: This empirical study analyzes individuals who appealed initially denied Disability Insurance (DI) claims in Norway during 1994–2005 (with linked administrative records spanning 1967–2010 and appeals 1989–2011). title: Reorganization or Liquidation: Bankruptcy Choice and Firm Dynamics
author: Dean Corbae, Pablo D'Erasmo
context: Using U.S. firm-level Compustat data (1980–2014) on publicly listed firms and bankruptcy events, the paper studies how U.S. bankruptcy law (Chapter 7 liquidation vs Chapter 11 reorganization) affects corporate capital structure, investment, entry/exit, and aggregate allocative efficiency. title: Does Strategic Ability Affect Efficiency? Evidence from Electricity Markets
author: Ali Hortaçsu, Fernando Luco, Steven L. Puller, Dongni Zhu
context: This paper studies firm bidding in Texas’s restructured wholesale electricity balancing (day-of) market in ERCOT during the early market years (sample spanning the first half of year 2: Aug 2002–Jan 2003). The unit of observation is hourly balancing auctions and firm-level bids (12 focal generating firms, covering ~66% capacity) with observed plant-level marginal costs. The institutional setting is a multi-unit, uniform-price balancing auction following the 2001 market restructuring; the study exploits a mid-sample two-month nuclear outage as a demand/cost shock and uses structural behavioral models to analyze efficiency and merger counterfactuals.
identification_methods: event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Targeted Debt Relief and the Origins of Financial Distress: Experimental Evidence from Distressed Credit Card Borrowers
author: Will Dobbie, Jae Song
context: Between January 2005 and August 2006 Money Management International randomized 79,739 prospective clients across U.S. states into debt-management-plan (DMP) offers. title: Vulnerability and Clientelism
author: Gustavo J. Bobonis, Paul Gertler, Marco Gonzalez-Navarro, Simeon Nichter

context: This paper studies rural, water‑vulnerable households in Brazil’s semi‑arid Northeast during 2011–2013. title: Surprise! Out-of-Network Billing for Emergency Care in the United States
author: Zack Cooper, Fiona Scott Morton, Nathan Shekita
context: Using commercial claims from a large insurer (covering tens of millions) over 2011–2015 across U.S. hospital emergency departments, the paper analyzes ED episodes and hospitals to study out-of-network physician billing. It exploits the entry timing of large ED outsourcing firms (EmCare, TeamHealth) into specific hospitals (2011–2015) and a 2014 New York arbitration law to estimate causal effects. Institutional context: hospitals outsource ED staffing, physicians and hospitals contract separately with insurers, producing surprise balance bills and a bargaining outside option for physicians.

title: Private Equity and Financial Fragility during the Crisis
author: Shai Bernstein, Josh Lerner, Filippo Mezzanotti
context: This paper examines UK private-equity-backed firms versus matched non-PE peers around the 2008 financial crisis, using firm-level balance-sheet and income data (Amadeus/Orbis) over 2004–2011. The unit of analysis is UK non-financial firms with PE buyouts by end‑2007. The institutional setting features a sharp UK credit freeze beginning in 2008, large pre-crisis PE fundraising and leverage, and heterogeneity in PE fund resources (dry powder and fund vintage) used to test mechanisms behind firms’ investment, financing, and performance responses.

title: The Housing Boom and Bust: Model Meets Evidence
author: Greg Kaplan, Kurt Mitman, Giovanni L. Violante
context: This result comes from the paper’s calibrated structural incomplete-markets overlapping-generations model of U.S. households over the 1997–2015 housing boom and bust. The paper evaluates a counterfactual principal-reduction (debt forgiveness) program implemented two years into the bust and covering over one-quarter of homeowners with mortgages. The unit of analysis is households interacting with competitive rental, construction, and mortgage intermediation sectors; the outcome studied is foreclosures.

title: The Housing Boom and Bust: Model Meets Evidence
author: Greg Kaplan, Kurt Mitman, Giovanni L. Violante
context: This triplet is analyzed within the paper’s calibrated structural incomplete-markets OLG model of the U.S. housing boom and bust (roughly 1997–2015), with households interacting with competitive rental, construction, and mortgage intermediation markets and features like long-term defaultable mortgages and HELOCs. The authors simulate a counterfactual large-scale mortgage principal forgiveness program implemented two years into the bust and affecting over one-quarter of homeowners to assess its effect on house prices (and foreclosures).

title: The Housing Boom and Bust: Model Meets Evidence
author: Greg Kaplan, Kurt Mitman, Giovanni L. Violante
context: This paper studies the U.S. housing boom and bust around the Great Recession (roughly 1997–2015), combining household-level lifecycle heterogeneous-agent data and aggregate time series. The unit of analysis is households in an overlapping-generations setting interacting with competitive rental, construction, and mortgage intermediation markets. It calibrates a structural incomplete-markets model with long-term defaultable mortgages, HELOCs, rental markets, and shocks to productivity, housing finance (LTV, PTI, origination costs) and beliefs about future housing demand, and evaluates counterfactuals including large-scale mortgage principal forgiveness.

title: Equilibrium Provider Networks: Bargaining and Exclusion in Health Care Markets
author: Kate Ho, Robin S. Lee
context: This paper studies insurer–hospital bargaining and the effects of narrow hospital networks in commercial health insurance using detailed CalPERS enrollment, claims, and admissions data from 2004 and the Blue Shield HMO’s 2005 network proposal in California. Units include individual enrollees, hospitals, and insurers across twelve California health service areas; institutional features include Knox‑Keene/DMHC network adequacy review, community rating and limited patient cost‑sharing, negotiated per‑admission hospital reimbursement, and employer‑level premium bargaining. The authors combine structural demand/cost estimates with a novel bargaining model to simulate counterfactual networks and welfare.

title: Tax Evasion and Inequality
author: Annette Alstadsæter, Niels Johannesen, Gabriel Zucman
context: This triplet concerns Norwegian households (tax units) who voluntarily disclosed previously hidden wealth under a tax amnesty; the analysis uses matched administrative wealth and amnesty records and an event-study design to compare taxpayers’ legal tax-avoidance behavior before and after the voluntary reduction of evasion. Institutional setting: Norway’s tax amnesty and Norwegian income/wealth tax regime.
identification_methods: event studies; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Personalized Pricing and Consumer Welfare
author: Jean-Pierre Dubé, Sanjog Misra
context: This paper studies welfare effects of personalized pricing using two large randomized pricing field experiments run with ZipRecruiter in 2015 (Aug–Sept and Oct–Nov). Units are prospective new employers (primarily small “starter” firms) encountering a paywall for a monthly subscription on the ZipRecruiter platform in the U.S. (with some Canadian observations). The authors combine randomized price assignment with rich self-reported registration features (133 dummies), estimate heterogeneous logit demand, and evaluate firm profits and consumer surplus in the context of data-privacy policy debates (GDPR/CCPA).

title: Multi-generational Impacts of Childhood Access to the Safety Net: Early Life Exposure to Medicaid and the Next Generation’s Health
author: Chloe N. East, Sarah Miller, Marianne Page, Laura R. Wherry
context: This paper studies U.S. cohorts born 1975–1988 (first generation) and their children born 1990–2017 (second generation), exploiting 1980s state-level expansions in prenatal Medicaid eligibility. title: How Segregated is Urban Consumption?
author: Donald R. Davis, Jonathan I. Dingel, Joan Monras, Eduardo Morales
context: Using Yelp reviews in New York City (roughly 2005–2011; ~18,000 reviews by 440 users) combined with 2010 Census tract demographics, Google Maps travel times, and 2007–2011 NYPD robbery data, the authors estimate a discrete-choice demand model of individual restaurant choices. For this triplet they use that estimated model to simulate counterfactual changes in transportation policy (major changes in transit/infrastructure) and predict effects on consumption dissimilarity across demographic groups.

title: General Equilibrium Effects of (Improving) Public Employment Programs: Experimental Evidence from India
author: Karthik Muralidharan, Paul Niehaus, Sandip Sukhtankar
context: This paper evaluates the causal impact of improving implementation of India’s National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (NREGS) by randomizing the 2010–2012 staggered rollout of biometric “Smartcard” payment reforms across sub-districts (mandals) in rural Andhra Pradesh. It uses an RCT at mandal-scale with household surveys of ~5,000 NREGS-registered jobcard holders and independent government censuses (SECC, Economic and Livestock Censuses) to measure effects on wages, employment, incomes, prices, and poverty in imperfectly competitive local labor markets.

title: Flood Risk Belief Heterogeneity and Coastal Home Price Dynamics: Going Under Water?
author: Laura A. Bakkensen, Lint Barrage
context: This paper studies coastal U.S. housing markets, combining a theoretical heterogeneous-beliefs housing model with empirical evidence from a door-to-door household survey (n=187) conducted in Rhode Island in Feb–July 2017 and property-level LiDAR/STORMTOOLS inundation estimates. Units are households/homeowners and individual properties; analysis contrasts coastal (within 400 feet) and non-coastal residents, links belief heterogeneity about 10-year flood risk to sorting, willingness-to-pay, and price dynamics, and calibrates structural simulations using sea-level-rise projections and US flood-insurance (NFIP/FEMA) institutions.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

title: Diagnostic Expectations and Stock Returns
author: Pedro Bordalo, Nicola Gennaioli, Rafael La Porta, Andrei Shleifer
context: This paper studies U.S. publicly traded firms from the early 1980s through 2016, using IBES analysts’ long-term (3–5 year) earnings growth forecasts merged with CRSP stock returns and COMPUSTAT accounting data. The unit of analysis is individual firms listed on NYSE/AMEX/NASDAQ; authors form annual December LTG decile portfolios and analyze earnings histories, forecast revisions, earnings-announcement abnormal returns, and subsequent one-year stock returns to evaluate a representativeness-based learning mechanism driving forecast errors and predictable returns in U.S. equity markets.

title: Children, Time Allocation and Consumption Insurance
author: Richard Blundell, Luigi Pistaferri, Itay Saporta-Eksten
treatment: Unconditional subsidy to households with young children
context: This paper studies married U.S. households using PSID (1999–2015), ATUS (2003–2015) and CEX data to estimate a life-cycle structural model of consumption, spouses' wages and time allocation to work, leisure and childcare. Units are married couples (wives aged 25–65), distinguishing families with young children (≤10). title: Bid Shading and Bidder Surplus in the U.S. Treasury Auction System
author: Ali Hortaçsu, Jakub Kastl, Allen Zhang
context: This paper studies 975 U.S. Treasury uniform-price auctions for bills and notes (4-week to 10-year) held between July 2009 and October 2013. The unit of observation is individual price-quantity bids and bidders (primary dealers, direct bidders, indirect bidders) within each auction. The institutional setting is sealed-bid uniform-price Treasury issuance, where primary dealers route and often observe indirect bids, creating information asymmetries. Using bid-level data and a structural Wilson-style model, the authors recover marginal valuations, quantify bid-shading (market power), bidder surplus, and efficiency losses.

title: The Impact of Bank Credit on Labor Reallocation and Aggregate Industry Productivity
author: John (Jianqiu) Bai, Daniel Carvalho, Gordon M. Phillips
context: This paper examines how U.S. state banking deregulation (intrastate and interstate branching reforms) between 1977–1993—with a deregulation wave in the mid-1980s—affected allocation of bank credit, employment, and capital across small manufacturing firms. Using plant- and firm-level data (LBD, Census of Manufacturers, QFR), the authors study single‑plant firms across states and industries, contrasting firm age groups (especially young firms, age 1–5) and linking prior total factor productivity to post-deregulation changes in bank-debt shares and labor/capital growth.

title: The Impact of Bank Credit on Labor Reallocation and Aggregate Industry Productivity

Using plant- and firm-level data (LBD, Census of Manufacturers, QFR) for single‑plant manufacturing firms across states and industries, the authors focus on young firms (roughly age 1–5) and test whether deregulation caused a convergence (reduction) in the labor marginal revenue product gap between high‑ and low‑productivity young firms.

title: Food Deserts and the Causes of Nutritional Inequality
author: Hunt Allcott, Rebecca Diamond, Jean-Pierre Dubé, Jessie Handbury, Ilya Rahkovsky, and Molly Schnell
context: The paper studies causes of nutritional inequality in the United States using 2004–2016 household and store scanner data. The setting is U.S. grocery markets and policy debates about “food deserts” and SNAP; analyses combine panel event studies, within-location comparisons, and a structural demand model to decompose supply versus demand drivers.

triplet_key: 24133|directed entry of a new public nursing home|entrant fixed-cost recovery
title: Incentivizing Better Quality of Care: The Role of Medicaid and Competition in the Nursing Home Industry
author: Martin B. Hackmann
treatment: Directed entry of a new public nursing home
context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s nursing home industry using resident-level MDS admissions and facility surveys and cost reports, primarily for 2000–2002 (with robustness to earlier years). Units are nursing homes and newly admitted residents; outcomes are skilled nurse staffing per resident and private/Medicaid pricing. The institutional setting is Pennsylvania’s cost-based, peer-group Medicaid reimbursement rule (rates set from 3–5 year lagged peer costs across counties), high Medicaid coverage among residents, and variation in local market structure (county/MSA competition, no CON law). The analysis evaluates causal effects of Medicaid rates and entry on staffing and welfare.

title: The Death of a Regulator: Strict Supervision, Bank Lending, and Business Activity
author: João Granja, Christian Leuz
context: This paper studies the effects of the July 21, 2011 abolition of the U.S. Office of Thrift Supervision under Dodd‑Frank Title III, which transferred supervision of 708 thrifts to the OCC and FDIC. Using U.S. bank-level and county-level data (2005–2015) on CRA small‑business originations, call reports, enforcement actions, and board/executive changes, the authors compare formerly OTS‑supervised thrifts to commercial banks in the same counties to identify how a permanent shift to stricter supervision affected provisioning, management reforms, and small‑business lending.

title: Should Retail Investors' Leverage Be Limited?
author: Rawley Z. Heimer, Alp Simsek
context: This study evaluates the January–October 2010 regulatory episode when the CFTC capped leverage for U.S. retail forex traders (50:1 for major pairs, 20:1 for others). Using account-level trade data (2009–2012) for U.S. and European retail forex traders, monthly panel data on brokerages’ capital, and high-frequency interbank quotes, it compares U.S. (treated) versus European (control) traders, brokerages, and execution prices to estimate causal effects of the leverage constraint on trading volume, returns, broker capital, and spreads.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evidence from San Francisco
author: Rebecca Diamond, Timothy McQuade, Franklin Qian
context: This paper exploits San Francisco’s 1994 ballot initiative that extended rent control to small (≤4‑unit) multifamily buildings built before 1980. Using address‑level panel data on nearly all adults (Infutor), DataQuick parcel records, permits and condo conversions, the authors track renters and landlords from the early 1990s through 2016 and analyze impacts mainly during 1995–2012. The unit of analysis includes individual tenants, parcels/landlords and neighborhoods (zipcodes); institutional details include within‑tenancy rent caps, eviction rules and condo/TIC conversion channels.

title: The Fragility of Market Risk Insurance
author: Ralph Koijen, Motohiro Yogo
context: This triplet is based on an insurer-level cross-sectional analysis of U.S. variable annuity firms, relating the change in reserve valuation (from annual financial statements) between 2007 and 2010 to subsequent variable annuity sales growth. It focuses on how shocks to reserve valuations during the 2007–2009 global financial crisis—within the institutional setting of risk-based capital regulation, reinsurance, and imperfect hedging of long-maturity guarantees—are associated with insurers’ sales responses.

triplet_key: 24267|in-kind provision of home care|recipient valuation of transfer
title: Targeting with In-kind Transfers: Evidence from Medicaid Home Care
author: Ethan M.J. Lieber, Lee M. Lockwood
context: This paper studies Medicaid home care in the United States using randomized Cash and Counseling experiments in Arkansas, Florida, and New Jersey (beginning 1998) and national NLTCS (1999) data. Units are elderly, Medicaid home-care-eligible individuals (65+) with two or more ADL limitations. The study compares traditional in-kind formal home-care benefits—care-plan determined, Medicaid-covered formal care—versus near-cash flexible benefits (including payments for informal care), exploiting randomized assignment and plan-based prices to estimate effects on formal care consumption, take-up, targeting, and welfare.
