title: Stress Tests and Small Business Lending
author: Kristle Cortés, Yuliya Demyanyk, Lei Li, Elena Loutskina, Philip E. Strahan
context: This paper studies how Federal Reserve stress tests (CCAR/DFAST) conducted 2012–2016 affected small-business lending by 32 large U.S. bank holding companies and their subsidiary banks. Using subsidiary-bank–county-year CRA loan originations (2012–2015) and micro loan records from the Survey of Terms of Business Lending (Q1 2013–Q4 2016), it links each BHC’s modeled decline in capital under the severely adverse stress scenario to changes in small-business loan quantities and prices across U.S. counties and states, exploiting local employment risk and branch presence.

title: Measuring Ex-Ante Welfare in Insurance Markets
author: Nathaniel Hendren
context: This paper develops and applies tools to measure ex-ante expected-utility welfare of insurance subsidies and mandates using reduced-form willingness-to-pay and cost curves from the Massachusetts subsidized health exchange (Commonwealth Care). It focuses on low-income adults at about 150% of the federal poverty level during the 2011 plan year (open enrollment fall 2010), exploiting subsidy-schedule discontinuities to identify demand and cost functions. The institutional setting includes subsidies, mandates/penalties, moral hazard, adverse selection, and uncompensated-care externalities affecting insurer costs.

triplet_key: 24478|profit subsidy to small cities|spatial disparities (gini index)
title: Firm Sorting and Agglomeration
author: Cecile Gaubert
context: This triplet refers to a model-based general-equilibrium counterfactual simulated on the structural model estimated with French firm-level data (primarily year 2000) across 314 commuting zones. The policy is an ad-valorem profit subsidy to firms locating in the smallest cities covering 2.3% of population (calibrated to France’s ZFU), set at about 12% of firms' profits and financed by a lump-sum tax; effects are computed in the long-run spatial equilibrium and summarized by the Gini index of GDP per capita across cities.

title: Sweat Equity in U.S. Private Business
author: Anmol Bhandari, Ellen R. McGrattan
context: The paper studies U.S. private businesses using national accounts, IRS microdata, the 2007 Survey of Business Owners, and a sample of 6,855 brokered private-business sales (1994–2017). Units of observation are private business owners and firms (pass-throughs and privately held C corporations). The authors calibrate a dynamic lifecycle model to 2007 moments, use it to value transferable and nontransferable “sweat” capital, and simulate tax-policy counterfactuals (including reforms comparable to the 2017 corporate tax cut) in the U.S. tax and institutional setting.

triplet_key: 24560|downward nominal wage rigidity|welfare gains from commodity super-cycle
title: The Transmission of Commodity Price Super-Cycles
author: Felipe Benguria, Felipe Saffie, Sergio Urzúa
context: This causal estimate comes from the paper’s calibrated dynamic two‑region quantitative model (heterogeneous firms and workers) calibrated to Brazil’s 1999–2013 experience (using price paths for São Paulo and Minas Gerais). The authors impose downward nominal wage rigidity (Schmitt‑Grohé & Uribe style) under a managed‑float exchange‑rate rule and simulate a commodity super‑cycle, comparing aggregate welfare (economy‑level welfare gains, unemployment, and real GDP) under rigidity versus a fully flexible labor‑market counterfactual.

title: Unions and Inequality Over the Twentieth Century: New Evidence from Survey Data
author: Henry S. Farber, Daniel Herbst, Ilyana Kuziemko, Suresh Naidu
context: This paper studies the long-run causal impact of unions on U.S. income inequality from 1936 through the late twentieth century (and into recent decades), using household-level survey microdata (primarily Gallup, ANES, CPS) and state-year aggregates across U.S. states. It exploits mid-century institutional shocks (the 1935 Wagner Act, the 1937 Supreme Court decision, and WWII-era War Labor Board default-enrollment on defense contracts), cross-state variation in union density over time, and household union membership to estimate effects on family income and distributional measures.

The researchers randomized provision of accurate signals about average manager or peer base salaries and tracked behavioral and survey outcomes (with pre-treatment administrative data and ~90-day post-treatment follow-up).

title: Inputs, Incentives, and Complementarities in Education: Experimental Evidence from Tanzania
author: Isaac Mbiti, Karthik Muralidharan, Mauricio Romero, Youdi Schipper, Constantine Manda, Rakesh Rajani
context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized controlled trial in Tanzania (2013–2014) across a nationally representative sample of 350 public primary schools in 10 districts, covering over 120,000 students in grades 1–3. title: Do Neighborhoods Affect Credit Market Decisions of Low-Income Borrowers? Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment
author: Sarah Miller, Cindy K. Soo
context: This triplet estimates the impact of using the MTO Experimental voucher (a voucher requiring a move to census tracts with <10% poverty plus relocation counseling) on adult credit scores for individuals who were under age 13 at random assignment (1994–1998). title: Do Neighborhoods Affect Credit Market Decisions of Low-Income Borrowers? Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment
author: Sarah Miller, Cindy K. Soo
context: This paper studies low-income families randomized in the U.S. Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment (random assignment 1994–1998) across five cities (Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, New York), linking HUD participant records to annual Experian credit reports (2001–2017) and Clarity payday-loan data. Units are MTO households, adult participants, and children who age into credit markets. Treatments compare Experimental vouchers (required move to <10% poverty tracts with counseling), unrestricted Section 8 vouchers, and control public-housing residents, exploiting random assignment to identify neighborhood effects on credit outcomes.

title: Do Neighborhoods Affect Credit Market Decisions of Low-Income Borrowers? Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment

Voucher take-up is instrumented by randomized offer (2SLS) to estimate treatment-on-the-treated effects.

title: Do Neighborhoods Affect Credit Market Decisions of Low-Income Borrowers? Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment

title: Do Neighborhoods Affect Credit Market Decisions of Low-Income Borrowers? Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment

context: This paper analyzes the effects of macroprudential foreign-exchange (FX) regulations on bank and corporate FX borrowing across 48 countries (excluding reserve issuers) over 1995–2014. title: The Causal Impact of Removing Children from Abusive and Neglectful Homes
author: Anthony Bald, Eric Chyn, Justine S. Hastings, Margarita Machelett
context: This analysis uses Rhode Island DCYF administrative records (investigations 2000–2015, joined to school records 2003–2016 and criminal records through 2017) and focuses on children investigated at age six or older. Identification exploits quasi-random assignment to Child Protective Investigators and investigator-level removal-tendency instruments to estimate LATEs in the Rhode Island CPS/foster-care setting.

title: The Causal Impact of Removing Children from Abusive and Neglectful Homes
author: Anthony Bald, Eric Chyn, Justine S. Hastings, Margarita Machelett
context: This analysis uses Rhode Island administrative records on DCYF child-protective investigations (2000–2015) joined to school records (2003–2016) and criminal records through 2017 to estimate causal effects of removing investigated children. For this specific triplet, the sample is older children investigated at age six or later, and the paper studies later-life outcomes (juvenile delinquency, high-school graduation, likelihood of teen birth, and postsecondary enrollment). Identification exploits quasi-random assignment of cases to CPIs via a rotation list and investigator-level removal tendencies as an IV to estimate LATEs.

title: Do Firms Respond to Gender Pay Gap Transparency?
author: Morten Bennedsen, Elena Simintzi, Margarita Tsoutsoura, Daniel Wolfenzon
context: Using Danish matched employer–employee administrative data over 2003–2008, the paper studies firms and individual employees around a legal threshold (firms with ≥35 employees) after Denmark’s 2006 Act No. 562 requiring firms above the cutoff to produce gender-disaggregated wage statistics (effective 2007). The analysis focuses on firms with 20–50 employees (treated: 35–50; controls: 20–34) to estimate how mandated pay-transparency affected wages, hiring, promotions, productivity, and profits in the private sector.

title: Marginal Jobs and Job Surplus: A Test of the Efficiency of Separations
author: Simon Jäger, Benjamin Schoefer, Josef Zweimüller
context: This triplet studies the REBP unemployment-insurance benefit extension in Austria (1988–1993) using male worker–establishment matches active in 1988 and followed through the repeal. Treatment: region- and age-specific PBD expansion (to 209 weeks) for residents aged 50+ with long prior employment in selected labor-market districts. Outcome: separations into persistent nonemployment (no employment at another employer between 1988 and 1993). Identification compares treated cohorts to slightly younger control cohorts across REBP vs non-REBP regions.

triplet_key: 25581|larger payment amount|concerns about fairness to recipients
title: Paying for Kidneys? A Randomized Survey and Choice Experiment
author: Julio J. Elias, Nicola Lacetera, Mario Macis
context: This paper analyzes responses to a randomized online survey and incentivized donation experiment fielded in the United States in Nov–Dec 2017 with 2,666 adult respondents matched to US demographics. Respondents evaluated hypothetical paid-kidney-donor procurement and allocation systems (varying payer: public agency vs. recipient; payment type: cash vs. noncash; amount: $30,000 vs. $100,000) across five assumed transplant-supply scenarios, set against the US kidney shortage and the 1984 National Organ Transplant Act prohibiting donor compensation.

title: The Return to Protectionism
author: Pablo D. Fajgelbaum, Pinelopi K. Goldberg, Patrick J. Kennedy, Amit K. Khandelwal
context: Using monthly U.S. trade data from 2017:01–2019:04 (focusing on the 2018 tariff waves), the paper studies ad‑valorem U.S. import tariff increases and subsequent retaliatory tariffs by partners (China, Canada, Mexico, EU, Russia, Turkey). Identification leverages rapid, product‑origin (HS‑10 country‑product “variety”) tariff changes across products and months. Units of analysis include country–product varieties, HS‑10 products, NAICS‑4 sectors, and U.S. counties. The setting is short‑run: tariffs implemented within weeks, administrative customs and price indices are used, and GE simulations assume immobile regional labor/capital.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Who’s Paying Attention? Measuring Common Ownership and Its Impact on Managerial Incentives
author: Erik P. Gilje, Todd Gormley, Doron Y. Levit
context: Analyzes U.S. publicly-traded firms, 1980–2012 (primary empirical analyses 1998–2012), using firm-pair observations to quantify how overlapping institutional ownership affects managers’ incentives to internalize inter-firm externalities. Combines 13F institutional holdings with proxy-voting (ISS) to model investor attention, and studies episodes such as index inclusions and the BlackRock–BGI merger (2009), with applications to industries like airlines and banking. The institutional background emphasizes rising index investing, asset-manager consolidation, and heterogeneous investor attention as mechanisms shaping governance and potential competitive effects.

title: Labor Market Power
author: David W. Berger, Kyle F. Herkenhoff, Simon Mongey
context: This paper analyzes U.S. local labor markets using Census microdata (Longitudinal Business Database) spanning 1977–2013 (concentration trends) and firm-level panels through 2011/2014 for estimation and calibration. Markets are defined as commuting-zone × 3-digit NAICS; units are firms (firm×market×year employment and payroll) and aggregated workers. The core empirical strategy exploits state corporate tax rate changes as quasi-exogenous shocks to firm labor demand—focusing on tradeable sectors and C‑corporations—combined with firm×market and year fixed effects to estimate size-dependent employment and wage responses.

title: Borrowing to Save? The Impact of Automatic Enrollment on Debt
author: John Beshears, James J. Choi, David Laibson, Brigitte C. Madrian, William L. Skimmyhorn
treatment: Automatic enrollment into the Thrift Savings Plan
context: This paper studies the causal effect of automatic enrollment into the Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) on employee debt by exploiting the U.S. federal policy that began automatically enrolling civilian U.S. Army hires at a 3% default on August 1, 2010. It compares pre- (Aug 1, 2009–Jul 31, 2010) and post-enrollment (Aug 1, 2010–Jul 31, 2011) hire cohorts of Army civilian employees using Department of Defense payroll/personnel data linked to national credit-bureau reports, analyzing outcomes through 2014–2015 while controlling for calendar time and demographics.

title: The Macroeconomics of the Greek Depression
author: Gabriel Chodorow-Reich, Loukas Karabarbounis, Rohan Kekre
context: This paper studies Greece’s macroeconomic boom (1998–2007) and prolonged depression (2007–2017), using national accounts, firm surveys, bank balance sheets, and financial‑market indicators for households, firms, banks, and the government in a small open‑economy euro‑area setting. It evaluates how external demand shocks, fiscal policy (spending/transfers and tax prepayments), sovereign and idiosyncratic risk, utilization and financial frictions, and international bailouts shaped output, consumption, wages, investment, and sectoral allocation.

title: Consumer-Lending Discrimination in the FinTech Era
author: Robert Bartlett, Adair Morse, Richard Stanton, Nancy Wallace
context: This triplet analyzes U.S. mortgage loan applications (accepted and rejected) over 2009–2015 using HMDA as the primary loan‑level source. For the accept/reject analysis the sample is limited to conventional, conforming applications (used as a proxy for GSE‑eligible loans) and pools applied‑for maturities (15, 20, and 30 years) because HMDA lacks maturity and GSE flags. Units are individual mortgage applications across lenders (FinTech vs. face‑to‑face); the paper models rejection with linear probability specifications controlling for HMDA observables and census‑tract proxies for GSE underwriting variables.

title: Consumer-Lending Discrimination in the FinTech Era
author: Robert Bartlett, Adair Morse, Richard Stanton, Nancy Wallace
context: This paper analyzes U.S. mortgage loan applications and originated 30‑year fixed‑rate GSE‑conforming mortgages from 2009–2015 using linked loan‑level HMDA, ATTOM, McDash, Equifax and GSE data. Units are individual mortgage applications and originated loans (including Latinx and African‑American borrowers), across lenders (FinTech vs. traditional), counties and months. It exploits the Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac LLPA/GSE pricing grid and U.S. fair‑lending legal framework to isolate lender strategic markups and accept/reject decisions while holding observed credit‑risk (credit score, LTV) fixed.

title: Imperfect Competition, Compensating Differentials and Rent Sharing in the U.S. Labor Market
author: Thibaut Lamadon, Magne Mogstad, Bradley Setzler
context: Using the universe of U.S. business and worker tax records for 2001–2015, the paper studies matched employer–employee relationships across commuting-zone × industry markets in the United States. Units are individual workers (W-2/1099) and firms (EINs). The authors exploit variation in firm- and market-level value added, worker mobility, procurement-auction shocks, and shift-share industry shocks to estimate a structural equilibrium model with worker heterogeneity over non-wage amenities, firm productivity, and progressive labor-income taxation (amenities treated as fixed during estimation).
identification_methods: DiD; IV; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Partial Default
author: Cristina Arellano, Xavier Mateos-Planas, José-Víctor Ríos-Rull
context: This effect comes from model-based counterfactuals in the paper’s estimated structural long-term-bond sovereign model (fit to 1970–2019 panel moments for 37 emerging-market sovereigns). Debt relief is implemented by lowering the recovery factor on defaulted debt; welfare is computed from the sovereign’s utility in simulated economies and the paper finds debt relief raises welfare only for high-debt states.

title: Partial Default
author: Cristina Arellano, Xavier Mateos-Planas, José-Víctor Ríos-Rull
context: Using annual data from 1970–2019 for 37 emerging-market sovereigns, the paper documents patterns of partial sovereign default and uses country-year observations of public debt service, arrears, debt levels (World Bank IDS/DRS) and EMBI+ spreads to study default episodes. The setting is emerging-market sovereign debt markets with long-term bonds, restructuring and accumulation of arrears. The authors build a structural long-term-bond sovereign model with partial default, estimate parameters by minimum-distance matching to panel moments, and run counterfactual policy experiments (pari passu, no-dilution, debt relief).

title: Medicaid and Mortality: New Evidence from Linked Survey and Administrative Data
author: Sarah Miller, Norman Johnson, Laura R. Wherry,
context: This paper studies the causal effect of the ACA Medicaid eligibility expansions (primarily 2014, staggered 2014–2017) on mortality in the United States by linking 2008–2013 American Community Survey respondents (panel tracked 2008–2017) to administrative Medicaid enrollment (CMS) and death records (Census Numident and MDAC). The unit is near-elderly low‑socioeconomic adults—primarily individuals ages 55–64 in 2014 who were citizens with family income ≤138% FPL or less than high‑school education—comparing residents of expansion versus non‑expansion states under the optional ACA expansion.

The structural counterfactuals evaluate alternative policies (including an unconditional cash transfer) and their effects on informal village risk-sharing and welfare.

title: Income Growth and the Distributional Effects of Urban Spatial Sorting
author: Victor Couture, Cecile Gaubert, Jessie Handbury, Erik Hurst
context: Analyzes U.S. urban neighborhood change from 1990–2014 across the 100 largest CBSAs using Census/ACS tract-level household incomes, Zillow housing costs, and smartphone venue-visit data. Units are households by income bracket and census-tract neighborhoods classified as downtown vs. suburban and high/low quality. The paper studies how rising top incomes reshape within-city sorting, house prices, and amenity supply, exploiting cross-CBSA variation in income distribution and industry-level national wage shocks (Bartik shift-share) within a model of non-homothetic preferences and endogenous neighborhood development.

title: A Unified Welfare Analysis of Government Policies
author: Nathaniel Hendren, Ben Sprung-Keyser
context: This paper synthesizes causal evidence on 133 historical U.S. policy changes over roughly the past half-century (1970s–2010s) across social insurance, education and child health, taxes and cash transfers, and in‑kind programs. The unit of analysis is policies’ impacts on individuals and households (with emphasis on children) and attendant long‑run fiscal consequences; the authors combine published administrative and research estimates, program eligibility/institutional details, and long‑run budget accounting to compute recipients’ willingness‑to‑pay and each policy’s Marginal Value of Public Funds.

title: The Effects of Foreign Multinationals on Workers and Firms in the United States
author: Bradley Setzler, Felix Tintelnot
context: This paper studies the effects of foreign-owned multinationals on U.S. workers and local firms using linked employer–employee tax records (W-2 and corporate tax filings including Form 5472) for 1999–2017, with main direct-effect estimates focused on 2010–2015. The setting is U.S. commuting zones across industries; units are individual workers (moves between firms) and firms (value added, employment, wage bill). It exploits country-of-origin spatial clustering and worker movers to identify causal direct and indirect wage and productivity spillovers of foreign multinationals.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

title: Diagnosing Physician Error: A Machine Learning Approach to Low-Value Health Care
author: Sendhil Mullainathan, Ziad Obermeyer
context: Using 2010–2015 EHR data from a large urban academic hospital (246,265 emergency department visits) and a national Medicare replication (2009–2013), the paper studies physicians’ decisions to test for acute coronary syndromes. The unit is ED visits/patients in U.S. hospital emergency departments where testing (ECG, troponin, stress test, catheterization) can lead to stenting/CABG. Key institutional features include triage shifts (exploited as shift-to-shift variation), clinical risk thresholds (e.g., HEART/TIMI), and cost–benefit trade-offs of invasive testing.

title: Dancing With Activists
author: Lucian A. Bebchuk, Alon Brav, Wei Jiang, Thomas Keusch
context: This paper studies 3,012 hedge‑fund activism campaigns targeting U.S. publicly listed firms (NYSE/NASDAQ/AMEX) initiated between 2000 and 2013, with settlement outcomes followed through 2018. The unit of analysis is activist–firm campaigns and target firms; the institutional setting includes U.S. corporate governance (Schedule 13(d) disclosures, proxy contests, AGMs) and settlement agreements (standstills and board‑composition provisions). The paper analyzes settlement incidence and timing, settlement terms (board turnover vs direct actions), stock‑market reactions, and post‑settlement effects on boards, CEOs, payouts, M&A, and operating performance.
identification_methods: DiD; PSM; event studies; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Persistent Government Debt and Aggregate Risk Distribution
author: Mariano Max Croce, Thien T. Nguyen, Steve Raymond
context: This paper links time‑varying debt‑to‑GDP persistence to the distribution of aggregate consumption and asset‑return risk using U.S. quarterly macro data (1947:Q2–2016:Q4) and an annual panel of 15 developed countries (1978–2014). The analysis operates at the national aggregate level (debt‑to‑GDP, consumption and GDP growth, consumption‑claim excess returns) within a fiscal policy environment characterized by debt‑management rules, tax dynamics, and stochastic government expenditure; results are complemented by an endogenous‑growth, recursive‑preferences general equilibrium model.

triplet_key: 26191|him+fe program (him plus teacher training)|child cognitive development
title: Preschool Quality and Child Development
author: Alison Andrew, Orazio Attanasio, Raquel Bernal, Lina Cardona Sosa, Sonya Krutikova, Marta Rubio-Codina
context: This paper evaluates a large-scale cluster randomized trial embedded in Colombia’s nationwide Hogares Infantiles Mejorados (HIM) rollout. Randomization occurred in late 2012 with baseline data collected March–May 2013 and an 18-month endline in Oct–Nov 2014 across 120 HIs in eight major Colombian cities. Units are preschools (HIs) and enrolled children (baseline ages 18–36 months; main analysis focuses on children ≥48 months at endline). HIM increased per-child funding—primarily to hire teaching assistants—while a second arm (HIM+FE) added low-cost video-conferenced teacher training.

title: How Do Foreclosures Exacerbate Housing Downturns?
author: Adam M. Guren, Timothy J. McQuade
context: This paper studies the U.S. housing bust during the Great Recession (roughly 2006–2013), using national time series and CBSA-level data (99 of the 100 largest metropolitan areas) from CoreLogic. Units include owner-occupied homes, REO sales, and metropolitan-area aggregates of prices, sales, LTV distributions and foreclosures. The institutional setting features large foreclosure waves, lender capital constraints, mortgage pre-approval rules and foreclosure-related credit flags; the authors combine cross-sectional regressions with a calibrated dynamic search model and counterfactual policy simulations.

title: The Economic Consequences of Bankruptcy Reform
author: Tal Gross, Raymond Kluender, Feng Liu, Matthew J. Notowidigdo, Jialan Wang
context: The paper studies the causal effects of the 2005 U.S. Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act (BAPCPA) — signed April 20, 2005 and implemented Oct 17, 2005 — on bankruptcy filings and consumer credit pricing. Using U.S. administrative bankruptcy dockets (2004–2007, ~86% coverage), national Mintel credit-card offer data, CFPB credit-report panels (credit-score bins), and California hospital discharge records, the units are households/individuals, credit-score segments, and credit-card offers. The institutional setting centers on reforms (means test, higher filing costs) that altered filing incentives and lender default risk perceptions.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other time-series regressions

triplet_key: 26270|equalization of cluster sizes across cities|aggregate number of patents produced
title: The Effect of High-Tech Clusters on the Productivity of Top Inventors
author: Enrico Moretti
treatment: Equalization of cluster sizes across cities
context: Using the COMETS universe of U.S. patents (1971–2007), the paper computes a partial‑equilibrium counterfactual—based on the estimated elasticity of inventor productivity with respect to city×field cluster size from the panel models—where cluster sizes are equalized across the 179 BEA Economic Areas within each research field. The exercise is applied to the 2007 distribution, holds inventor and firm quality constant, and reports the implied change in the aggregate annual number of U.S. patents by field and in total.

title: A Welfare Analysis of Occupational Licensing in U.S. States
author: Morris M. Kleiner, Evan J. Soltas
context: The paper studies occupational licensing across U.S. states using individual-level CPS data from January 2015–December 2018. It exploits interstate variation in the share of workers holding government-issued licenses within Census occupations (state–occupation cells) to estimate effects on workers (wages, hours, education, age) and cell-level employment. The institutional setting is state-level occupational licensing regimes (treated as mandated training/delay costs); analyses combine two-way occupation and state comparisons with structural modeling to assess welfare, incidence, and selection.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: A Welfare Analysis of Occupational Licensing in U.S. States
author: Morris M. Kleiner, Evan J. Soltas
context: The estimate refers to marginal occupations across U.S. states using individual-level CPS data (January 2015–December 2018), exploiting interstate variation in the state–occupation share of government-issued licenses. The paper uses two-way (state and occupation) comparisons together with a structural model to infer welfare — where occupational surplus is defined as the welfare value of trade in an occupation’s labor services — and estimates that licensing reduces occupational surplus by about 12 percent for these marginal occupations.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; others

triplet_key: 26458|borrowing from a nonbank lender|initial interest rate
title: Why Do Firms Borrow Directly from Nonbanks?
author: Sergey Chernenko, Isil Erel, Robert Prilmeier
context: Using hand-collected credit agreements for U.S. publicly traded middle-market firms (annual revenues $10M–$1B) during 2010–2015, the paper studies why these firms borrow directly from nonbank financial intermediaries. The analysis exploits variation across local banking markets (MSA/non-MSA counties) and bank regulator leniency (OCC vs. state supervision) and regulatory guidance that treats cash-flow loans to firms with negative EBITDA as “substandard.” Units are individual loan contracts and firms; loan pricing, covenant structure, and lender identity are analyzed using SEC-filed agreements.

title: Labor in the Boardroom
author: Simon Jäger, Benjamin Schoefer, Jörg Heining
context: This paper exploits a 1994 German reform that abolished the one‑third worker‑representative mandate on supervisory boards for stock corporations incorporated on/after August 10, 1994 (grandfathering older cohorts). Using matched IAB‑Orbis employer–employee data, firm financials, and incorporation cohorts (units: firms and workers) for 1989–1999 entrants, it estimates effects on wages, firm pay premia (AKM), productivity, capital and the labor share within Germany’s two‑tier board and collective‑bargaining institutional context, noting a 500‑employee exemption.
identification_methods: RDD; DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Labor in the Boardroom
author: Simon Jäger, Benjamin Schoefer, Jörg Heining
context: This analysis examines whether board-level codetermination affects firm survival in Germany following the August 10, 1994 reform that abolished the one‑third worker‑representative mandate for newly incorporated stock corporations. For survival, the paper uses the Mannheim Enterprise Panel covering all firm incorporations from 1991 onward and plots survival probabilities for stock corporations incorporated in two‑year windows before versus after the cutoff, comparing them also to LLC cohorts as a placebo. The unit of observation is firms (stock corporations), with survival/exit and bankruptcy analyzed up to twenty years post‑entry in Germany’s two‑tier board institutional setting.
identification_methods: RDD; DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

author: Simon Jäger, Benjamin Schoefer, Jörg Heining

Using matched IAB‑Orbis employer–employee data, BvD firm financials, and incorporation cohorts for entrants 1989–1999, it estimates firm‑level effects (unit: firms) on profitability within Germany’s two‑tier board and collective‑bargaining setting (500‑employee exemption). Profitability measures exclude dividend payouts because most stock corporations in the sample are not publicly traded.

title: Labor in the Boardroom
author: Simon Jäger, Benjamin Schoefer, Jörg Heining
context: This triplet analyzes firms' labor productivity (value added per worker) in the paper's 1994 reform setting: stock corporations incorporated 1989–1999 are compared in two‑year cohorts around the August 10, 1994 cutoff. Value added per worker is measured using Bureau van Dĳk (BvD) value added divided by BvD employment, and the productivity analysis relies on the Orbis‑ADIAB/BvD component (coverage begins in 2006). Units are firms (stock corporations), with LLCs as an additional control group.
identification_methods: RDD; DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Health Insurance and Mortality: Experimental Evidence from Taxpayer Outreach
author: Jacob Goldin, Ithai Z. Lurie, Janet McCubbin
context: This triplet examines the randomized IRS pilot's effect of informational letters (mailed Nov 2016–Jan 2017) on two-year mortality (deaths in 2017–2018) among previously uninsured non-elderly adults ages 45–64 in the United States. Randomization occurred at the household (tax-return) level across ~4.5 million returns (3.9M letters); analysis links monthly Form 1095 coverage records (Jan 2015–Dec 2018) and Social Security death records through Dec 2018, and reports the intent-to-treat difference in mortality between treatment and control.

triplet_key: 26582|public pension investor type|willingness to pay for impact
title: Impact Investing
author: Brad M. Barber, Adair Morse, Ayako Yasuda
context: The study analyzes global venture- and growth-equity fundraising and investor choices over 1995–2014 using Preqin (and UNPRI) data: 4,659 funds (159 manually-identified impact funds) and ~3,500 limited partners. The institutional setting is private VC/growth funds that raise capital at inception with long lock-ups; impact funds explicitly state dual social and financial objectives. Units of analysis are fund-level realized performance (IRR, percentile rank) and investor–fund discrete investment choices across regions (North America, Europe, Emerging Markets) and investor types (pensions, foundations, development orgs, banks, etc.).
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 26591|city productivity and population|strictness of planning regulations
title: Urban Growth and its Aggregate Implications
author: Gilles Duranton, Diego Puga
context: The paper analyzes systems of US metropolitan areas (county-based 1999 definitions, ≈275 cities) using city-, block-group-, household-, trip-, and longitudinal worker-level data spanning roughly mid-20th century to 2010 (with microdata windows such as CPS 1986–2016, ACS 2008–2012, NHTS 2008–09). It studies how human-capital spillovers, commuting and housing costs, and local planning regulations (zoning/restrictiveness) determine city sizes and affect aggregate income and growth in the US institutional setting.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

title: General Equilibrium Effects of Cash Transfers: Experimental Evidence from Kenya
author: Dennis Egger, Johannes Haushofer, Edward Miguel, Paul Niehaus, Michael W. Walker
context: This paper analyzes the local general-equilibrium effects of large one-time cash transfers delivered by GiveDirectly to over 10,500 poor households across 653 villages (84 sublocations) in rural Siaya County, western Kenya. Transfers (≈USD 1,000 per eligible household, mid-2014 to early‑2017) amounted to roughly 15–24% of local GDP during rollout. The study uses household and enterprise censuses (≈65,000 households, 12,095 firms), representative household and enterprise surveys, and monthly market price data to trace impacts on consumption, assets, firm revenue, wages, and prices across households, firms, villages, and markets.
identification_methods: RCT; IV; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

triplet_key: 26733|autonomy (shift authority to procurement officers)|quality of purchases
title: The Allocation of Authority in Organizations: A Field Experiment with Bureaucrats
author: Oriana Bandiera, Michael Carlos Best, Adnan Qadir Khan, Andrea Prat
context: This paper reports a large-scale field experiment in Punjab, Pakistan (pilot 2014–15; main year 2015–16) that randomized roughly 600 procurement officers/offices across 26 districts into autonomy, performance-pay, combined, or control arms. Units are procurement officers and their procurement transactions (≈21,000 purchases of 25 homogeneous goods). The institutional setting is public procurement subject to pre-audit by district Accountant General offices (notably end-of-year budget lapsing and approval delays); treatments altered AG discretion (petty cash, checklist) and/or introduced rank-order bonuses; outcomes include quality-adjusted unit prices, approval delays, and officer time use.
identification_methods: RCT; DiD; IV; other panel regressions

title: Issuance Overpricing of China’s Corporate Debt Securities
author: Yi Ding, Wei Xiong, Jinfan Zhang
context: This paper studies corporate debt issuance in China’s interbank market during 2015–2019, using 18,229 commercial paper and medium-term note issues by 2,558 nonfinancial firms. The institutional setting is auction-based single-price issuance dominated by banks (notably the Big Four) that act as underwriters and investors; central state-owned enterprises are high-value issuers. The authors exploit a natural experiment—the NAFMII ban on underwriter rebates effective Oct 1, 2017—and variation in underwriter competition and self-purchases to study effects on issuance pricing.
identification_methods: DiD; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Reasonable Doubt: Experimental Detection of Job-Level Employment Discrimination
author: Patrick M. Kline, Christopher R. Walters
context: This paper analyzes three large resume correspondence experiments (Bertrand & Mullainathan 2004 in Boston and Chicago; Nunley et al. 2015 on an online U.S. job board for recent college graduates; Arceo-Gómez & Campos‑Vasquez 2014 in Mexico City). The unit of observation is job vacancies (employers); researchers sent randomized fictitious applications with racially- or gender‑distinctive names and measured callbacks. The institutional setting is employer hiring on online/offline job platforms, used to infer job‑level discrimination heterogeneity and to evaluate detection rules for enforcement.

title: Measuring Welfare and Inequality with Incomplete Price Information
author: David Atkin, Benjamin Faber, Thibault Fally, Marco Gonzalez-Navarro
context: Using repeated cross-sections of India’s National Sample Survey, the paper estimates income-specific consumer price indices and welfare changes for rural households across districts between 1987/88 and 1999/2000 and re-evaluates district-level effects of India’s 1991 trade reforms. Units are rural households and districts; the setting features substantial tariff reductions, rapid expansion of services and manufacturing quality/variety, and incomplete price measurement (reliable unit-value prices mainly for foods and fuels). Identification exploits variation in relative expenditure shares, within-G price changes, and cross-district exposure to import competition.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

triplet_key: 26942|food stamps exposure in early childhood|adult economic self-sufficiency
title: Is the Social Safety Net a Long-Term Investment? Large-Scale Evidence from the Food Stamps Program
author: Martha J. Bailey, Hilary W. Hoynes, Maya Rossin-Slater, Reed Walker
treatment: Food Stamps exposure in early childhood
context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of childhood access to the U.S. Food Stamps (SNAP) program that rolled out across counties from 1961–1975. Using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2013 ACS data merged to the SSA NUMIDENT for individuals born 1950–1980 (observed as adults, roughly ages 25–54), the authors exploit county-by-county staggered adoption under the War on Poverty to estimate how in utero and early-childhood exposure (especially conception to age five) affected adult education, earnings, health, incarceration, and neighborhood outcomes across U.S. counties.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

title: Measuring Racial Discrimination in Bail Decisions
author: David Arnold, Will S. Dobbie, Peter Hull
context: This analysis uses 595,186 New York City arraignments (367,434 defendants) from November 1, 2008 to November 1, 2013 and exploits the court rotation calendar that quasi-randomly assigns bail judges. The paper estimates judge-level unwarranted release-rate disparities (the average level of discrimination) from defendant/arraignment outcomes and fits a hierarchical MTE model. Counterfactual simulations impose race-specific release-rate quotas on judges (targeting the most discriminatory judges) to predict reductions in the average level of discrimination, with identification using court-by-time strata and judge quasi-random assignment.

title: Zombie Credit and (Dis-)Inflation: Evidence from Europe
author: Viral V. Acharya, Matteo Crosignani, Tim Eisert, Christian Eufinger
context: This paper studies the rise of “zombie credit” and its effect on inflation using firm-level (≈1.17 million firms) and product‑level CPI data across 65 industries in 12 European countries (Austria, Belgium, Germany, Denmark, Spain, Finland, France, Italy, Poland, Portugal, Sweden, Slovakia) roughly over 2009–2016 with emphasis on the post‑2012 period. The setting is post‑global financial and sovereign‑debt crises under ultra‑accommodative ECB policies (negative rates, asset purchases), where weakly‑capitalized banks and search‑for‑yield behavior provided cheap credit to impaired firms; units are firms and industry‑country “markets.”
identification_methods: IV; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Public Procurement in Law and Practice
author: Erica Bosio, Simeon Djankov, Edward L. Glaeser, Andrei Shleifer
context: Using a 2018–2019 World Bank "Doing Business" procurement module, the paper analyzes procurement laws, actual procurement practices, and outcomes for a hypothetical $2.5 million road-resurfacing contract across 187 countries. Units are countries (responses from lawyers, contractors, and procuring entities). It links cross-country variation in legal rules versus practice to outcomes (perceived corruption, procedural efficiency, cost/time/quality) and examines heterogeneity by public-sector capacity (human capital, government efficiency).

title: Public Procurement in Law and Practice
author: Erica Bosio, Simeon Djankov, Edward L. Glaeser, Andrei Shleifer

triplet_key: 27258|bank capital requirement|monetary policy transmission to lending
title: Bank Market Power and Monetary Policy Transmission: Evidence from a Structural Estimation
author: Yifei Wang, Toni M. Whited, Yufeng Wu, Kairong Xiao
context: Using U.S. commercial bank balance-sheet and branch data (Call Reports and FDIC Summary of Deposits) over 1994–2017, the paper structurally estimates how federal-funds-rate movements transmit through banks to borrowers. The unit of analysis is bank-level deposits, loans, spreads, and market shares in the U.S. market; the model incorporates deposit and loan market power, reserve and capital requirements, maturity mismatch, and costly non-deposit funding in the U.S. regulatory environment.
identification_methods: IV; other cross-sectional regressions; other time-series regressions; others

title: Deadly Debt Crises: COVID-19 in Emerging Markets
author: Cristina Arellano, Yan Bai, Gabriel P. Mihalache
treatment: Debt service suspension with payment reduction
context: This paper studies how sovereign default risk shaped health and economic outcomes during the COVID-19 pandemic in nine Latin American countries. Using weekly data from the pandemic onset (March 2020) through end-2021, the authors build and calibrate a small-open-economy SIR-sovereign-default model where the sovereign (country-level unit) chooses debt, partial default and social-distancing to maximize welfare. The setting emphasizes emerging-market fiscal constraints, international lenders, long-term debt with endogenous default duration, and counterfactual debt-relief programs.

triplet_key: 27283|increase in long-term earnings growth forecast|future stock returns
title: Belief Overreaction and Stock Market Puzzles
author: Pedro Bordalo, Nicola Gennaioli, Rafael La Porta, Andrei Shleifer
treatment: Increase in long-term earnings growth forecast
context: Using U.S. equity market and analyst-forecast data, the paper studies S&P500 and broader U.S. firms from the early 1980s through the mid-2010s (primary regression sample 1981–2015; figures extend to 2020). Units of observation are the aggregate market and individual firms (IBES median analyst forecasts, firm EPS, prices, shares). The analysis exploits long-term earnings-growth (LTG) forecasts, forecast revisions, portfolio deciles, firm fixed effects and common macro/market proxies (spc, cay, SVIX) to link belief overreaction to multi-year earnings surprises and stock-return predictability in the U.S. market.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Old Age Risks, Consumption, and Insurance
author: Richard Blundell, Margherita Borella, Jeanne Commault, Mariacristina De Nardi
context: Using U.S. Health and Retirement Study (HRS) and the CAMS spending supplement for 2001–2013, the paper analyzes households aged 65+ (heads and spouses) to estimate how transitory income and health shocks affect non-durable consumption and out-of-pocket medical spending. The institutional setting is U.S. retirement with Medicare coverage; the analysis exploits biennial panel variation, transitory–permanent shock decomposition, and a structural utility/savings framework to disentangle resource versus marginal-utility channels.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

triplet_key: 27351|consumption tax financing (vs. labor tax)|welfare of future generations
title: Universal Basic Income: A Dynamic Assessment
author: Diego Daruich, Raquel Fernández
context: This paper evaluates the long-run general-equilibrium effects of a universal basic income (UBI) for the United States by estimating and simulating an overlapping-generations life-cycle model calibrated to U.S. microdata (PSID, CDS, NLSY79, SCF, CEX) spanning cohorts and outcomes largely in the late 20th–early 21st century and using 2000-dollar prices. Units are two-adult households/agents. Institutional features include progressive labor and capital taxation, federal student loans, Social Security pensions, and wage/employment shocks (including prolonged out-of-work events). The model embeds intergenerational skill formation, parental transfers, college choice, and savings.
identification_methods: others; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

triplet_key: 27416|federal reserve direct purchases of treasuries|market stress
title: Treasury Inconvenience Yields during the COVID-19 Crisis
author: Zhiguo He, Stefan Nagel, Zhaogang Song
treatment: Federal Reserve direct purchases of Treasuries
context: This paper studies the U.S. Treasury and repo markets during the COVID‑19 crisis (early 2020, with focus on March 9–23, 2020) and contrasts it with the 2007–09 crisis. The analysis uses market‑level transactions and aggregate holdings by primary dealers, hedge funds, mutual funds and foreign investors to explain movements in Treasury yields, Treasury–OIS spreads, and GCF vs. triparty repo spreads. The institutional setting emphasizes dealer intermediation, post‑crisis regulation (the SLR), and Federal Reserve interventions (repo facilities, large Treasury purchases, and a temporary SLR exemption).

triplet_key: 27435|private equity buyout|entry into new product categories
title: Barbarians at the Store? Private Equity, Products, and Consumers
author: Cesare Fracassi, Alessandro Previtero, Albert W. Sheen
context: This paper studies the effects of private-equity buyouts of 236 consumer-goods manufacturers in the United States using monthly Nielsen retail scanner data from 2006–2016, focusing on deals closed 2007–2015. Units of observation include firms, firm-category product lines, and individual UPCs sold in ~43,000 stores across 91 retail chains. The analysis examines product prices, unit sales, new-product introductions, and geographic/chain distribution after buyouts, comparing treated units to closely matched non-treated competitors (including same-store shelf neighbors) to infer causal impacts.

The analysis examines product prices, unit sales, new-product introductions, and geographic/chain distribution after buyouts, comparing treated units to closely matched non-treated competitors (including same-store shelf neighbors) to infer causal impacts.

title: Rationing the Commons
author: Nicholas Ryan, Anant Sudarshan
context: This paper analyzes agricultural groundwater use in Rajasthan, India, using an original household survey of 4,262 farmers (farmer–crop observations) collected April–August 2017 referencing the Rabi 2016–17 season across six subdivisions served by Jaipur Vidyut Vitran Nigam. The institutional setting is state-managed electricity rationing (six hours/day) with heavily subsidized power (≈INR 0.9/kWh) and no groundwater pricing or defined property rights, amid severe aquifer depletion; geological variation in well depth is exploited to identify impacts and simulate Pigouvian reforms.

title: Kicking the Can Down the Road: Government Interventions in the European Banking Sector
author: Viral V. Acharya, Lea Borchert, Maximilian Jager, Sascha Steffen
context: This paper studies eurozone bank-level interventions during the Global Financial Crisis and subsequent sovereign-debt shock, using hand-collected State Aid Register data on interventions from 2007–2009 and balance-sheet and loan-level outcomes through 2012. The unit of analysis is ultimate-parent banks (N=830) and bank–firm loan relationships, across eurozone countries differing in fiscal capacity and access to ECB facilities (including LTRO). The institutional setting includes national government recapitalizations, guarantees, liquidity measures, and EU Commission approvals, with fiscal constraints proxied by revenue/GDP, debt/GDP and maturing debt.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; PSM; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Conflicting Interests and the Effect of Fiduciary Duty — Evidence from Variable Annuities
author: Mark L. Egan, Shan Ge, Johnny Tang
context: This paper analyzes U.S. variable annuity markets from 2005–2020 (focus 2013–2020 for policy analysis), using product-quarter observations (individual annuity share-classes), insurer and broker-level data on commissions, expenses, sales, and FINRA complaints. It exploits the 2015–2017 Department of Labor fiduciary rule proposal/implementation (issued 2016, vacated 2018) and cross-sectional and time-series variation in commissions, expenses, and product availability to estimate how broker incentives and the fiduciary rule affected annuity sales and investor welfare.

title: Mortality Effects and Choice Across Private Health Insurance Plans
author: Jason Abaluck, Mauricio M. Caceres Bravo, Peter Hull, Amanda Starc
context: This paper uses 2006–2011 U.S. Medicare data (main IV sample 2008–2011) covering beneficiaries aged 65+ across counties in 50 states and DC to estimate causal effects of Medicare Advantage (MA) plans on one-year mortality. It exploits county-level variation in plan offerings and exogenous plan terminations—including a 2008 reimbursement shock to PFFS plans—to instrument changes in observational plan mortality. The analysis combines beneficiary-level panel regressions with empirical-Bayes adjusted plan mortality, event-study checks, and plan-level comparisons to assess causal impacts and consumer sorting.
identification_methods: IV; event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

title: The Ends of 27 Big Depressions
author: Martin Ellison, Sang Seok Lee, Kevin Hjortshøj O'Rourke
context: This paper studies 27 national economies during the interwar Great Depression (monthly/quarterly data from 1919–1936, focusing on the 1929–1936 recovery window). Using country-level units and a newly assembled database (>230,000 observations across ~1,500 series plus commodity futures), it examines how departures from the gold standard (suspensions, devaluations, or exchange controls) and associated monetary regime shifts affected inflation expectations, ex ante real interest rates, and economic recovery.

title: Designing Disability Insurance Reforms: Tightening Eligibility Rules or Reducing Benefits
author: Andreas Haller, Stefan Staubli, Josef Zweimüller
context: This paper studies welfare effects of Austrian disability insurance reforms using linked administrative social-security data and the universe of DI applications. It exploits two quasi-experiments: the 2003 pension reform that changed DI benefit formulas (variation 2004–2017) and the 2012–2017 stepwise increase in the relaxed screening age (RSA) from 57 to 60. The unit of analysis is (predominantly male) private-sector workers aged roughly 30–62 (focus on 54–61), observed at quarterly/annual frequency; institutional setting: payroll‑funded DI with RSA-based eligibility rules.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Designing Disability Insurance Reforms: Tightening Eligibility Rules or Reducing Benefits
author: Andreas Haller, Stefan Staubli, Josef Zweimüller
context: This triplet refers to the paper's application of its theoretical welfare framework to the U.S. disability insurance system. Instead of the Austrian administrative quasi-experiments, the authors apply their sufficient-statistics formulas to existing U.S. empirical estimates and conclude that loosening DI eligibility rules (and increasing benefits) would be welfare-increasing in the U.S. context.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Taking It to the Limit: Effects of Increased Student Loan Availability on Attainment, Earnings, and Financial Well-Being
author: Sandra E. Black, Jeffrey T. Denning, Lisa J. Dettling, Sarena Goodman, Lesley J. Turner
context: This paper studies causal effects of federal undergraduate Stafford loan limit increases (legislation that raised annual loan maxima in 2007–2009) on dependent first‑year student borrowers’ enrollment, degree completion, earnings, and credit outcomes. The authors combine Texas administrative higher‑education records linked to Texas UI earnings for cohorts entering 2001–2008 (focusing on pre‑Great Recession entrants) with a national 5% CCP/Equifax credit‑report panel (cohorts 2004–2008), comparing students who borrowed at the statutory first‑year federal limit (constrained) to those who borrowed less and exploiting staggered cohort exposure to higher limits.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 27671|outlawing tracking of individual credit actions|young adults' welfare (decrease)
title: A Quantitative Theory of the Credit Score
author: Satyajit Chatterjee, Dean Corbae, Kyle P. Dempsey, José-Víctor Ríos-Rull
treatment: Outlawing tracking of individual credit actions
context: This paper studies unsecured consumer credit and credit-score dynamics in the United States, using individual- and aggregate-level data from the mid-2000s (e.g., FRBNY CCP/Equifax credit records, 2004 bankruptcy filings, and 2007 SCF). The unit of analysis is individuals/households across the life cycle (primarily ages 20–60). The authors develop a dynamic model of hidden borrower types (patience), map it to U.S. credit-market institutions (credit bureaus, Chapter 7 bankruptcy), estimate parameters by simulated method of moments, and use the calibrated model for counterfactual policy experiments.

title: Understanding Tax Policy: How Do People Reason?
author: Stefanie Stantcheva
context: This paper analyzes two large-scale representative U.S. surveys conducted Feb–May 2019 (n≈2,780 for income tax; n≈2,360 for estate tax) of individuals aged 18–69. It studies knowledge, beliefs, and normative views about U.S. federal income and estate taxation, elicits perceived behavioral and distributional mechanisms, and implements randomized short instructional video treatments to test how pedagogical explanations (Redistribution, Efficiency, Economist) causally shift support for progressive tax policies.

title: Investor Tax Credits and Entrepreneurship: Evidence from U.S. States
author: Matthew R. Denes, Sabrina T. Howell, Filippo Mezzanotti, Xinxin Wang, Ting Xu
context: The paper studies state-level angel investor tax credit programs across 31 U.S. states from 1988–2018, exploiting staggered introductions and terminations. It analyzes state-year outcomes (firm entry, jobs) and deal-, firm-, and investor-level data (Crunchbase, Venture databases, Form D, AngelList) as well as beneficiary vs failed-applicant firms and a survey of angels. The institutional setting is state personal income tax credits for accredited investors (certification required), with variation in credit rates, eligibility restrictions, and program budgets, intended to spur high-tech entrepreneurial entry and job creation.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions; others

title: Monetary Stimulus Amidst the Infrastructure Investment Spree: Evidence from China's Loan-Level Data
author: Kaiji Chen, Haoyu Gao, Patrick C. Higgins, Daniel F. Waggoner, Tao Zha
context: Using confidential loan-level data for 2007–2013 (with focus on the 2009 stimulus), this paper studies China’s banking and investment response to the 2008–09 crisis. The unit of observation is firm-quarter loans (new long-term loans) from 17 large commercial banks to over 160,000 firms across sectors, distinguishing SOEs and non-SOEs. The institutional setting features the People’s Bank of China’s large M2 expansion and the State Council’s 2008–09 four-trillion RMB fiscal package that prioritized infrastructure and involved government guarantees for infrastructure projects.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; other panel regressions; IV

title: Monetary Stimulus Amidst the Infrastructure Investment Spree: Evidence from China's Loan-Level Data
author: Kaiji Chen, Haoyu Gao, Patrick C. Higgins, Daniel F. Waggoner, Tao Zha
context: Using confidential loan-level data for 2007–2013 (focus on the 2009 stimulus), the paper analyzes firm-quarter outcomes in China: newly issued long-term loans from 17 large commercial banks to over 160,000 firms (distinguishing SOEs and non-SOEs). For the private-investment result, the authors merge these bank loans with CSMAR listed-firm data to estimate the elasticity of capital investment to bank credit, then use a back-of-envelope calculation combining that elasticity with the estimated elasticity of bank credit to the 2009 monetary stimulus. "Private investment" refers to non-SOEs; the setting features the PBC's large M2 expansion and the 2008–09 fiscal infrastructure package.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; other panel regressions; IV

title: Crimes Against Morality: Unintended Consequences of Criminalizing Sex Work
author: Lisa Cameron, Jennifer Seager, Manisha Shah
context: This empirical paper exploits an unexpected local policy in Malang district, East Java, Indonesia, where formal sex worksites were criminalized (announcement July 11, 2014; enforcement from Nov 28, 2014). The authors collected baseline (Feb–Mar 2014) and endline (May–Jun 2015; biological tests Sept 2014/Sept 2015) panel data on female sex workers, male clients, and nearby community members across Malang and neighboring Pasuruan and Batu districts, comparing criminalized worksites to non-criminalized controls. The institutional setting contrasts formal versus informal worksites, police raids, and loss of health/condom outreach.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Flexible Wages, Bargaining, and the Gender Gap
author: Barbara Biasi, Heather Sarsons
context: This paper studies Wisconsin public‑school teachers around the June 2011 passage of Act 10, exploiting staggered expirations of pre‑Act collective bargaining agreements across school districts (using data roughly 2006–2016, student test scores through 2017). The unit is individual teachers (linked to schools/districts and principals/superintendents), and the institutional change removed union control over salary schedules and allowed district‑level flexible, individually negotiated pay. The analysis combines administrative payroll, assignment, and value‑added data with a teacher survey to estimate effects on the gender wage gap and bargaining behavior.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; IV; other panel regressions

title: Fintech Borrowers: Lax-Screening or Cream-Skimming?
author: Marco Di Maggio, Vincent Yao
context: Using individual-level monthly credit-bureau data from the U.S. (2012–2017), this study compares unsecured personal loans originated by online fintech firms and traditional lenders. The unit is the loan/borrower (monthly panel spanning 3 months pre- to 15 months post-origination) across U.S. zip codes and states. Fintechs are defined as non-deposit, online-only, recently founded lenders; the institutional background includes rising fintech market share and regulation primarily by state regulators/CFPB. Analyses exploit local time variation and lender switching to infer causal effects on defaults, balances, and pricing.
identification_methods: PSM; event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: The Market Risk Premium for Unsecured Consumer Credit Risk
author: Matthias Fleckenstein, Francis A. Longstaff
context: Analyzes U.S. credit-card asset-backed securities and issuer master trusts over 2000–2020 (monthly observations) for the ten largest card issuers, using tranche prices, portfolio yields, and charge-offs to identify the market risk premium on unsecured consumer credit. Exploits the post-2008 regulatory environment — notably the Jan 1, 2010 FAS 166/167 reconsolidation, Dodd-Frank risk-retention and Basel III capital rules — comparing pre/post-crisis dynamics and cross-sectional issuer variation in balance-sheet capital intensity to infer causal effects.

title: Social Positions and Fairness Views on Inequality
author: Kristoffer B. Hvidberg, Claus Kreiner, Stefanie Stantcheva
context: This paper links a large Feb–Mar 2019 survey of Danish-born individuals (born 1969–1973, aged 45–50) to administrative tax, employment, schooling, and parental records across Denmark. Using detailed income histories and mapped reference groups (cohort, municipality, education, sector, workplace, neighbors, schoolmates), the authors analyze perceptions and fairness views of within-group inequality, exploit life‑events (unemployment, promotions, health shocks) in registers to study changes over time, and implement a preregistered randomized information treatment revealing true social positions.
identification_methods: RCT; event studies; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 28175|universal or partial loan forgiveness|benefits to high earners
title: The Distributional Effects of Student Loan Forgiveness
author: Sylvain Catherine, Constantine Yannelis
context: This paper uses the 2019 Survey of Consumer Finances to analyze U.S. households (individuals aged 22–60) and their federal student loans, projecting repayment under federal programs (FFEL/DL) and Income-Driven Repayment plans (IBR, PAYE, REPAYE) that tie payments to 10–15% of discretionary income above 150% of the federal poverty line and forgive remaining balances after 20–25 years. The authors simulate earnings growth and discount future payments to compute present values and evaluate distributional impacts of universal, capped ($10k/$50k) and expanded-IDR forgiveness policies.

triplet_key: 28197|business credit boom|real gdp per capita recovery
title: Zombies at Large? Corporate Debt Overhang and the Macroeconomy
author: Òscar Jordà, Martin Kornejew, Moritz Schularick, Alan M. Taylor
context: This paper exploits a newly assembled long-run panel of business (corporate) credit and macroeconomic data for 17 advanced economies from the late 19th century through 2019, analyzing country-level business-cycle peaks, recessions and recoveries (outcomes include GDP per capita, investment, consumption, credit and asset prices). It contrasts corporate versus household credit booms and, for 1978–2019, studies how insolvency/restructuring frictions (creditor rights, recovery rates) affect outcomes, using legal-origin variation as an instrument for institutional differences.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions

title: Prep School for Poor Kids: The Long-Run Impacts of Head Start on Human Capital and Economic Self-Sufficiency
author: Martha J. Bailey, Shuqiao Sun, Brenden D. Timpe
context: This study estimates long-run effects of Head Start’s county-level rollout in the United States (1965–1980) on cohorts born 1950–1980, using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2018 ACS data merged to the SSA Numident. The unit is individuals (adults observed at ages 25–54), and identification exploits county Head Start launch dates combined with school-entry age eligibility (ages 3–5 vs age 6+), controlling for county fixed effects and state-by-birth-year trends. The setting features a rapid, idiosyncratic OEO grant roll-out and phased program implementation.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

context: This study estimates long-run effects of Head Start’s county-level rollout in the United States (1965–1980) on cohorts born 1950–1980, using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2018 ACS data merged to the SSA Numident. The unit is individuals (adults observed at ages 25–54), and identification exploits county Head Start launch dates combined with school-entry age eligibility (ages 3–5 vs age 6+), controlling for county fixed effects and state-by-birth-year trends. The setting features a rapid, idiosyncratic OEO grant roll-out and phased program implementation.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; other panel regressions

title: Efficiency and Equity Impacts of Energy Subsidies
author: Robert W. Hahn, Robert D. Metcalfe
context: This paper studies the California Alternate Rates for Energy (CARE) subsidy using a natural field experiment with Southern California Gas (SoCalGas) during 2014–2015 (using monthly smart‑meter data back to 2012) on 70,000+ low‑income households who had lapsed from CARE. Researchers randomized encouragement letters to re‑enroll eligible households and used that variation to instrument CARE take‑up and estimate the price elasticity of residential natural‑gas demand; they then combine estimates with a structural partial‑equilibrium model to evaluate welfare, emissions, and policy counterfactuals across California utilities.
identification_methods: RCT; IV; other panel regressions; others

triplet_key: 28381|size-contingent labor regulation|firm innovation response to demand shocks
title: The Impact of Regulation on Innovation
author: Philippe Aghion, Antonin Bergeaud, John Van Reenen
outcome: Firm innovation response to demand shocks
context: This paper studies French firms using administrative firm panel data (FICUS) matched to patents (PATSTAT) over 1994–2007, exploiting size-contingent labor regulations that sharply increase at 50 employees. Units are firms (manufacturing exporters for dynamic tests). Identification uses discontinuities near the 50-worker threshold and firm-level export demand shocks constructed from product–destination changes (shift-share). The institutional setting is France’s labor law (works councils, firing/consultation requirements) and the authors combine reduced-form regressions with a structural Schumpeterian growth model and calibrated counterfactuals.
identification_methods: RDD; IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Selection on Welfare Gains: Experimental Evidence from Electricity Plan Choice
author: Koichiro Ito, Takanori Ida, Makoto Tanaka
context: This triplet is evaluated using counterfactual simulations based on estimates from the Yokohama randomized field experiment (pre-period 2013; interventions in summer 2014 and winter 2015) with about 970 participating households and hourly smart‑meter data. The mandatory take‑up policy was not implemented experimentally; the paper uses the estimated selection equation and MTE/MTRs from the RCT and survey data to simulate welfare implications, finding a mandate is superior only if its net benefit (including political cost) exceeds $26 per customer per year.

title: The Economic Geography of Global Warming
author: José-Luis Cruz, Esteban Rossi-Hansberg
context: The paper builds a global spatial dynamic assessment model quantified at 1°×1° grid cells using G-Econ, HDI, and other datasets to estimate how temperature changes (historical decades 1990–2005 for damage estimation, natality calibrated back to 1950–1999) affect local fundamental productivity, amenities, and natality. Units are grid-cell locations (population, wages, energy use) worldwide. The model combines empirical panel estimation of temperature damage functions with structural inversion, carbon-cycle calibration and climate downscaling to simulate migration, trade, innovation and policy counterfactuals from 2000 forward (projections to 2400).
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Owner Incentives and Performance in Healthcare: Private Equity Investment in Nursing Homes
author: Atul Gupta, Sabrina T. Howell, Constantine Yannelis, Abhinav Gupta
context: This paper studies the causal effects of private equity (PE) ownership on U.S. for-profit nursing homes and Medicare short-stay patients using facility-level data (2000–2017) and patient-level Medicare/MDS claims (patient first stays 2005–2016). It matches 128 PE buyout deals (2004–2015) to ~1,674 facilities and 4.2 million Medicare patients, exploiting institutional features—Medicare/Medicaid per-diem payments, CMS Five Star ratings, and high government subsidy—to examine outcomes (90-day mortality, spending, staffing, quality) in the U.S. long-term care sector.

title: Global Pricing of Carbon-Transition Risk
author: Patrick Bolton, Marcin Kacperczyk
context: This study analyzes firm-level financial and emissions data for 14,468 publicly listed companies in 77 countries over 2005–2018. It links annual Trucost scope 1–3 emissions and year-to-year emission changes to monthly stock returns (FactSet), exploiting within-country, industry, time, and firm variation. The paper examines how country features—GDP per capita, energy mix, rule of law, domestic vs international climate policy—and the 2015 Paris Agreement (pre/post) influence pricing of carbon-transition risk across regions and sectors.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; event studies

title: Dynamic Bank Expansion: Spatial Growth, Financial Access, and Inequality
author: Yan Ji, Songyuan Teng, Robert Townsend
context: This triplet refers to a model-based result for Thailand (1986–1996). The authors calibrate a spatial equilibrium model defined on GIS local markets (≈1,000+), using micro DID estimates of branch-opening effects to discipline key parameters, and then simulate aggregate outcomes. The simulation evaluates how endogenous branch placement (number of new branches taken from the data, locations chosen within the model) affects cross-market credit-access inequality and finds a downward trend driven by the credit channel.

title: Dynamic Bank Expansion: Spatial Growth, Financial Access, and Inequality
author: Yan Ji, Songyuan Teng, Robert Townsend
context: Using the paper's calibrated spatial equilibrium model, validated to Thailand's 1986–1996 period with GIS-defined local spatial markets and village/household data, the authors simulate counterfactual policies that subsidize branch profits in markets distant from existing branches. The simulations trace effects on local deposit-cash ratios, wealth accumulation, endogenous branch openings, and aggregate income inequality across local markets and heterogeneous households.

triplet_key: 28582|subsidizing household portfolio adjustment costs|overall income inequality
title: Dynamic Bank Expansion: Spatial Growth, Financial Access, and Inequality
author: Yan Ji, Songyuan Teng, Robert Townsend
context: This causal claim comes from the paper’s calibrated and validated spatial-equilibrium structural model of Thailand (validated to the 1986–1996 branching-expansion period). The authors simulate a counterfactual policy that subsidizes household portfolio-adjustment costs for households in markets distant from existing bank branches and report model-implied effects on household- and market-level outcomes, including overall income inequality.

triplet_key: 28596|exposure to immigrant students|academic achievement of us-born students
title: Diversity in Schools: Immigrants and the Educational Performance of U.S. Born Students
author: David N. Figlio, Paola Giuliano, Riccardo Marchingiglio, Umut Özek, Paola Sapienza
context: This paper analyzes Florida K–12 public-school students observed 2002–2011/12 (cohorts born 1994–2002), linking statewide FCAT math and reading scores and disciplinary records to birth certificates. The unit is individual US-born students (grades 3–10), using sibling comparisons and school-grade-year cohorts across Florida districts to estimate effects of cumulative exposure to foreign-born classmates. Institutional context includes Florida public-school testing, free/reduced-price lunch eligibility, and heterogeneous family sorting (e.g., white flight); the authors address selection with within-family controls and an IV based on school-to-school transition probabilities.

triplet_key: 28600|nonprosecution of misdemeanor case|likelihood of new criminal complaint
title: Misdemeanor Prosecution
author: Amanda Y. Agan, Jennifer L. Doleac, Anna Harvey
context: This paper studies causal effects of prosecuting nonviolent misdemeanor complaints in Suffolk County, Massachusetts (including Boston, Chelsea, Revere, Winthrop) using DA office administrative records from 2004–2018 (with criminal histories back to 2000 and follow-up through 2020). The unit is individual nonviolent misdemeanor complaints/defendants arraigned in nine municipal/district courts; treatment is whether the arraigning ADA advances the complaint to prosecution (creating a DCJIS record). The analysis exploits as-if random assignment to ADAs and a later DA policy shift toward presumptive nonprosecution, measuring subsequent criminal complaints over two to six years.

title: Career Spillovers in Internal Labor Markets
author: Nicola Bianchi, Giulia Bovini, Jin Li, Matteo Paradisi, Michael L. Powell
context: This paper studies causal effects of Italy’s 2011 Fornero pension reform (implemented Dec 2011) using matched employer–employee and full pension-contribution records for private-sector firms (10–200 employees) over 2009–2015. Units are firms and individual workers, with focus on workers “close-to-retirement” (would have been eligible by end-2014 under pre-reform rules). The reform unexpectedly raised retirement-eligibility ages with limited grandfathering, creating heterogeneous retirement delays by age, gender, and contribution history that the authors exploit to study impacts on coworkers’ wage growth, promotions, hiring, and layoffs.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Big Data in Finance
author: Itay Goldstein, Chester S. Spatt, Mao Ye
treatment: Tick Size Pilot constraints on routing
context: This introduction synthesizes six empirical studies of U.S. financial markets and firms in the machine-learning/big-data era. The papers analyze regulatory and commercial datasets—FINRA OATS and the SEC Tick Size Pilot, earnings-call transcripts for S&P firms, high-frequency trades and cross-asset microstructure data, Bitly clickstream around macro announcements, and hedge-fund returns—using units such as firms, directors, trades/orders, securities, and funds across mainly 2000s–2010s institutional settings in U.S. equity and Treasury markets and corporate governance contexts.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

triplet_key: 28640|pro-continuation judicial bias|probability of formal employment post-bankruptcy
title: The Labor Effects of Judicial Bias in Bankruptcy
author: Aloisio Araujo, Rafael Ferreira, Spyridon Lagaras, Flavio Moraes, Jacopo Ponticelli, Margarita Tsoutsoura
context: This paper analyzes bankruptcy cases filed in the State of São Paulo, Brazil between June 2005 and December 2015, linking court records to administrative RAIS employer–employee data to follow firms and workers up to five years after filing. It exploits the institutional rule of random assignment of cases across local courts within judicial districts and the 2005 bankruptcy-law framework to construct a leave-one-out court-level pro-continuation bias and estimate causal effects on firm survival, employee continuation, mobility, and wages.

title: The Long-Term Effects of Universal Preschool in Boston
author: Guthrie Gray-Lobe, Parag A. Pathak, Christopher R. Walters
context: This paper studies long-term causal effects of Boston Public Schools' universal K1 preschool on educational and behavioral outcomes for first-time preschool applicants (four-year-olds) who applied between 1997 and 2003 in Boston, MA. Using randomized school-assignment tie-break lotteries within BPS, linked to Massachusetts DESE administrative records and National Student Clearinghouse data with ~20 years of follow-up, the study evaluates impacts on college enrollment, SAT-taking, MCAS scores, high-school graduation, and disciplinary outcomes for a largely low-income, nonwhite urban cohort.

title: The Long-Term Effects of Universal Preschool in Boston
author: Guthrie Gray-Lobe, Parag A. Pathak, Christopher R. Walters
context: This triplet measures the effect of enrollment in Boston Public Schools K1 preschool on on-time and ever high-school graduation for first-time preschool applicants (four-year-olds) who applied in Boston between 1997 and 2003. Graduation outcomes are taken from Massachusetts DESE public-school records and the sample for these estimates is restricted to applicants observed in the state public-school database; results use lottery-induced offers as an instrument and are interpreted with caution due to differential attrition.

title: The Eﬀects of Disclosure and Enforcement on Payday Lending in Texas
author: Jialan Wang, Kathleen Burke
context: This paper analyzes payday loan outcomes using CFPB administrative loan-level data covering July 2011–June 2012 (six months before and after Jan 1, 2012) to evaluate a Texas statewide behaviorally‑motivated disclosure mandate and concurrent Austin and Dallas municipal loan‑size and amortization ordinances. The setting compares loans, stores, and borrowers in Texas to lenders in other states and across Texas cities, exploiting policy timing and enforcement start‑dates (Austin May 1, Dallas June 17, 2012) to study effects on loan volume, renewals, pricing, and compliance.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 28779|allocating all households to optimal single contract|social welfare
title: Should There Be Vertical Choice in Health Insurance Markets?
author: Victoria R. Marone, Adrienne Sabety
treatment: Allocating all households to optimal single contract
context: Analyzes employer-sponsored health insurance for public school employees in Oregon using administrative data from 2008–2013 (≈44,562 households) across 187 school districts. The Oregon Educators Benefit Board set a state master list of plans (Kaiser, Moda, Providence) while districts independently chose menus and employer premium contributions, creating plausibly exogenous variation in available coverage levels and prices. The unit of analysis is the household (employee plus dependents); the paper combines a theoretical model of optimal menu design with structural estimation of demand, utilization (moral hazard), and welfare under regulated, community-rated pricing.
