triplet_key: 28780|adding an early childhood education facilitator|height-for-age z-score (haz)
title: Augmenting State Capacity for Child Development: Experimental Evidence from India
author: Alejandro J. Ganimian, Karthik Muralidharan, Christopher R. Walters
treatment: Adding an early childhood education facilitator
context: This paper reports a large-scale randomized evaluation (Sep 2016–Apr/Jun 2018) in Tamil Nadu, India, studying 320 government anganwadi centers (AWCs) serving children aged roughly 3–6. The Government of Tamil Nadu hired half-time early childhood education (ECE) facilitators for randomly selected centers; outcomes include center time-use, child test scores (math, language, executive function), and anthropometrics. Data come from baseline (Sept–Nov 2016), unannounced classroom observations, AWC endline testing (Mar–Apr 2018), and household follow-up (May–Jun 2018). The intervention was implemented by the state within existing ICDS institutional routines.

title: The Legal Origins of Financial Development: Evidence from the Shanghai Concessions
author: Ross Levine, Chen Lin, Chicheng Ma, Yuchen Xu
treatment: Rendition of Mixed Courts (switch to Chinese civil law)
context: This paper studies how transplanting British common law versus French civil law affected financial development across Western concessions in China, focusing on Shanghai (1845–1936) and additional concessions in Tianjin, Hankou, and Guangzhou. Units are concession- and grid-cell-level observations and firms (banks, qianzhuang, stock companies, and stock returns). Key institutional shocks are the 1869 creation of Mixed Courts (strengthening British/French legal jurisdiction) and the 1926–27 rendition returning courts to Chinese authority; authors exploit these temporal and spatial discontinuities to identify causal effects.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Are High-Interest Loans Predatory? Theory and Evidence from Payday Lending
author: Hunt Allcott, Joshua J. Kim, Dmitry Taubinsky, Jonathan Zinman
context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment run Jan–Mar 2019 in 41 storefronts of a large payday lender in Indiana, surveying 1,205 payday-borrower customers and matching responses to administrative loan records (including the state-wide Veritec database) to study repeat borrowing, present-bias, and demand for commitment. It also analyzes the lender’s nationwide 2017 data and elicits expert beliefs. Indiana’s regulatory environment (interest and loan-size caps, reporting rules) and common two-week loan maturities frame the institutional setting for structural estimation and welfare simulations of policy changes.

title: Are High-Interest Loans Predatory? Theory and Evidence from Payday Lending
author: Hunt Allcott, Joshua J. Kim, Dmitry Taubinsky, Jonathan Zinman
context: This paper uses a randomized field experiment run Jan–Mar 2019 in 41 storefronts of a large payday lender in Indiana, surveying 1,205 payday-borrower customers and matching responses to administrative loan records (including the state-wide Veritec database) to study repeat borrowing, present-bias, and demand for commitment. It also analyzes the lender’s nationwide 2017 data and elicits expert beliefs. Indiana’s regulatory environment (interest and loan-size caps, reporting rules) and common two-week loan maturities frame the institutional setting for structural estimation and welfare simulations of policy changes.

title: Earnings Inequality and the Minimum Wage: Evidence from Brazil
author: Niklas Engbom, Christian Moser
context: This paper studies Brazil’s labor market and wage inequality over 1985–2014 with a focus on 1996–2012, using administrative linked employer–employee data (RAIS) and household surveys (PNAD, PME). Units include male workers aged 18–54, firms, and state-year aggregates. The institutional setting is Brazil’s federal minimum wage, which rose sharply from 1996 to 2012 and applied uniformly across states but varied in effective bindingness by local wage distributions. Analyses combine firm-worker decompositions, cross-state panel regressions, and a structural equilibrium model.

title: Evidence and Lessons on the Health Impacts of Public Health Funding from the Fight against HIV/AIDS
author: Marcus Dillender
context: This estimate examines the effect of Ryan White Title 1 funding on city-level counts of residents living with HIV in 2008—the first year city-level HIV data are available—across large U.S. cities in the AIDS Public Information Data Set. Identification exploits a regression-discontinuity at the 2,000 cumulative AIDS-case cutoff (cases reported by March 31, 1995) that produced a sharp jump in Title 1 funding; a Susceptible-Infected-Removed epidemiological model is used to interpret mechanisms.

title: Achieving Scale Collectively
author: Vittorio Bassi, Raffaela Muoio, Tommaso Porzio, Ritwika Sen, Esau Tugume
context: Using a representative firm survey conducted late 2018–early 2019 in urban and semi‑urban Uganda (Central, Western, Eastern regions), the paper studies roughly 1,115 manufacturing firms—focused on carpentry, metal fabrication and grain milling—collecting machine‑level, worker and production‑step data. It examines how informal spatial clusters and an active inter‑firm rental market for large machines, subject to transportation and time transaction costs, shape mechanization, productivity, and the effectiveness of policies (e.g., machine subsidies) by combining micro regressions with a structural equilibrium model.

title: How Americans Respond to Idiosyncratic and Exogenous Changes in Household Wealth and Unearned Income
author: Mikhail Golosov, Michael Graber, Magne Mogstad, David Novgorodsky
context: Using U.S. administrative data (W‑2G lottery forms linked to W‑2 wage records and 1040 returns) for 1999–2016 and working‑age households (ages 21–64), the paper estimates marginal propensities to earn and consume from idiosyncratic lottery windfalls via event‑study/DiD and IV methods. It then maps these empirical MPE/MPC estimates into a calibrated life‑cycle model with heterogeneous households and non‑linear taxes to obtain informative bounds on policy counterfactuals: for example, a UBI is predicted to reduce total earnings by at least $0.52 per $1 of UBI.

title: Quantifying the High-Frequency Trading Arms Race""
author: Matteo Aquilina, Eric Budish, Peter O'Neill
context: This paper analyzes microsecond‑timestamped exchange message data from the London Stock Exchange for all FTSE‑350 stocks over Aug 17–Oct 16, 2015 (43 trading days), obtained via an FCA Section 165 request. Units are inbound/outbound messages and reconstructed symbol‑day events; the authors identify microsecond “latency‑arbitrage” races, firm (UserID/FirmID) participation, race frequency/duration, and mark‑to‑market profits (primarily using a 10‑second horizon). Institutional setting: continuous electronic LSE limit‑order book with outer‑wall timestamps and fee incentives affecting IOC vs limit order usage.

title: Systemic Discrimination Among Large U.S. Employers
author: Patrick M. Kline, Evan K. Rose, Christopher R. Walters
context: A nationwide correspondence field experiment (conducted in repeated waves around 2019–2021, paused Mar–Aug 2020 for COVID) sent over 83,000 fictitious applications to entry-level job postings across U.S. counties on the web portals of 108 large employers. title: The Lasting Effects of Early Childhood Education on Promoting the Skills and Social Mobility of Disadvantaged African Americans
author: Jorge Luis García, James J. Heckman, Victor Ronda
context: This paper analyzes the randomized Perry Preschool Project that enrolled 123 disadvantaged African-American children born in the 1960s in Ypsilanti, Michigan. The authors follow the original participants through age 54 using surveys and administrative criminal/education records and link to outcomes for their biological children into young adulthood to estimate intergenerational effects on cognition, health, education, crime, employment, and family stability.
identification_methods: RCT; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

title: What Happens When Employers Can No Longer Discriminate in Job Ads?
author: Peter J. Kuhn, Kailing Shen

title: Regulatory Costs of Being Public: Evidence from Bunching Estimation
author: Michael Ewens, Kairong Xiao, Ting Xu
context: This paper analyzes U.S. public firms (and VC‑backed private firms for IPO analysis) from the 1990s through 2018 using 10‑K reported public float to quantify net regulatory costs tied to SEC public‑float thresholds. It exploits institutional eligibility rules for scaled disclosure ($25m), SOX/non‑accelerated filer and Section 404 exemptions ($75m), and Emerging Growth Company benefits under the JOBS Act ($700m) to study firm‑level responses (leverage, payouts) and IPO/going‑private decisions around regulatory cutoffs.
identification_methods: DiD; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: Opportunity Unraveled: Private Information and the Missing Markets for Financing Human Capital
author: Daniel Herbst, Nathaniel Hendren
context: Using the 2012 Beginning Postsecondary Students (BPS) cohort in the United States, followed to 2017, the paper studies first-time college enrollees’ baseline subjective expectations and realized outcomes (degree completion, employment, earnings, loan delinquency). In the institutional setting of federally-backed, largely non-dischargeable student loans and limited private income-share agreements, the authors link survey elicitations, FAFSA and administrative loan records to quantify private information, adverse selection in hypothetical equity and state-contingent contracts, and welfare gains from government subsidies.

title: Community Colleges and Upward Mobility
author: Jack Mountjoy
context: This study analyzes Texas public high‑school cohorts (10th graders in 1998–2002; projected graduates 2000–2004), linking education records (TEA, THECB) and quarterly earnings (Texas Workforce Commission) for individual students. It evaluates causal effects of access to 2‑year community colleges on educational attainment and earnings, exploiting geographic variation in proximity to 2‑year and 4‑year college campuses across Texas commuting zones while controlling for neighborhood, school, cohort, and local labor‑market factors within the U.S. community‑college institutional setting.

title: The Geography of Unemployment
author: Adrien Bilal
context: This paper analyzes French matched employer–employee administrative data (DADS) merged with the French Labor Force Survey for 1997–2007, with robustness checks using the CPS in the United States. Units include individual male workers (age 30–52), establishments/firms and 328 commuting-zone local labor markets. The setting emphasizes housing constraints, search frictions, and place-based policies (e.g., French Zones Franches Urbaines). The author combines panel flow decompositions with structural equilibrium estimation to identify employers’ role in spatial unemployment and to simulate policy counterfactuals.

title: Measuring the Welfare Cost of Asymmetric Information in Consumer Credit Markets
author: Anthony A. DeFusco, Huan Tang, Constantine Yannelis
context: This paper analyzes a randomized pricing experiment run by a large Chinese fintech lending platform during January–March 2018. The unit is ~11,000 pre‑approved individual loan applicants offered 12‑month unsecured installment loans; applicants were randomized to either the platform’s standard terms or substantially reduced borrowing costs (roughly 36% vs 21.5% total). The platform provides internal credit ratings (1–4), bundles loans for investors, and records take‑up, default (charge‑offs), and recovery outcomes to assess welfare losses from asymmetric information.

title: Market Segmentation and Competition in Health Insurance
author: Michael J. Dickstein, Kate Ho, Nathaniel D. Mark
context: This paper empirically analyzes U.S. health‑insurance market segmentation using Oregon all‑payer claims and plan files (2014–2016), comparing small‑group (employer) and individual marketplace enrollment and premiums for households. Units are households and plans; authors exploit “forced switchers” who lose employer coverage, insurer premium and MLR data, and ACA institutional features (guaranteed issue, metal tiers, risk adjustment, risk corridors, reinsurance). They structurally estimate demand and insurer pricing to simulate counterfactual pooling (e.g., ICHRA employer shifts) and validate results using multi‑state premium/MLR data.

title: AI-tocracy
author: Martin Beraja, Andrew Kao, David Y. Yang, Noam Yuchtman

context: Study of facial-recognition AI in China (2013–2020). Identification compares firms receiving public-security versus non-public-security contracts in the same politically sensitive environment using event-study / triple-differences specifications.

title: Local Economic and Political Effects of Trade Deals: Evidence from NAFTA
author: Jiwon Choi, Ilyana Kuziemko, Ebonya L. Washington, Gavin Wright
context: This paper studies local economic and political effects of NAFTA’s tariff reductions around its 1993–1994 passage, using U.S. county and commuting-zone annual data from the mid-1980s through the early 2000s and individual survey/panel data (ANES repeated cross-sections and 1992–1994 panel, PSID). It exploits cross-sectional variation in 1990 industry employment shares, Mexico’s revealed comparative advantage, and 1990 tariff protection to measure county-level NAFTA exposure and estimates impacts on employment, migration, transfer-program take-up, mortality, and voting/party identification.

title: Information versus Investment
author: Stephen J. Terry, Toni M. Whited, Anastasia A. Zakolyukina
context: This paper studies U.S. publicly listed firms (Compustat and Audit Analytics) over 1999–2015, using annual firm-level panels of earnings restatements (revenue-recognition and irregularity cases) and measures of intangible investment (SG&A or R&D). title: Empirical Strategies in Economics: Illuminating the Path from Cause to Effect

title: Indirect Effects of Access to Finance
author: Jing Cai, Adam Szeidl
context: This paper reports a field experiment in Jiangxi province, China, around a new no-collateral SME loan introduced in 2013. The authors randomized monthly loan-officer outreach to 3,173 small and medium retail and service firms clustered in 78 local geographic “markets” (treatment intensity: 0%, 50%, 80%), collected baseline data in summer 2013 and followed firms in 2015, 2016 and a 2020 follow-up. The institutional setting features concentrated local retail markets, pervasive credit constraints, and bank-mediated loan approval with standardized terms.
identification_methods: RCT; DiD; IV; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 29905|20% decrease in social security benefits|human capital investment
title: Estimation of a Life-Cycle Model with Human Capital, Labor Supply and Retirement
author: Xiaodong Fan, Ananth Seshadri, Christopher R. Taber
context: This paper uses U.S. microdata (SIPP panels 1984–2008 and CPS panels 1979–2018) to estimate a structural life-cycle model of consumption, human-capital investment, labor supply and Social Security claiming. Focusing on male high-school graduates (individual-level units, estimation from ages ~22 up to 80), it models U.S. Social Security rules (claiming from age 62, normal retirement 65, using 2004 rules) and simulates counterfactual benefit and tax changes. Estimation employs indirect inference to match life-cycle employment and measured-wage profiles; extensions include health shocks and part-time work.

title: Financial Inclusion, Economic Development, and Inequality: Evidence from Brazil
author: Julia Fonseca, Adrien Matray
context: This analysis studies Brazil’s 2004 “Banks for All” program using administrative employer–employee (RAIS) and bank-branch (ESTBAN) data from 2000–2014, comparing minimum comparable areas (municipalities/AMCs) without federal government bank branches prior to 2004 (eligible/treated) to control cities with pre-existing public-bank presence. The reported effect on firm exit rate refers specifically to formal-sector firms observed in the administrative data and is estimated at the city level (AMCs) around the 2004 branch-expansion reform.

title: Financial Inclusion, Economic Development, and Inequality: Evidence from Brazil
author: Julia Fonseca, Adrien Matray
context: The paper evaluates Brazil’s 2004 ‘Banks for All’ (Banco para Todos) program using administrative employer–employee and bank-branch data from 2000–2014. It compares municipalities (minimum comparable areas) without federal government bank branches prior to 2004 (eligible) to control cities with pre-existing public-bank presence. The analysis links bank-branch entry, deposits and lending to firm entry, employment, wages, and wage inequality across Brazilian cities, exploiting ex-ante eligibility, branch expansion, and rich city-by-industry and worker panels.

title: Dividend Taxes, Firm Growth, and the Allocation of Capital
author: Adrien Matray
context: This paper studies the 2013 French dividend payroll-tax reform that tripled the tax rate on dividends to owner-managers of SARL firms (from 15.5% to 46%). It uses administrative tax and accounting panel data covering the universe of private firms over 2008–2017. The unit of analysis is closely-held firms/owner-managers (SARL treated, SAS controls), exploiting legal-status variation and staggered fiscal-year treatment timing to estimate effects on payouts, retained earnings, capital and labor accumulation, revenues, and exit.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; other panel regressions

title: Investing with the Government: A Field Experiment in China
author: Emanuele Colonnelli, Bo Li, Ernest Liu
context: This study examines preferences and matching in China’s venture capital and private equity (VCPE) market using administrative investment data (2015–2019), a large non-deceptive field experiment run with Zero2IPO in 2019, and a follow-up qualitative survey in 2021. Units are fund managers (General Partners, GPs) and capital providers (Limited Partners, LPs) observed as firms and senior respondents. The institutional setting is China, where central/provincial/local governments and SOEs play major LP roles; the paper studies how government ties affect GP–LP matching, firm preferences, and equilibrium allocation.
identification_methods: RCT; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

triplet_key: 30222|increase in treasury supply|dealers' net treasury position
title: Intermediary Balance Sheets and the Treasury Yield Curve
author: Wenxin Du, Benjamin M. Hébert, Wenhao Li
context: This paper studies the U.S. Treasury market across the pre- and post-Global Financial Crisis era through the March 2020 COVID episode (roughly 2000s–2020). It analyzes maturity-specific Treasury yields, OIS swap rates, cross-currency basis (CIP deviations), and primary dealers’ net Treasury positions (FR2004) as market-level time series and maturity buckets. The institutional setting includes post-GFC regulatory shifts (higher balance-sheet costs, SLR), large increases in Treasury issuance, and Fed policy interventions (asset purchases, swap lines). Dealers are treated as balance-sheet-constrained arbitrageurs.
identification_methods: other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; others

triplet_key: 30373|cash transfers during infancy|federal income tax revenue
title: Investing in Infants: The Lasting Effects of Cash Transfers to New Families
author: Andrew C. Barr, Jonathan Eggleston, Alexander A. Smith
context: This paper studies U.S. children born around January 1 in cohorts from the 1980s and 1990s (recentered birth years 1981–82, 1985–86, 1991–92), leveraging the federal tax code’s January 1 cutoff for dependent status that creates large discontinuities in EITC and dependent-exemption refunds in the child’s first year. Using linked administrative IRS 1040 tax data (selected years) and SSA Numident, plus North Carolina K–12 records, the unit is low-income first‑born children and their families, evaluating long‑run educational and earnings impacts of infancy cash transfers.

title: Investing in Infants: The Lasting Effects of Cash Transfers to New Families
author: Andrew C. Barr, Jonathan Eggleston, Alexander A. Smith
context: This triplet is estimated using North Carolina administrative K–12 records for students born near January 1 in recentered birth years 1993–1998. The analysis focuses on students ever eligible for free and reduced-price lunch (FRL) as a proxy for likely EITC eligibility, restricted to births within 28 days of January 1 (excluding an eight-day donut). Leveraging the January 1 dependent-status cutoff, the paper uses an RD design to estimate the effect of infancy cash-transfer eligibility on test scores, suspensions, and high-school graduation.

title: Investing in Infants: The Lasting Effects of Cash Transfers to New Families
author: Andrew C. Barr, Jonathan Eggleston, Alexander A. Smith
context: Using North Carolina administrative K–12 data, the analysis focuses on students born in recentered birth years 1993–1998 who were ever eligible for free and reduced-price lunch (FRL). It leverages the January 1 dependent-status cutoff to identify likely receipt of additional child-related tax benefits in the child’s first year (cash transfers during infancy) via an RD design. The unit is FRL-eligible K–12 students born within 28 days of January 1 (excluding an eight-day donut); the outcome is the student’s likelihood of suspension in middle or high school.

title: Investing in Infants: The Lasting Effects of Cash Transfers to New Families
author: Andrew C. Barr, Jonathan Eggleston, Alexander A. Smith

triplet_key: 30576|priming corporate responsibility salience|support for corporate bailouts
title: Selfish Corporations
author: Emanuele Colonnelli, Niels Joachim Gormsen, Timothy McQuade
context: This paper analyzes responses from a large nationally representative online survey of 6,727 U.S. adults launched May 5, 2020 (with a one-week follow-up and an additional June 2022 survey) to study how priming about corporate social responsibility (ESG) versus economic stabilization shapes support for corporate bailouts. Units are individual U.S. respondents asked to evaluate bailouts of the top 500 U.S. corporations during the COVID-19-era policy debate; outcomes include stated policy support, perceptions of corporate behavior, and behavioral actions (petitions, emails, donations).

title: A Fiscal Theory of Persistent Inflation
author: Francesco Bianchi, Renato Faccini, Leonardo Melosi
context: This paper studies the U.S. macroeconomy from 1960:Q1 through 2022:Q3, using aggregate quarterly series (GDP, consumption, investment, hours, inflation, real and nominal interest rates, transfers, government consumption, debt/GDP and breakeven inflation). It analyzes episodes including the Great Inflation (1960s–1980s), post‑1990s trends, the Great Recession, and the COVID‑19 recession and stimulus (CARES, ARPA), within the institutional setting of Federal Reserve monetary policy, fiscal transfers and debt maturity to assess funded versus partially unfunded fiscal shocks.

title: A Fiscal Theory of Persistent Inflation

title: The Economics of the Public Option: Evidence from Local Pharmaceutical Markets
author: Juan Pablo Atal, José Ignacio Cuesta, Felipe González, Cristóbal Otero
context: This paper studies the decentralized rollout of municipal public pharmacies in Chile from October 2015 to April 2018 (146 of 344 counties adopted). It analyzes county- and local-market outcomes using drug-level IQVIA data (2014–2018), municipal finance records (2013–2019), avoidable hospitalizations, and an individual-level informational field experiment conducted just before the October 2016 local election. The institutional setting is a highly concentrated private retail and wholesale pharmaceutical sector; public pharmacies are single municipal stores that procure via a public intermediary and target local residents, especially chronic-patient buyers.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; RCT; other panel regressions

title: Predicting and Preventing Gun Violence: An Experimental Evaluation of READI Chicago
author: Monica P. Bhatt, Sara B. Heller, Max Kapustin, Marianne Bertrand, Christopher Blattman
context: This estimate comes from the randomized READI Chicago trial (rolling referrals Aug 2017–Mar 2020, 20-month follow-ups through late 2021) evaluating an 18-month bundled intervention (subsidized supported work, CBT, outreach) in five high-violence Chicago neighborhoods. The triplet-specific effect is for the pre-specified subgroup of individual men referred by outreach workers; randomization occurred at the individual level within the outreach pathway, and the outcome is the standardized serious-violence involvement index measured using Chicago police and administrative records and compared to control compliers in the same pathway.

title: Predicting and Preventing Gun Violence: An Experimental Evaluation of READI Chicago
author: Monica P. Bhatt, Sara B. Heller, Max Kapustin, Marianne Bertrand, Christopher Blattman
context: This paper reports a randomized trial of READI Chicago (N=2,456 men) conducted with rolling referrals from August 2017–March 2020 and 20-month follow-ups through late 2021. It evaluates an 18-month bundled intervention (subsidized supported work, CBT, and outreach referrals) in five high-violence Chicago neighborhoods (e.g., Austin/West Garfield Park, North Lawndale, Englewood). Units are individual men identified by algorithmic, outreach, or re-entry pathways; outcomes come from Chicago police and administrative records.

title: How the 1963 Equal Pay Act and 1964 Civil Rights Act Shaped the Gender Gap in Pay
author: Martha J. Bailey, Thomas E. Helgerman, Bryan A. Stuart
treatment: Equal Pay Act and Title VII
context: Analyzes U.S. labor markets from the 1950s through the 1970s with emphasis on the 1960s, leveraging the passage and implementation of the 1963 Equal Pay Act and 1964 Civil Rights Act. Uses individual-level census and CPS ASEC data (ages 25–64) and aggregated industry-occupation-state-group cells across 21 state groups to compare states with and without pre-existing equal-pay laws and jobs with larger 1960 gender wage gaps. Institutional context includes FLSA coverage, WHD versus EEOC enforcement, and staggered state law variation.
identification_methods: event studies; DiD; IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

author: Martha J. Bailey, Thomas E. Helgerman, Bryan A. Stuart
treatment: Equal Pay Act and Title VII

triplet_key: 31512|information on predicted ssi removal|parental take-up of human capital investments
title: The (Lack of) Anticipatory Effects of the Social Safety Net on Human Capital Investment
author: Manasi Deshpande, Rebecca Dizon-Ross
outcome: Parental take-up of human capital investments
context: This paper reports a nationally drawn randomized controlled trial (Oct 2021–Jan 2022, U.S., with oversamples in Michigan, Wisconsin, and Massachusetts) of about 6,000 parents of 14–17-year-old children receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI). Using SSA administrative data to predict each child’s probability of removal at the age-18 redetermination, the experiment randomized a video giving that prediction and measured parents’ belief updates and take-up of offered human-capital resources (tutoring, job training, math lessons). The institutional setting is the U.S. SSI program, where child and adult eligibility differ and many youth lose benefits at 18.

title: The Impact of Public School Choice: Evidence from Los Angeles' Zones of Choice
author: Christopher Campos, Caitlin Kearns
context: This paper studies Los Angeles Unified School District’s Zones of Choice (ZOC) reform—implemented starting around 2012–2013—using administrative data from roughly 2008–2019. It evaluates effects on high-school students (chiefly 11th graders and graduating cohorts) and market-level school outcomes across ZOC versus non-ZOC neighborhoods in Los Angeles. ZOC combined catchment areas, expanded within-district choice with centralized rank-ordered (immediate-acceptance) assignment and lotteries for oversubscription, thereby changing competitive incentives among attendance-zone schools.

title: Regulatory Arbitrage or Random Errors? Implications of Race Prediction Algorithms in Fair Lending Analysis
author: Daniel Greenwald, Sabrina T. Howell, Cangyuan Li, Emmanuel Yimfor
context: This paper studies how race-prediction algorithms (BISG) shape fair-lending measurement and lender incentives in U.S. small-business credit markets using Lendio loan application data (2017–2019) and Paycheck Protection Program first-draw loans (Apr 2020–Feb 2021). Units are individual applicants/borrowers and lenders. The authors construct an image-based race measure from LinkedIn photos, benchmark it to self-reported PPP race, estimate how BISG errors bias approval gaps and lender-level loan shares, and simulate counterfactual regulatory shifts from BISG to self-identified race under U.S. fair-lending rules.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

triplet_key: 31646|regulation using self-identified race|share of loans to black borrowers
title: Regulatory Arbitrage or Random Errors? Implications of Race Prediction Algorithms in Fair Lending Analysis
author: Daniel Greenwald, Sabrina T. Howell, Cangyuan Li, Emmanuel Yimfor
outcome: Share of loans to Black borrowers
context: This triplet comes from a simulated counterfactual in which regulators switch from BISG-based evaluations to regulation using self-identified race — operationalized in the paper by using image-based race (from LinkedIn photos) as a proxy for actual race. The exercise uses U.S. small-business lending data (Lendio applications, 2017–2019, and PPP first-draw loans, Apr 2020–Feb 2021) at the applicant/borrower and lender level and finds higher shares of loans to actual Black borrowers but a reallocation toward more affluent, better-educated areas.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; others

title: The Long-Run Effects of America's Largest Residential Racial Desegregation Program: Gautreaux
author: Eric Chyn, Robert Collinson, Danielle H. Sandler
context: This paper analyzes long-run causal effects of the Gautreaux Assisted Housing Program (program operated 1976–1998; main sample 1982–1994) in the Chicago metropolitan area, linking historical placement records to administrative data through 2019/2020. Units are Black public-housing/voucher recipient households and their children. The program—created by a class-action settlement and a 1981 consent decree—placed families into “General” (<30% Black) or “Revitalizing” tracts based largely on registration order and unit availability. Authors use linked LEHD, MAF-ARF, Decennial Census and Numident data to estimate placement effects.

title: Is Inequality Harmful for Growth? Theory and Evidence
author: Torsten Persson, Guido Tabellini
context: Combining a politico‑economic growth model with empirical tests, the paper examines whether initial income inequality reduces subsequent per‑capita GDP growth. It uses two complementary data sets: a historical panel of nine now‑developed countries divided into 20‑year episodes covering primarily 1830–1970, and a post‑war cross‑section of about 67 countries over 1960–85. Units are country (or country‑period) observations; key covariates are pre‑period inequality (top‑20% share or top20/bottom40 ratio), schooling, franchise restrictions, and initial GDP per capita, exploiting cross‑country and over‑time variation in institutions and redistribution.

triplet_key: 3981|sluggish public capital investment relative to output|productivity growth
title: State Infrastructure and Productive Performance
author: Catherine J. Morrison, Amy Ellen Schwartz
treatment: Sluggish public capital investment relative to output
context: This paper studies the effect of public infrastructure (state-level public capital) on manufacturing firms' costs and productivity using annual state-level manufacturing data for the 48 contiguous U.S. states over 1970–1987. Units of observation are state manufacturing sectors (output and inputs: private capital, public capital, production and nonproduction labor, energy). The institutional setting is U.S. state public capital investment across regions (Northeast, North, South, West); the authors estimate a structural variable-cost model with state fixed effects to quantify shadow values and scale effects of public capital.

title: Wages, Profits and Rent-Sharing
author: David G. Blanchflower, Andrew J. Oswald, Peter Sanfey
context: This paper analyzes US manufacturing wage determination using CPS March microdata for roughly 400,000 workers aggregated into 19 industry-by-year cells over 1964–1985. Industry-level profit and unemployment series (profit data available 1958–1985, aggregated to 2-digit SIC) are merged with cell means of individual characteristics to test rent-sharing versus competitive and contract models. Estimations use dynamic panel specifications with year dummies and both fixed- and random-effects to trace contemporaneous and lagged profit effects on mean wages.

title: Heterogeneity, Stratification, and Growth
author: Roland Benabou
context: This triplet comes from a theoretical comparative‑statics analysis of economies with heterogeneous agents interacting via local public goods and economy‑wide linkages. The paper compares integrated (mixed) versus segregated local communities—using examples like local versus national school funding—and evaluates short‑run and long‑run effects on aggregate output and productivity. Units are families/communities and aggregate economies in a general (non–country‑specific) setting, with emphasis on long‑run output/productivity under varying local and global complementarities.

title: Does Head Start Make a Difference?
author: Janet Currie, Duncan Thomas
context: This paper uses the US National Longitudinal Survey of Youth Child–Mother data (children of the 1979 NLSY cohort, surveyed in 1986–1990) to estimate effects of participation in the federal Head Start preschool program on children’s cognitive scores, grade repetition, immunizations, and height, and on longer‑run outcomes for women. Units are children (and mothers with sister comparisons); the sample is national with oversamples of African‑Americans and Hispanics. Identification relies on within‑family comparisons to address time‑invariant family confounders.

title: Does Head Start Make a Difference?
author: Janet Currie, Duncan Thomas
context: This analysis uses the NLSY Child–Mother data (children of the 1979 cohort, surveyed 1986–1990) and focuses on child height-for-age (standardized to the US median), using the first height measure taken after age 5. Units are children; identification for this triplet relies on mother fixed effects (sibling comparisons) to control time-invariant family factors. The paper reports heterogeneous effects by race: Head Start enrollees are taller than their siblings among African‑American and Hispanic families, while the sibling comparison for whites shows the opposite.

title: Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania

title: Foreign Competition, Market Power and Wage Inequality: Theory and Evidence
author: George J. Borjas, Valerie A. Ramey
treatment: Share employed in high-concentration import industries
outcome: Relative wage of high school dropouts
context: This paper examines how import competition in concentrated durable-goods industries affected wage inequality in the United States during 1976–1990 (using 1977–1991 CPS microdata). It analyzes 44 SMSAs and full-time civilian workers aged 18–64, linking local changes in employment shares in high‑concentration import industries, unionization, immigration, and labor-market conditions to cross‑city and within‑city changes in college vs. high‑school/less‑educated wage gaps. The setting emphasizes oligopolistic rents in durable-goods sectors, spillovers across local labor markets, and rising 1980s wage inequality.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Saving Babies: The Efficacy and Cost of Recent Expansions of Medicaid   Eligibility for Pregnant Women
author: Janet Currie, Jonathan Gruber
context: This paper studies US Medicaid eligibility expansions for pregnant women across 49 states (plus DC) during 1979–1990. Units include state-year aggregates (Vital Statistics infant outcomes) and individual women aged 15–44 (CPS, NLSY). It exploits cross-state, over-time variation from targeted AFDC-linked expansions in the early 1980s and broader income-based expansions (1987–1990, e.g. 133%–185% FPL) to assess effects on low birthweight, infant mortality, Medicaid take-up, service use, and program expenditures.

title: Executive Pay and Performance: Evidence from the U.S. Banking Industry
author: R. Glenn Hubbard, Darius Palia
context: This paper analyzes U.S. commercial banks in the 1980s (roughly 1980–1989/1981–1990) using a panel of 147 publicly traded banks across 36 states and DC. It exploits staggered state-level interstate banking deregulation (changes in state laws permitting out-of-state entry) to study how increased corporate-control market competitiveness affected CEO pay levels, pay structure (equity/options), and turnover. Units of observation are banks and bank CEOs; identification leverages cross-state and over-time variation in regulatory regimes with bank- and CEO-fixed effects and year controls.

title: Evidence on Growth, Increasing Returns and the Extent of the Market
author: Alberto F. Ades, Edward L. Glaeser
context: This triplet is estimated on the cross‑country sample of 65 low‑income countries (those with 1960 per‑capita GDP roughly ≤ $1,500), using data for 1960–1985. Units are countries; openness is measured by the share of total trade (exports+imports of goods and non‑factor services) in GDP, and the outcome is average per‑capita GDP growth over 1960–85. The paper implements unconditional cross‑sectional growth regressions for this sample and explicitly notes the openness measure may be endogenous.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Trade and Industrial Policy Reform in Latin America
author: Sebastian Edwards
context: Focuses on trade and industrial policy reforms in Latin America and the Caribbean (mid-1980s–1993). For the short-run employment effect of trade liberalization, the paper cites Michaely et al. (1991), a World Bank cross-country study of liberalization episodes in 19 countries, using country-level episodes to assess short-run unemployment consequences; it reports that in sustainable and successful reforms the net effect on short-run employment was negligible.
identification_methods: other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions

It follows roughly a 1/25 sample of private-sector workers (about 1 million observations, 1976–1987) and a probability sample of firms (≈20,000 firms followed 1978–1988, with firm-level data linked via SIREN), measuring real annual compensation (wages plus benefits). The setting is French private employment and social‑security reporting; analyses exploit within-person mobility and firm averages to relate high‑wage workers and high‑wage firms to productivity, capital intensity and survival.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Physician Payments and Infant Mortality: Evidence from Medicaid Fee     Policy
author: Janet Currie, Jonathan Gruber, Michael Fischer
context: This empirical study analyzes annual state-level data for U.S. states over 1979–1992 (mainly 1980–1992, with robustness to 1983–1992). The unit is state-year (≈45 states); outcomes are infant mortality rates and Medicaid physician, clinic, and hospital expenditures per woman aged 15–44. It leverages cross-state, over-time variation in Medicaid ob/gyn fee-to-private-fee ratios and expansions of Medicaid pregnancy eligibility, using Vital Statistics and HCFA payment data and state and year fixed effects.

title: The Causes and Effects of Liability Reform: Some Empirical Evidence
author: Thomas J. Campbell, Daniel P. Kessler, George B. Shepherd
context: This paper analyzes the effects and determinants of state-level tort liability reforms in the United States over 1969–1990. The authors compile a new panel of state tort statutes and judicial decisions matched to industry–state–year data (17 local industries plus manufacturing) from the BEA, and study how adoption of eight reforms (e.g., damage caps, contingency-fee limits, joint-and-several, comparative negligence, prejudgment interest) affected industry productivity and employment. Units are state-industry-year observations; authors exploit staggered cross-state adoption and use political/professional variables (lawyers/doctors per capita, party control, ADA scores) as instruments.

title: Do Doctors Practice Defensive Medicine?
author: Daniel P. Kessler, Mark McClellan
context: The analysis examines U.S. state-level malpractice liability reforms that directly reduce provider liability pressure and their effects on cardiac complications for elderly Medicare beneficiaries treated for serious heart disease (acute myocardial infarction) in 1984, 1987, and 1990, comparing outcomes in reform versus nonreform states.

title: Do Doctors Practice Defensive Medicine?
author: Daniel P. Kessler, Mark McClellan
context: The triplet concerns state-level malpractice liability reforms in the United States that directly reduce provider liability pressure, and their effect on mortality for elderly Medicare beneficiaries treated for serious heart disease in 1984, 1987, and 1990. triplet_key: 5653|bankruptcy exemption level|probability of being turned down for credit
title: Personal Bankruptcy and Credit Supply and Demand
author: Reint Gropp, John Karl Scholz, Michelle White

The setting is U.S. household-level credit markets in the early 1980s, with controls for demographics, local banking structure, and regional labor‑market conditions.

title: The Economic Consequences of Parental Leave Mandates: Lessons from Europe
author: Christopher J. Ruhm
context: This study uses annual country-year gender-specific labor-market aggregates for 16 European countries from 1969–1988 to estimate the effects of parental leave legislation (paid and unpaid weeks, job-protection and eligibility rules) on employment-to-population ratios, weekly hours, and wages. The unit of observation is sex (and in some specifications age) within country-year; most leave is taken by women and is often publicly financed. Policy variation arises from staggered enactments and extensions of leave across countries and time, with comparisons between women and men and between younger (25–34) and older (45–54) women.

title: The Economic Consequences of Parental Leave Mandates: Lessons from Europe
author: Christopher J. Ruhm
context: This analysis uses annual country-year gender-specific labor-market aggregates for 16 European countries from 1969–1988 to estimate the effect of paid parental leave entitlements (paid weeks with job protection) on weekly work hours. The unit of observation is sex within country-year (with most leave taken by women); identification contrasts women and men (a DDD-style comparison) with country and year fixed effects and country-specific time trends in some specifications.

title: The Response of Wages and Actual Hours Worked to the Reductions of Standard Hours
author: Jennifer Hunt
context: Using German Socio-Economic Panel data 1984–1989, this paper studies individual workers (person-job spells) in West Germany—particularly Arbeiter (hourly) and Angestellten (salaried) in manufacturing, construction and services—during union-negotiated stepwise reductions in industry-level standard weekly hours (starting in 1985, notably metalworking/printing). The institutional setting features collective bargaining, overtime premiums, flexibility clauses, and industry-level wage negotiations; the paper exploits within-job temporal variation across industries to estimate effects of standard-hours reductions on actual hours and straight-time hourly wages.

title: Technology, Employment, and the Business Cycle: Do Technology Shocks Explain Aggregate Fluctuations
author: Jordi Gali

title: Has Work-Sharing Worked in Germany?
author: Jennifer Hunt

The institutional setting is industry-level union bargaining (notably IG Metall) that implemented stepwise cuts in standard hours, overtime premia, and greater hours flexibility; outcomes examined include employment, hours per worker, worker‑hours, and wage bills.

triplet_key: 5758|credit to public sector|growth of financially dependent industries
title: Financial Dependence and Growth
author: Raghuram G. Rajan, Luigi Zingales
context: This study examines whether financial development in 1980 affects industry-level real value-added growth over 1980–1990 across 43 countries (the U.S. is excluded), using manufacturing industry–country pairs as units. For this triplet the treatment is the 1980 ratio of domestic credit to the public sector (public-sector credit/GDP), interacted with an industry’s financial dependence (proxied from U.S. Compustat firm financing patterns); the outcome is the industry’s real value-added growth 1980–1990. Key assumptions and instruments (legal origin, judicial quality) follow the paper.

title: Does Head Start Help Hispanic Children?
author: Janet Currie, Duncan Thomas
context: The analysis uses within-family (sibling) comparisons: children are compared to their siblings using family fixed effects and difference-in-differences to estimate the impact of Head Start on cognitive test scores (notably PPVT and Piat-Math, and Piat-Reading). Estimates compare Head Start participants to siblings who stayed at home or attended other preschools, with outcomes including test scores and grade repetition.

triplet_key: 5866|increase in disability insurance benefits|labor force non-participation
title: Disability Insurance Benefits and Labor Supply
author: Jonathan Gruber
context: This paper studies the labor-supply response to a January 1987 Canadian Disability Insurance (DI) reform that raised CPP disability benefits (a ~36% relative increase versus Quebec's QPP). Using April Canadian Survey of Consumer Finances data from before (1985–86) and after (1987–89), Gruber analyzes men aged 45–59 across provinces (CPP regions versus Quebec), exploiting differences in program generosity under CPP and QPP and institutional rules (eligibility, flat-rate vs earnings components) to identify causal effects.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; other panel regressions; others

title: Disability Insurance Benefits and Labor Supply
author: Jonathan Gruber
context: This triplet evaluates social-welfare effects of the January 1987 increase in CPP disability benefits (≈36% relative to Quebec's QPP). Gruber uses elasticity estimates of labor‑force non‑participation (0.25–0.32) from April SCF data on men aged 45–59 (1985–89) and conducts simulations with a utilitarian CES social‑welfare function, two groups (workers and the disabled), tax financing of the benefit increase, and parameters for crowd‑out and risk aversion to assess net welfare gains.
identification_methods: DiD; IV; other panel regressions; others

title: VAT Base Broadening, Self Supply, and The Informal Sector
author: John Piggott, John Whalley
context: This triplet is evaluated within a calibrated general equilibrium tax model applied to Canada, capturing the Canadian GST introduction in 1990. The analysis focuses on household-level supply choices and informal-sector producers (e.g., small-scale construction and untaxed services), with tax-induced substitution into household production and underground activity. The model treats informal-sector supply as predominantly from lower-income households selling tax-free output to higher-income buyers.

title: VAT Base Broadening, Self Supply, and The Informal Sector
author: John Piggott, John Whalley
context: This triplet is evaluated using a calibrated general-equilibrium tax model applied to Canada’s introduction of the GST in 1990. The model simulates equal-yield VAT base broadening from a high-rate, narrow-based tax on manufactures to a broad-based VAT including services, explicitly modeling market versus self-supplied goods and informal-sector production. Units of analysis are households (poor and rich) with welfare measured by individual utilities and aggregate welfare (sum of utilities); numerical examples illustrate effects on market time, self-supply, and informal-sector activity.

triplet_key: 6437|energy prices and regulations|rate of overall innovation
title: The Induced Innovation Hypothesis and Energy-Saving Technological Change
author: Richard G. Newell, Adam B. Jaffe, Robert N. Stavins
context: This paper examines model-level technological change in energy-using consumer durables (residential air conditioners and water heaters) sold in the U.S. from roughly the early 1970s through the 1990s. Units of observation are product models (menus of new models offered for sale), their characteristics, prices, and measured energy efficiencies. The institutional setting includes large energy-price increases since 1973, the introduction of labeling requirements, and adoption of minimum efficiency standards, which the authors analyze as drivers of innovation direction and product offerings.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; other time-series regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Do Foreign Investors Destabilize Stock Markets?  The Korean Experience  in 1997
author: Hyuk Choe, Bong-Chan Kho, Rene M. Stulz
context: This paper examines how foreign investors traded and affected stock prices on the Korean Stock Exchange using transaction-level trades and daily foreign-ownership data from Nov 30, 1996 through Dec 1997 (covering the Oct–Dec 1997 Korean crisis). The units are trades and daily observations for 414 common stocks and investor classes (foreign, Korean individual, Korean institutional). The institutional setting includes KSE trading rules (two sessions, batch auctions), SSB foreign registration and binding foreign-ownership limits that changed in 1997; analyses use intraday (5-minute) and daily event windows.
identification_methods: event studies; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: Is Free Trade Good for the Environment?
author: Werner Antweiler, Brian R. Copeland, M. Scott Taylor
context: This paper investigates how international trade openness affects urban sulfur dioxide (SO2) concentrations using panel data from the Global Environment Monitoring System (GEMS) covering 1971–1996. The unit of observation is site‑year SO2 median concentrations for 293 monitoring sites in 109 cities across 44 countries. The analysis links site‑level pollution to country‑level GDP per km2, capital–labor ratios, lagged GDP per capita, and trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), controlling for site fixed effects, weather, and time trends to decompose scale, composition and technique effects.

context: This paper investigates how international trade openness affects urban sulfur dioxide (SO2) concentrations using panel data from the Global Environment Monitoring System (GEMS) covering 1971–1996. The analysis links site‑level pollution to country‑level GDP per km2, capital–labor ratios, lagged GDP per capita, and trade openness (exports+imports/GDP), controlling for site fixed effects, weather, and time trends to decompose scale, composition and technique effects.

title: Dating the Integration of World Equity Markets
author: Geert Bekaert, Campbell R. Harvey, Robin L. Lumsdaine

author: Brigitte C. Madrian, Dennis F. Shea

context: Analyzes employee-level 401(k) behavior at a large U.S. Fortune 500 health insurance company using administrative data from June 1997–June 1999 around an April 1, 1998 policy change. context: This paper analyzes cross-country differences in factor-specific efficiencies and the choice of "appropriate" technologies using a cross-section of 52 countries in the late 1980s (GDP and capital for 1988, schooling 1985). context: The paper studies former European colonies worldwide, comparing precolonial prosperity (urbanization and population density circa 1500, with checks using 1000 AD and 1 AD) to modern national income per capita and industrialization outcomes. Units are countries/colonial territories and broader civilizations. The institutional shock is European colonization from the late 15th century onward, with emphasis on settler presence, disease environment, extractive vs. private-property institutions, and 19th-century industrialization as mechanisms linking historical conditions to modern incomes.
identification_methods: IV; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Income Inequality in the United States, 1913-1998 (series updated to 2000 available)
author: Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez
context: This paper analyzes U.S. income and wage concentration using annual IRS tax-return and estate-return tabulations and national accounts from roughly 1913–1998 (estates to 1997). The unit of observation is the tax unit (household) and decedents, ranked into top fractiles (P90–P99.99) with decomposition by income sources (wages, dividends, business income). The institutional background includes the U.S. federal income and estate tax system, corporate taxation, wartime wage controls, the Great Depression and World Wars, and major tax reforms (e.g., TRA86), used as historical shocks.

title: The Impacts of Environmental Regulations on Industrial Activity: Evidence from the 1970 & 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments and the Census of Manufactures
author: Michael Greenstone
context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. It exploits county-level, pollutant-specific attainment/nonattainment designations for four criteria pollutants across 3,070 counties and 1.75 million plant observations; polluting plants in nonattainment counties faced stricter plant-specific regulation. The unit of analysis is individual manufacturing plants; identification leverages cross-sectional and temporal variation in county nonattainment status and industry emitting status to estimate regulation impacts on employment, capital, and shipments.

title: The Impacts of Environmental Regulations on Industrial Activity: Evidence from the 1970 & 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments and the Census of Manufactures
author: Michael Greenstone
context: This estimate measures the effect of a county-level SO2 nonattainment designation on employment of individual manufacturing plants during the first 15 years after the 1970 and 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments (1972–1987). It uses plant-level observations from the 1967–1987 Census of Manufactures across U.S. counties, comparing polluting (SO2-emitting) plants in nonattainment versus attainment counties. Identification exploits cross-sectional and temporal variation in county pollutant-specific nonattainment status and emitter status in panel regressions with plant fixed effects and industry×time and county×time controls.

title: The Impacts of Environmental Regulations on Industrial Activity: Evidence from the 1970 & 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments and the Census of Manufactures
author: Michael Greenstone
context: This estimate measures the impact of TSPs (total suspended particulates) nonattainment designations from the 1970 and 1977 Clean Air Act Amendments on employment of TSP-emitting manufacturing plants, using plant-level Census of Manufactures data for 1967–1987. It reports the cumulative change in employment in nonattainment counties over the first 15 years the Amendments were in force (roughly 1972–1987), comparing TSP emitters in nonattainment versus attainment counties and exploiting cross-sectional and temporal variation in county pollutant-specific nonattainment status.

triplet_key: 8521|asian financial crisis (initial impact)|expected value of government subsidies
title: Cronyism and Capital Controls: Evidence from Malaysia
author: Simon Johnson, Todd Mitton
context: Analyzes how the 1997–98 Asian financial crisis and Malaysia’s September 1998 capital controls affected market valuations of 424 Malaysian listed firms. Using Gomez and Jomo’s coding of political ties (Mahathir- and Anwar-connected firms), the paper examines July 1997–August 1998 (crisis) and the September 1998 policy/political shock (capital controls and Anwar’s dismissal), exploiting variation in political connections, Bumiputera status, and prior foreign listings to infer changes in expected subsidies and access to international finance.

triplet_key: 8521|imposition of capital controls|stock price of mahathir-connected firms without foreign listings

title: Unraveling Reduces the Scope of an Entry Level Labor Market: Gastroenterology With and Without a Centralized Match
author: Muriel Niederle, Alvin E. Roth
context: This paper examines U.S. gastroenterology fellowship matches using physician-level AMA data for fellows finishing mostly between the late 1970s and 2003. It exploits the introduction of a centralized clearinghouse (MSMP/NRMP) that organized fellow appointments from 1986 (positions starting 1987) through the mid-1990s and its subsequent breakdown beginning ~1997–2000, alongside a 1996 policy shock that reduced positions and extended training, to compare individual mobility (hospital, city, state) across market regimes.

triplet_key: 8629|per-unit export subsidies|export revenues per peso spent
title: Market Entry Costs, Producer Heterogeneity, and Export Dynamics
author: Sanghamitra Das, Mark J. Roberts, James R. Tybout
context: Using annual plant-level panel data on 62 Colombian chemical plants operating continuously during 1982–1991, this paper estimates a dynamic structural model of firms’ export participation and export revenue decisions. The institutional setting is Colombia’s trade and exchange-rate environment (real peso depreciation and export subsidy regimes) in the 1980s–early 1990s. Units are manufacturing plants; key features include firm heterogeneity in marginal costs and foreign demand, stochastic AR processes for exchange rates and profit shocks, and stochastic sunk start-up and fixed costs of exporting. The estimated model is used to simulate devaluations and subsidy policies.
identification_methods: others; other panel regressions; other time-series regressions

title: Is More Information Better? The Effects of 'Report Cards' on Health Care Providers
author: David Dranove, Daniel Kessler, Mark McClellan, Mark Satterthwaite
context: Using U.S. Medicare claims and AHA hospital data from 1987–1994, the paper studies the short-run causal effects of mandatory public CABG surgery report cards introduced in New York (early 1990s, effective ~1991) and Pennsylvania (effective ~1993). The analysis examines both hospital- and patient-level outcomes for elderly Medicare beneficiaries (AMI cohorts and CABG recipients), exploiting treated (NY, PA) versus control states to evaluate how public disclosure of risk-adjusted surgeon/hospital CABG mortality altered selection, treatment incidence, resource use, and health outcomes.

title: Related Lending
author: Rafael La Porta, Florencio Lopez-de-Silane, Guillermo Zamarripa
context: This paper studies loan-level lending by 17 Mexican banks in the mid-1990s, focusing on the top-300 loans (snapshot Dec 1995/March 1996) and a random sample of ~1,500 loans followed through December 1999. It compares loans to related borrowers (bank owners, officers, family, or firms the bank owns) versus unrelated ones across the 1994 devaluation and ensuing banking crisis, in a setting of privatized banks, weak supervision and blanket deposit insurance (FOBAPROA). Units are individual loans/borrowers and bank-level episodes of distress.

title: Does Local Financial Development Matter?
author: Luigi Guiso, Paola Sapienza, Luigi Zingales
context: Within Italy during the late 1980s–1990s, the paper uses firm-level balance-sheet data (Centrale dei Bilanci) across provinces/regions to assess how regional financial development affects firms' mark-ups. Mark-up is measured as EBITDA divided by output, and the unit of observation is individual firms (mostly small and medium). Regressions include industry and calendar-year controls and instrument regional financial development (e.g., judicial inefficiency, social capital) to address endogeneity.
identification_methods: IV; other cross-sectional regressions; other panel regressions

title: Industry Growth and Capital Allocation: Does Having a Market- or Bank-Based System Matter?
author: Thorsten Beck, Ross Levine
context: This paper studies how financial structure affects industry growth, new establishment formation, and capital allocation using a cross-industry, cross-country panel of 42 countries and 36 manufacturing industries over 1980–1990 (industry growth and establishments) and cross-country investment-elasticity estimates mainly covering 1963–95. Units are country–industry pairs and countries. The analysis exploits variation in financial structure (market vs bank size, regulatory restrictions, state ownership), overall financial development, and legal-system efficiency; industry traits come from U.S. firm data and legal origin/religion are used as instruments.
identification_methods: IV; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions

title: How Important are Classroom Peer Effects? Evidence from Boston's Metco Program
author: Joshua D. Angrist, Kevin Lang
context: This paper studies 3rd–7th grade students in the Brookline (MA) public schools using ITBS scores from 1994–2000 to measure how Boston’s METCO desegregation program — which buses predominantly Black students from Boston into suburban districts — altered class composition and peer achievement. The unit of observation is individual students and grade/school/year classroom cells; the setting features voluntary METCO placements, contractual class-size caps, and administrative assignment rules used to probe peer and class-size effects.

title: Competition and Innovation: An Inverted U Relationship
author: Philippe Aghion, Nicholas Bloom, Richard Blundell, Rachel Griffith, Peter Howitt
context: This paper studies UK publicly listed firms over 1968–1997 (panel of firms classified by 2-digit SIC), linking firm-year accounting data to US patent grants (citation-weighted) to estimate how product-market competition (Lerner index / implied α) affects innovation. Identification exploits cross-industry, over-time variation from UK competition policy reforms (European Single Market, MMC investigations, privatisations) and additional industry-level instruments (OECD/US technology measures) to instrument competition and technology-gap measures; unit of observation is the firm-year within industries.

title: Equity market liberalizations as country IPOs
author: Rodolfo Martell, Rene M. Stulz
context: The paper studies equity-market liberalizations in emerging-market countries roughly from the late 1970s through September 2000, using country-level S&P EMDB total-return indices for liberalizing countries (about 15 with 5-year pre/post windows around liberalization months from Bekaert & Harvey and Henry). Units are national stock-market indices; empirical analyses focus on returns around policy events that opened markets to foreign investors. The setting emphasizes institutional features—liquidity/turnover, investor protection, concentrated controlling-shareholder ownership, and cross-country variation in liberalization dates and credibility.

triplet_key: 9488|opening stock markets to foreign investors|growth rate of output per worker
title: Capital Account Liberalization, The Cost of Capital, and Economic Growth
author: Peter Blair Henry
treatment: Opening stock markets to foreign investors
outcome: Growth rate of output per worker
context: This paper studies 18 developing countries that liberalized their stock markets—mainly via country funds or policy decrees—during the late 1980s and early 1990s (roughly 1986–1995). The unit of observation is the country (annual country-level aggregates): market dividend yields, capital stock growth, and output per worker. The institutional setting is emerging-market stock-market openings to foreign investors, often occurring alongside other macroeconomic and structural reforms; the analysis compares pre- and post-liberalization outcomes in event time.

title: Strikes, Scabs and Tread Separations: Labor Strife and the Production of Defective Bridgestone/Firestone Tires
author: Alan B. Krueger, Alexandre Mas
treatment: Concurrence of replacement and union workers
context: This triplet concerns P235/75R15 tires produced at Firestone’s Decatur, IL plant during the 1994–1996 labor dispute. The specific analysis uses claims disaggregated by month of production to detect excess defective tires in the months just before the contract expired and in the period when replacement workers and recalled union members worked side-by-side. Units are individual tires observed via month-of-production counts (aggregated into plant×production‑month cells), within the broader 1991–1999 dataset comparing Decatur to Joliette and Wilson.
identification_methods: DiD; other panel regressions; other cross-sectional regressions; event studies

title: Public Housing, Housing Vouchers and Student Achievement: Evidence from Public Housing Demolitions in Chicago
author: Brian A. Jacob
context: Using administrative records for Chicago public housing and Chicago Public Schools from the 1990s (student semesters 1991–92 through 2001–02), the paper studies how high-rise CHA building closures and demolitions in Chicago affected individual students and households. Units are individual students matched to building addresses; affected families were offered Section 8 vouchers or CHA transfers (forced relocations, limited counseling) amid CHA redevelopment and crisis-driven closures, altering housing, neighborhood poverty, and school assignments.
identification_methods: DiD; event studies; IV; other panel regressions

triplet_key: 9661|elimination of estate and inheritance taxes|charitable bequests
title: Charitable Bequests and Taxes on Inheritance and Estates: Aggregate Evidence from Across States and Time
author: Jon Bakija, William Gale, Joel Slemrod
treatment: Elimination of estate and inheritance taxes
context: Using pooled cell-level aggregates of U.S. federal estate tax returns across states and years (1924–1945 and selected postwar years: 1969, 1976, 1982, 1985–1998), the paper analyzes charitable bequests by second‑to‑die spouses (widows/widowers) sorted into five real-wealth categories. It exploits variation in federal and state estate/inheritance tax schedules, marginal rates, exemptions, and bracket changes over time and across states, using a combined federal–state tax calculator to construct tax prices and disposable wealth for each state×year×wealth cell.

triplet_key: 9704|bank integration|employment growth sensitivity to bank capital
title: Bank Integration and State Business Cycles
author: Donald Morgan, Bertrand Rime, Philip Strahan
outcome: Employment growth sensitivity to bank capital
context: The paper analyzes U.S. state-level business volatility over 1976–1994 using annual state-year observations (total and small-firm employment). It studies how banking integration—the share of state bank assets owned by multi-state bank holding companies—changed with staggered state deregulation of interstate banking (late 1970s through the 1994 Riegle-Neal Act). The institutional setting is state-level banking deregulation and holding-company entry rules; identification exploits cross-state, over-time variation in deregulation while controlling for industry composition and common time effects.

title: Bank Integration and State Business Cycles
author: Donald Morgan, Bertrand Rime, Philip Strahan
context: This triplet focuses on U.S. state-level small-firm employment growth volatility using annual state-year observations over the deregulation era; small-firm employment (establishments with <20 employees) is drawn from County Business Patterns and the growth-rate series effectively covers 1978–1994. The paper links volatility to banking integration—the share of state bank assets owned by multi-state bank holding companies—exploiting staggered state-level interstate-banking deregulation and controlling for industry composition, and state and year fixed effects.

title: Bank Integration and State Business Cycles
author: Donald Morgan, Bertrand Rime, Philip Strahan

title: The Labor Demand Curve is Downward Sloping: Reexamining the Impact of Immigration on the Labor Market
author: George J. Borjas
context: Using U.S. microdata (1960–1990 Decennial Census PUMS and pooled 1999–2001 CPS), the paper studies how immigration altered wages and labor supply of prime‑age men (ages 18–64) grouped by education and experience across U.S. labor markets (nationally and by state/region) over 1960–2000. It exploits variation in immigrant shares across schooling‑experience cells, assumes similarly educated workers with different experience are imperfect substitutes, and combines reduced‑form regressions with structural CES demand exercises to quantify wage and employment effects.
identification_methods: other panel regressions; IV; other cross-sectional regressions; others

title: The Regulation of Labor
author: Simeon Djankov, Rafael La Porta, Florencio Lopez-de-Silane, Andrei Shleifer, Juan Botero
context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in labor-market regulation using detailed indices of employment law, collective-bargaining law, and social‑security law measured around 1997 for 85 countries. Units are countries; political controls use the fraction of years that executives and legislatures were left/center between 1975–1995 (and robustness to 1928–1995). The institutional context includes legal origin (common, French, German, Scandinavian, socialist), trade openness and political constraints. Main outcomes include unofficial-economy size, labor-force participation, unemployment (especially ages 20–24), and relative wages.

title: Judicial Checks and Balances
author: Rafael LaPorta, Florencio Lopez-de-Silane, Cristian Pop-Eleches, Andrei Shleifer
context: This triplet is estimated in cross-country OLS regressions using the subset of countries for which the employment laws index is available (54 of the 71 non-transition countries). Judicial independence is drawn from constitutions (tenure of supreme and administrative judges, case law) and the employment laws index comes from Botero et al. (2003). Regressions (Table 3) are shown with and without controls (ethnolinguistic fractionalization, latitude, and ln GDP per capita); units are countries and constitutional measures are treated as pre-determined.

title: What Works in Securities Law?
author: Rafael La Porta, Florencio Lopez-de-Silane, Andrei Shleifer
context: This paper empirically examines how securities laws affect stock market development across 49 countries. Laws governing IPO prospectuses and enforcement in each country (legal rules as of Dec 2000) are coded, and market outcomes use five-year averages (1996–2000) of capitalization, number of listed firms, and IPO activity; country is the unit of observation. The setting contrasts private-enforcement features (disclosure requirements, burden of proof) and public enforcement (regulators’ powers), and exploits legal-origin variation as an instrument to address endogeneity.
